# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Block-by-Block Analysis of a Digital Control System

A typical **single-loop digital control loop** operates in the following sequence:

1. **Reference Generation** — r(t) is the continuous desired input.
2. **Error Formation** — e(t) = r(t) − b(t) is formed at the summing junction (continuous).
3. **Sampling** — A sampler with period **T** captures e(kT). This models the A/D converter.
4. **Digital Control Law** — The digital computer computes u(kT) = D(z)·E(z) where D(z) is the discrete controller.
5. **Reconstruction (Hold)** — The ZOH converts u(kT) into a staircase continuous signal uh(t).
6. **Plant Actuation** — The continuous plant Gp(s) responds to uh(t) producing output c(t).
7. **Feedback** — H(s) senses c(t) and feeds b(t) back to the summer.

> [!NOTE]
> The sampler and hold are **not** physical components in the classical s-domain sense — they introduce time delay and amplitude quantisation. The ZOH transfer function is the only "transfer function" of a hold device that is analytically tractable.

---

## 2.2 Transfer Function of the Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)

The **Zero-Order Hold** produces a piecewise-constant output:

$$u_h(t) \;=\; u(kT), \quad \text{for } kT \le t < (k+1)T$$

The impulse response is a unit rectangle of width **T** starting at t = 0. Its Laplace transform is:

$$G_{ZOH}(s) \;=\; \frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}$$

This is **the** most important expression in Module 2 — the entire ZOH + Plant reduction depends on it.

---

## 2.3 Pulse Transfer Function of ZOH + Plant Cascade

When a ZOH precedes a plant Gp(s) in the forward path, the **effective pulse transfer function** is:

$$G(z) \;=\; \mathcal{Z}\!\left\{G_{ZOH}(s)\cdot G_p(s)\right\} \;=\; (1 - z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\}$$

**Why this form?** Because of the **shifting theorem** of Z-transform:

$$\mathcal{Z}\{e^{-sT}\,F(s)\} \;=\; z^{-1}\,\mathcal{Z}\{F(s)\}$$

So the term $e^{-sT}G_p(s)/s$ corresponds to a **one-sample delay** in the discrete domain.

---

## 2.4 Open-Loop Pulse Transfer Function — Cascaded Blocks

**Case A — Sampler between every block (samplers in series):**

$$G(z) \;=\; G_1(z)\cdot G_2(z)\cdot G_3(z) \;=\; \mathcal{Z}\{G_1(s)\}\cdot \mathcal{Z}\{G_2(s)\}\cdot \mathcal{Z}\{G_3(s)\}$$

**Case B — No sampler between cascaded blocks (continuous chain):**

$$G(z) \;=\; \mathcal{Z}\{G_1(s)\cdot G_2(s)\cdot G_3(s)\}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** In Case A, you **cannot** combine the s-functions first and then take Z. That is, $\mathcal{Z}\{G_1 G_2\} \ne \mathcal{Z}\{G_1\}\mathcal{Z}\{G_2\}$ in general. Always preserve the sampler boundary.

---

## 2.5 Closed-Loop Pulse Transfer Function — Unity Feedback

For a **unity feedback** sampled-data system with single forward sampler:

$$\frac{C(z)}{R(z)} \;=\; \frac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)}$$

For a **non-unity feedback** system:

$$\frac{C(z)}{R(z)} \;=\; \frac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)H(z)}$$

The **error transfer function** (when sampler is on the error line) is:

$$\frac{E(z)}{R(z)} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + G(z)}$$

---

## 2.6 Steady-State Error via Final Value Theorem

$$E(z) \;=\; \frac{R(z)}{1 + G(z)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad e_{ss} \;=\; \lim_{z \to 1}\left(\frac{z-1}{z}\right)\frac{R(z)}{1 + G(z)}$$

The three canonical results, in their KTU-board-ready form, are:

| Input | R(z) | e_ss | Error Constant Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Step** u(t) | $\dfrac{z}{z-1}$ | $\dfrac{1}{1+K_p}$ | $K_p = \lim_{z \to 1} G(z)$ |
| **Ramp** t·u(t) | $\dfrac{Tz}{(z-1)^2}$ | $\dfrac{T}{K_v}$ | $K_v = \dfrac{1}{T}\lim_{z \to 1}(z-1)G(z)$ |
| **Parabolic** $\tfrac{t^2}{2}u(t)$ | $\dfrac{T^2 z(z+1)}{2(z-1)^3}$ | $\dfrac{T^2}{K_a}$ | $K_a = \dfrac{1}{T^2}\lim_{z \to 1}(z-1)^2 G(z)$ |

---

## 2.7 System Type and Steady-State Error Summary

The **system type** in the z-domain is defined as the number of poles of G(z) at **z = 1**.

| Type | Poles at z=1 | Kp | Kv | Ka | e_ss (Step) | e_ss (Ramp) | e_ss (Parabolic) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | K (finite) | 0 | 0 | $\tfrac{1}{1+K}$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| 1 | 1 | $\infty$ | finite | 0 | 0 | $\tfrac{T}{K_v}$ | $\infty$ |
| 2 | 2 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | finite | 0 | 0 | $\tfrac{T^2}{K_a}$ |
| $\ge$ 3 | $\ge$ 3 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | 0 | 0 | 0 |

---

## 2.8 Bilinear Transformation — Derivation & Properties

**Geometric Derivation (Trapezoidal Rule):**
Integrating from t = (k−1)T to kT using the trapezoidal rule:

$$u(kT) - u((k-1)T) \;=\; \int_{(k-1)T}^{kT} \dot{u}(\tau)\,d\tau \;\approx\; \frac{T}{2}\bigl[\dot{u}(kT) + \dot{u}((k-1)T)\bigr]$$

Taking the Z-transform and writing in terms of s and z:

$$s \;\approx\; \frac{2}{T}\cdot\frac{z-1}{z+1}$$

**Key Properties (must be memorised for KTU):**

1. **Conformal Mapping** — Preserves angles; left-half s-plane maps to interior of unit circle.
2. **Stability Preservation** — Stable analog filter → Stable digital filter.
3. **Frequency Pre-warping** — $\omega_a = \dfrac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\omega T}{2}\right)$. The analog frequency must be pre-warped to retain the same magnitude response at a critical frequency.
4. **No Aliasing** — Unlike the impulse-invariance method, bilinear transformation **does not** suffer from aliasing because it is a one-to-one mapping.
5. **Imaginary axis ↔ Unit circle** — $s = j\Omega$ maps to $z = e^{j\omega T}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The bilinear transformation is the standard method for converting **analog controller designs (Lead/Lag/PID)** to **digital equivalents** in industrial DCS (Distributed Control Systems) and embedded controllers (STM32, Arduino, DSP TMS320).

---

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Quick Reference)

| **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | ZOH transfer function | $G_{ZOH}(s) = \dfrac{1-e^{-sT}}{s}$ |
| 2 | ZOH + Plant PTF | $G(z) = (1-z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\!\bigl\{\dfrac{G_p(s)}{s}\bigr\}$ |
| 3 | Cascade with sampler | $G(z) = G_1(z)\,G_2(z)$ |
| 4 | Cascade without sampler | $G(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{G_1(s) G_2(s)\}$ |
| 5 | Closed-loop (unity) PTF | $\dfrac{C(z)}{R(z)} = \dfrac{G(z)}{1+G(z)}$ |
| 6 | Closed-loop (non-unity) PTF | $\dfrac{C(z)}{R(z)} = \dfrac{G(z)}{1+G(z)H(z)}$ |
| 7 | Error transfer function | $\dfrac{E(z)}{R(z)} = \dfrac{1}{1+G(z)}$ |
| 8 | Final Value Theorem (z) | $e_{ss} = \lim_{z \to 1}\bigl(\tfrac{z-1}{z}\bigr)E(z)$ |
| 9 | Kp | $\lim_{z \to 1} G(z)$ |
| 10 | Kv | $\tfrac{1}{T}\lim_{z \to 1}(z-1)G(z)$ |
| 11 | Ka | $\tfrac{1}{T^2}\lim_{z \to 1}(z-1)^2 G(z)$ |
| 12 | Step error | $e_{ss} = \tfrac{1}{1+K_p}$ |
| 13 | Ramp error | $e_{ss} = \tfrac{T}{K_v}$ |
| 14 | Parabolic error | $e_{ss} = \tfrac{T^2}{K_a}$ |
| 15 | Bilinear transformation (s → z) | $s = \tfrac{2}{T}\cdot\tfrac{z-1}{z+1}$ |
| 16 | Bilinear transformation (z → s) | $z = \tfrac{2+sT}{2-sT}$ |
| 17 | Pre-warping relation | $\Omega = \tfrac{2}{T}\tan\!\bigl(\tfrac{\omega T}{2}\bigr)$ |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Pulse Transfer Function of ZOH + Plant

**Statement:** Show that for a system consisting of a ZOH in cascade with a plant Gp(s), the pulse transfer function is

$$G(z) \;=\; (1 - z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\}$$

**Step 1 — Write the ZOH + Plant forward transfer function.**

The ZOH has the transfer function derived from its impulse response (a unit rectangle of width T starting at 0):

$$G_{ZOH}(s) \;=\; \frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}$$

The combined continuous transfer function of ZOH and plant is:

$$G_{eq}(s) \;=\; G_{ZOH}(s)\cdot G_p(s) \;=\; \left(\frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}\right)G_p(s) \;=\; (1 - e^{-sT})\cdot\frac{G_p(s)}{s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Z-transform to the equivalent continuous function.**

$$G(z) \;=\; \mathcal{Z}\{G_{eq}(s)\} \;=\; \mathcal{Z}\!\left\{(1 - e^{-sT})\cdot\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\}$$

**Step 3 — Split the Z-transform using linearity.**

$$G(z) \;=\; \mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\} - \mathcal{Z}\!\left\{e^{-sT}\cdot\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the shifting property of the Z-transform.**

For any F(s), we have $\mathcal{Z}\{e^{-sT}F(s)\} = z^{-1}\mathcal{Z}\{F(s)\}$. Therefore:

$$\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{e^{-sT}\cdot\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\} \;=\; z^{-1}\,\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\}$$

**Step 5 — Combine the two terms.**

$$G(z) \;=\; \mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\} - z^{-1}\,\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\} \;=\; (1 - z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\}$$

**Hence proved.** $\blacksquare$

**Sanity Check (Worked Example):** Let $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+a)}$.

Then $\dfrac{G_p(s)}{s} = \dfrac{K}{s^2(s+a)}$.

Using partial fractions:

$$\frac{K}{s^2(s+a)} \;=\; \frac{K}{a^2}\left[\frac{1}{s^2} - \frac{1}{a}\cdot\frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{a}\cdot\frac{1}{s+a}\right]$$

Z-transforming term by term:

$$\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\} \;=\; \frac{K}{a^2}\!\left[\frac{Tz}{(z-1)^2} - \frac{1}{a}\cdot\frac{z}{z-1} + \frac{1}{a}\cdot\frac{z}{z-e^{-aT}}\right]$$

Hence:

$$G(z) \;=\; \frac{K}{a^2}\!\left[\frac{Tz}{(z-1)^2} - \frac{z}{a(z-1)} + \frac{z}{a(z-e^{-aT})}\right](1 - z^{-1})$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Closed-Loop Pulse Transfer Function (Unity Feedback)

**Statement:** Derive the closed-loop PTF of a unity feedback sampled-data system.

**Step 1 — Write the output equation in the discrete domain.**

The output is the response of G(z) to the sampled error E(z):

$$C(z) \;=\; G(z)\cdot E(z)$$

**Step 2 — Write the error equation.**

For unity feedback, the sampled error is the difference between sampled reference R(z) and sampled output C(z):

$$E(z) \;=\; R(z) - C(z)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute C(z) into the error equation.**

$$E(z) \;=\; R(z) - G(z)E(z)$$

**Step 4 — Collect E(z) terms on one side.**

$$E(z) + G(z)E(z) \;=\; R(z)$$

$$E(z)[1 + G(z)] \;=\; R(z)$$

$$E(z) \;=\; \frac{R(z)}{1 + G(z)}$$

**Step 5 — Recover C(z).**

$$C(z) \;=\; G(z)E(z) \;=\; \frac{G(z)\,R(z)}{1 + G(z)}$$

**Step 6 — Form the closed-loop pulse transfer function.**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{C(z)}{R(z)} \;=\; \frac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)}\;}$$

**Hence proved.** $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Steady-State Error for Standard Inputs

**Step 1 — Generic Final Value Theorem result.**

$$e_{ss} \;=\; \lim_{z \to 1}\left(\frac{z-1}{z}\right)E(z) \;=\; \lim_{z \to 1}\left(\frac{z-1}{z}\right)\frac{R(z)}{1 + G(z)}$$

### Case 1 — Unit Step Input

$$R(z) \;=\; \frac{z}{z-1}$$

Substitute:

$$e_{ss} \;=\; \lim_{z \to 1}\left(\frac{z-1}{z}\right)\cdot\frac{z/(z-1)}{1 + G(z)} \;=\; \lim_{z \to 1}\frac{1}{1 + G(z)} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + \lim_{z \to 1}G(z)} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + K_p}$$

### Case 2 — Unit Ramp Input

$$R(z) \;=\; \frac{Tz}{(z-1)^2}$$

Substitute:

$$e_{ss} \;=\; \lim_{z \to 1}\left(\frac{z-1}{z}\right)\cdot\frac{Tz/(z-1)^2}{1 + G(z)} \;=\; \lim_{z \to 1}\frac{T}{(z-1)[1 + G(z)]}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by 1 and rearranging:

$$e_{ss} \;=\; \lim_{z \to 1}\frac{T}{(z-1) + (z-1)G(z)}$$

As $z \to 1$, the term $(z-1) \to 0$, but $(z-1)G(z)$ is non-zero for a type-1 or higher system. Hence:

$$e_{ss} \;=\; \frac{T}{\lim_{z \to 1}(z-1)G(z)} \;=\; \frac{T}{T\,K_v} \;=\; \frac{1}{K_v/T} \;=\; \frac{T}{K_v}$$

where $K_v = \dfrac{1}{T}\lim_{z \to 1}(z-1)G(z)$.

### Case 3 — Unit Parabolic Input

$$R(z) \;=\; \frac{T^2 z(z+1)}{2(z-1)^3}$$

Substitute:

$$e_{ss} \;=\; \lim_{z \to 1}\left(\frac{z-1}{z}\right)\cdot\frac{T^2 z(z+1)}{2(z-1)^3[1 + G(z)]} \;=\; \lim_{z \to 1}\frac{T^2(z+1)}{2(z-1)^2[1 + G(z)]}$$

For a type-2 system, $(z-1)^2 G(z)$ is finite and non-zero at $z=1$:

$$e_{ss} \;=\; \lim_{z \to 1}\frac{T^2(z+1)}{2(z-1)^2 G(z)} \;=\; \frac{T^2 \cdot 2}{2 \cdot T^2 K_a} \;=\; \frac{T^2}{K_a}$$

where $K_a = \dfrac{1}{T^2}\lim_{z \to 1}(z-1)^2 G(z)$.

**Hence proved.** $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Bilinear Transformation via Trapezoidal Rule

**Step 1 — Discretise the integral of the derivative.**

Let $u(t)$ be a continuous signal. Its samples at the instants kT are $u(kT)$. The trapezoidal rule gives:

$$u(kT) - u((k-1)T) \;=\; \int_{(k-1)T}^{kT} \dot{u}(\tau)\,d\tau \;\approx\; \frac{T}{2}\bigl[\dot{u}(kT) + \dot{u}((k-1)T)\bigr]$$

**Step 2 — Take the Z-transform of both sides.**

Using the time-shift property:

$$\mathcal{Z}\{u(kT) - u((k-1)T)\} \;=\; (1 - z^{-1})\,U(z)$$

$$\mathcal{Z}\{\dot{u}(kT) + \dot{u}((k-1)T)\} \;=\; (1 + z^{-1})\,sU(z) \quad \text{(since differentiation multiplies Laplace by s)}$$

Wait — let us be careful. The Z-transform of a sampled signal $u(kT)$ corresponds to evaluating the Laplace transform of the signal at discrete points. The relationship is:

$$\mathcal{Z}\{u(kT)\} \;=\; U(z) \;=\; \left.U^*(s)\right|_{z = e^{sT}}$$

For the trapezoidal rule:

$$U(z)(1 - z^{-1}) \;=\; \frac{T}{2}\,sU(z)(1 + z^{-1})$$

**Step 3 — Solve for s.**

$$1 - z^{-1} \;=\; \frac{T}{2}\,s(1 + z^{-1})$$

$$s \;=\; \frac{2}{T}\cdot\frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}} \;=\; \frac{2}{T}\cdot\frac{z - 1}{z + 1}$$

**Step 4 — Invert to obtain z as a function of s.**

$$s(z+1) \;=\; \frac{2}{T}(z-1)$$

$$sTz + sT \;=\; 2z - 2$$

$$z(sT - 2) \;=\; -(sT + 2)$$

$$z \;=\; \frac{2 + sT}{2 - sT}$$

**Hence proved.** $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Static Error Coefficients

**Problem:** A digital control system has the open-loop pulse transfer function

$$G(z) \;=\; \frac{0.5\,(z + 0.2)}{(z - 1)(z - 0.6)}$$

with sampling period **T = 0.5 s**. Determine (a) the system type, (b) the static error coefficients, (c) the steady-state errors for step, ramp, and parabolic inputs.

### (a) System Type

Number of poles of G(z) at z = 1 = 1 (only the factor (z − 1) in the denominator). Therefore the system is **Type 1**.

### (b) Static Error Coefficients

**Position constant Kp:**

$$K_p \;=\; \lim_{z \to 1} G(z) \;=\; \frac{0.5(1 + 0.2)}{(1 - 1)(1 - 0.6)} \;=\; \frac{0.5 \cdot 1.2}{0 \cdot 0.4} \;=\; \infty$$

**Velocity constant Kv:**

$$K_v \;=\; \frac{1}{T}\lim_{z \to 1}(z-1)G(z) \;=\; \frac{1}{0.5}\cdot\frac{0.5(1 + 0.2)}{1 - 0.6} \;=\; 2 \cdot \frac{0.6}{0.4} \;=\; 2 \cdot 1.5 \;=\; 3.0$$

**Acceleration constant Ka:**

$$K_a \;=\; \frac{1}{T^2}\lim_{z \to 1}(z-1)^2 G(z) \;=\; \frac{1}{0.25}\cdot\frac{0.5(1+0.2)(z-1)}{1 - 0.6}\bigg|_{z \to 1} \;=\; \frac{0.6 \cdot 0}{0.25 \cdot 0.4} \;=\; 0$$

### (c) Steady-State Errors

- **Step:** $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1+K_p} = \dfrac{1}{1+\infty} = 0$
- **Ramp:** $e_{ss} = \dfrac{T}{K_v} = \dfrac{0.5}{3.0} = 0.1\overline{6}$
- **Parabolic:** $e_{ss} = \dfrac{T^2}{K_a} = \dfrac{0.25}{0} = \infty$

This matches the **Type 1** system pattern: finite ramp error, infinite parabolic error.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — Bilinear Transformation of a First-Order System

**Problem:** Convert the analog compensator $H_a(s) = \dfrac{s+1}{s+5}$ into discrete form using the bilinear transformation with T = 0.1 s.

**Step 1 — Substitute the bilinear expression for s.**

$$H(z) \;=\; H_a(s)\bigg|_{s = (2/T)\cdot(z-1)/(z+1)} \;=\; H_a(s)\bigg|_{s = 20(z-1)/(z+1)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute in the numerator and denominator separately.**

Numerator: $s + 1 = 20\dfrac{z-1}{z+1} + 1 = \dfrac{20(z-1) + (z+1)}{z+1} = \dfrac{21z - 19}{z+1}$

Denominator: $s + 5 = 20\dfrac{z-1}{z+1} + 5 = \dfrac{20(z-1) + 5(z+1)}{z+1} = \dfrac{25z - 15}{z+1}$

**Step 3 — Form H(z).**

$$H(z) \;=\; \frac{21z - 19}{25z - 15} \;=\; \frac{21(z - 19/21)}{25(z - 15/25)} \;=\; \frac{21(z - 0.9048)}{25(z - 0.6)}$$

**Step 4 — Check pole location for stability.**

Pole at z = 0.6, which is **inside the unit circle** (since |0.6| < 1). The system is **stable**. ✓

**Note:** The analog pole at s = −5 mapped to z = 0.6 (inside the unit circle), confirming the stability-preservation property of the bilinear transformation.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Single-Loop Digital (Sampled-Data) Control System

This diagram captures the complete forward and feedback signal flow of a typical KTU-modeled digital control system. The sampler and ZOH are explicitly shown because they define the discrete/continuous boundary.

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN[Reference Input R t] --> SUM((Summation Junction Plus))
    SUM --> SAM[Sampler Period T Seconds]
    SAM --> CON[Digital Controller D z]
    CON --> HOL[Zero Order Hold ZOH]
    HOL --> PLA[Continuous Plant Gp s]
    PLA --> OUT[Output C t]
    OUT --> SEN[Feedback Sensor H s]
    SEN --> SUM
    SAM -.->|Sampled Error E kT| SAM_E[Sampled Error E z]
    SUM --> ERR[Continuous Error E t]
    style SUM fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000
    style SAM fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366
    style CON fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
    style HOL fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    style PLA fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#330099
    style SEN fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#330099
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Red block** = error formation (continuous).
- **Blue block** = the A/D sampler (the boundary between continuous and discrete worlds).
- **Green block** = the digital controller (computed by microprocessor / DSP).
- **Yellow block** = the ZOH (D/A converter + reconstruction filter).
- **Purple blocks** = the continuous-time physical plant and feedback sensor.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop z-Domain Equivalent

Once the ZOH + Plant cascade is reduced to a single pulse transfer function G(z), the entire loop can be redrawn purely in the z-domain. This is the **canonical form** used for closed-loop analysis.

```mermaid
graph LR
    RZ[Reference R z] --> SS((Plus))
    SS --> EZ[Sampled Error E z]
    EZ --> GZ[Forward PTF G z]
    GZ --> CZ[Output C z]
    CZ --> HZ[Feedback PTF H z]
    HZ --> SS
    EZ --> FVT[Final Value Theorem]
    FVT --> ESS[Steady State Error E ss]
    style SS fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000
    style FVT fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    style ESS fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
```

**Key derived quantities shown in the z-domain:**

$$\frac{C(z)}{R(z)} \;=\; \frac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)H(z)} \qquad \frac{E(z)}{R(z)} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + G(z)H(z)}$$

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Bilinear Transformation Mapping

The diagram below illustrates the **conformal mapping** between the s-plane and the z-plane under the bilinear transformation. Stability regions are colour-coded for instant visual recognition.

```mermaid
graph LR
    SA[S Plane Continuous Domain] -->|s equals 2 over T times z minus 1 over z plus 1| ZP[Z Plane Discrete Domain]
    SA --> LHP[Left Half S Plane]
    SA --> IMG[Imaginary Axis j Omega]
    SA --> RHP[Right Half S Plane]
    LHP -->|Maps to| INSD[Interior of Unit Circle Stable]
    IMG -->|Maps to| UNC[On Unit Circle Marginally Stable]
    RHP -->|Maps to| OUTD[Exterior of Unit Circle Unstable]
    style LHP fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
    style INSD fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
    style IMG fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    style UNC fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    style RHP fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000
    style OUTD fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000
```

**Engineering interpretation:**
- **Green regions** (LHP ↔ inside unit circle) = stable system poles.
- **Yellow regions** (jω axis ↔ unit circle) = poles giving sustained oscillations.
- **Red regions** (RHP ↔ outside unit circle) = unstable poles — design forbidden.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — ZOH Equivalent in Sampled-Data System

When a ZOH is present, the **single equivalent G(z)** is obtained by treating the ZOH as part of the plant. This is the standard simplification used in KTU problems.

```mermaid
graph LR
    RZ[Input R z] --> GZZ[Equivalent G z equals 1 minus z to power negative 1 times Z of Gp s over s]
    GZZ --> CZ[Output C z]
    RZ -.->|Sampler T| SP1[Sampled Reference R kT]
    CZ -.->|Sampler T| SP2[Sampled Output C kT]
    style GZZ fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366
```

The compact z-domain block on the right contains **the entire ZOH + Plant reduction in a single transfer function**, which is then used in the standard closed-loop formulas.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define pulse transfer function. Why is it defined only for sampled signals?
**Model Answer:** The pulse transfer function G(z) of a linear time-invariant discrete system is the ratio of the Z-transform of the output sequence c(kT) to the Z-transform of the input sequence r(kT), with all initial conditions set to zero: $G(z) = C(z)/R(z)$. It is defined only for sampled signals because the Z-transform operates on discrete-time sequences; if a continuous signal is present between two cascaded blocks, individual Z-transforms cannot be assigned to each block. [3 marks]

### Question 2. What are static error coefficients? List the static error coefficients of a digital control system.
**Model Answer:** Static error coefficients are numerical constants that characterise the ability of a feedback system to follow polynomial inputs (step, ramp, parabolic) with finite steady-state error. For a digital control system, the three coefficients are:
- **Static position error constant** $K_p = \lim_{z \to 1} G(z)$
- **Static velocity error constant** $K_v = \dfrac{1}{T}\lim_{z \to 1}(z-1)G(z)$
- **Static acceleration error constant** $K_a = \dfrac{1}{T^2}\lim_{z \to 1}(z-1)^2 G(z)$

They depend on the system type and the sampling period T. [3 marks]

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes in This Module:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 1/T and 1/T²** in the Kv and Ka definitions. This is the #1 reason students lose 1–2 marks per problem.
> 2. **Applying the Z-transform to cascaded blocks without checking the sampler location.** If no sampler is between the blocks, you must Z-transform the *product*, not the individual factors.
> 3. **Mis-ordering the ZOH derivation** — the factor $(1 - z^{-1})$ must be factored *outside* the Z-transform, not inside.
> 4. **Forgetting to verify the FVT applicability condition** — poles of $E(z)$ must be inside the unit circle except possibly at $z = 1$.
> 5. **Confusing the analog and discrete error constants** — analog Kv is dimensionally different from digital Kv (the factor 1/T restores dimensional consistency).

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Derive the closed-loop pulse transfer function $\dfrac{C(z)}{R(z)}$ for a unity feedback sampled-data control system. Also state the corresponding error transfer function. [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — System equations.** The sampled error is

$$E(z) \;=\; R(z) - C(z)$$

**Step 2 — Forward path.** The output of the forward path is the response of G(z) to E(z):

$$C(z) \;=\; G(z)\cdot E(z)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute E(z).**

$$C(z) \;=\; G(z)\cdot [R(z) - C(z)]$$

$$C(z) \;=\; G(z)R(z) - G(z)C(z)$$

**Step 4 — Collect C(z) terms.**

$$C(z) + G(z)C(z) \;=\; G(z)R(z)$$

$$C(z)\,[1 + G(z)] \;=\; G(z)R(z)$$

**Step 5 — Closed-loop transfer function.**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{C(z)}{R(z)} \;=\; \frac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)}\;}$$

**Step 6 — Error transfer function.**

$$E(z) \;=\; R(z) - C(z) \;=\; R(z) - \frac{G(z)R(z)}{1 + G(z)} \;=\; R(z)\cdot\frac{1 + G(z) - G(z)}{1 + G(z)} \;=\; \frac{R(z)}{1 + G(z)}$$

$$\boxed{\;\frac{E(z)}{R(z)} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + G(z)}\;}$$

[7 marks — 1 for system equation, 1 for forward path, 1 for substitution, 1 for collection, 1 for final form, 1 for error derivation, 1 for correct boxed form]

#### (b) A digital control system has the forward pulse transfer function $G(z) = \dfrac{0.368\,z + 0.264}{(z-1)(z-0.368)}$ with sampling period T = 1 s. Find the static error coefficients and steady-state errors for step, ramp, and parabolic inputs. [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — System type.** Number of poles at z = 1 is 1 ⇒ **Type 1 system**.

**Step 2 — Compute Kp.**

$$K_p \;=\; \lim_{z \to 1} G(z) \;=\; \frac{0.368(1) + 0.264}{(1-1)(1-0.368)} \;=\; \frac{0.632}{0} \;=\; \infty$$

**Step 3 — Compute Kv.**

$$K_v \;=\; \frac{1}{T}\lim_{z \to 1}(z-1)G(z) \;=\; 1 \cdot \frac{0.368\,z + 0.264}{z - 0.368}\bigg|_{z=1} \;=\; \frac{0.632}{0.632} \;=\; 1$$

**Step 4 — Compute Ka.**

$$K_a \;=\; \frac{1}{T^2}\lim_{z \to 1}(z-1)^2 G(z) \;=\; \frac{0.368\,z + 0.264}{1}\bigg|_{z=1}\cdot\lim_{z \to 1}(z-1) \;=\; 0.632 \cdot 0 \;=\; 0$$

**Step 5 — Steady-state errors.**

- **Step:** $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1+K_p} = \dfrac{1}{1+\infty} = \mathbf{0}$
- **Ramp:** $e_{ss} = \dfrac{T}{K_v} = \dfrac{1}{1} = \mathbf{1}$
- **Parabolic:** $e_{ss} = \dfrac{T^2}{K_a} = \dfrac{1}{0} = \mathbf{\infty}$

[7 marks — 1 for type identification, 1 for Kp, 1 for Kv, 1 for Ka, 1 each for step/ramp/parabolic error]

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a sampled-data control system. Derive the pulse transfer function of a ZOH + plant cascade. [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Block diagram description.** (Refer to Section 4.1 diagram.) The reference r(t) is compared with the feedback b(t) to form error e(t). The error is sampled by a sampler of period T, giving e(kT). The digital controller computes the control u(kT). The ZOH reconstructs a continuous signal uh(t), which drives the plant Gp(s). The output c(t) is sensed by H(s) and fed back.

**Step 2 — ZOH transfer function.** The impulse response of a ZOH is a unit rectangle of width T, whose Laplace transform is

$$G_{ZOH}(s) \;=\; \frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}$$

**Step 3 — ZOH + Plant forward path.** The combined continuous transfer function is

$$G_{eq}(s) \;=\; \frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}\cdot G_p(s) \;=\; (1 - e^{-sT})\cdot\frac{G_p(s)}{s}$$

**Step 4 — Z-transform.**

$$G(z) \;=\; \mathcal{Z}\!\left\{(1 - e^{-sT})\cdot\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\} \;=\; \mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\} - \mathcal{Z}\!\left\{e^{-sT}\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\}$$

**Step 5 — Use shifting property.** $\mathcal{Z}\{e^{-sT}F(s)\} = z^{-1}\mathcal{Z}\{F(s)\}$.

$$G(z) \;=\; \mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\} - z^{-1}\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\} \;=\; (1 - z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\}$$

$$\boxed{\;G(z) \;=\; \left(\frac{z-1}{z}\right)\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\}\;}$$

[7 marks — 1 for block diagram, 1 for ZOH TF, 1 for combined s-function, 1 for Z-transform split, 1 for shifting property, 1 for final boxed result, 1 for explanation]

#### (b) Explain the bilinear transformation method. State any four important properties. [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Definition.** The bilinear transformation is a conformal mapping between the s-plane and the z-plane defined by

$$s \;=\; \frac{2}{T}\cdot\frac{z-1}{z+1} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad z \;=\; \frac{2 + sT}{2 - sT}$$

It is derived from the **trapezoidal rule** for numerical integration. To convert an analog transfer function Ha(s) to digital H(z), substitute the above expression for s:

$$H(z) \;=\; H_a(s)\bigg|_{s = (2/T)\cdot(z-1)/(z+1)}$$

**Step 2 — Properties.**

1. **Stability preservation:** The left-half s-plane (Re(s) < 0) maps to the interior of the unit circle (|z| < 1). Hence, a stable analog filter yields a stable digital filter.
2. **Imaginary axis to unit circle:** The jω axis of the s-plane maps to the unit circle |z| = 1 of the z-plane. The mapping is one-to-one.
3. **Conformal mapping:** Both magnitude and phase relationships are preserved locally; angles are preserved.
4. **Frequency pre-warping:** To preserve the magnitude response at a critical frequency ωc, the analog frequency must be pre-warped using $\Omega_c = \dfrac{2}{T}\tan\!\bigl(\dfrac{\omega_c T}{2}\bigr)$.
5. **No aliasing:** Unlike the impulse invariance method, bilinear transformation does not suffer from aliasing because there is no spectral replication.
6. **Algebraic simplicity:** Only algebraic substitution is required; no partial fractions or Z-transform table lookups.

[7 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for substitution, 1 each for four properties (4 marks), 1 for engineering relevance]

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Pin These to Your Wall:**

- ✅ **PTF definition:** $G(z) = C(z)/R(z)$ — requires zero initial conditions.
- ✅ **ZOH transfer function:** $G_{ZOH}(s) = (1 - e^{-sT})/s$. Memorise this — it appears in **every** KTU problem with a ZOH.
- ✅ **ZOH + Plant shortcut:** $G(z) = (1 - z^{-1}) \cdot \mathcal{Z}\{G_p(s)/s\}$. This is the *single most useful* Module-2 identity.
- ✅ **Cascade rules:** Sampler between blocks → multiply G1(z)·G2(z). No sampler → Z-transform the product.
- ✅ **Closed-loop PTF (unity):** $G(z)/[1 + G(z)]$. **Non-unity:** $G(z)/[1 + G(z)H(z)]$.
- ✅ **Error TF:** $E(z)/R(z) = 1/[1 + G(z)]$.
- ✅ **Final Value Theorem:** $e_{ss} = \lim_{z \to 1}[(z-1)/z]\cdot E(z)$. Always check **applicability** (poles of E(z) inside unit circle except possibly at z=1).
- ✅ **Static error constants — do not forget the 1/T factors!**
  - $K_p = \lim_{z \to 1} G(z)$
  - $K_v = (1/T) \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1)G(z)$
  - $K_a = (1/T^2) \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1)^2 G(z)$
- ✅ **Standard test inputs in z-domain:**
  - Step → $z/(z-1)$
  - Ramp → $Tz/(z-1)^2$
  - Parabolic → $T^2 z(z+1)/[2(z-1)^3]$
- ✅ **Steady-state errors:** $1/(1+K_p)$ for step, $T/K_v$ for ramp, $T^2/K_a$ for parabolic.
- ✅ **System type** = number of poles of G(z) at z = 1.
- ✅ **Type vs Error:** Type 0 → finite step error, infinite ramp error. Type 1 → zero step error, finite ramp error, infinite parabolic error. Type 2 → all finite (parabolic included).
- ✅ **Bilinear transformation:** $s = (2/T)(z-1)/(z+1)$. Maps LHP ↔ inside unit circle. Preserves stability. Causes frequency warping — use pre-warping formula $\Omega = (2/T)\tan(\omega T/2)$ at critical frequencies.
- ✅ **Bilinear vs Impulse Invariance:** Bilinear → no aliasing, but warps frequency. Impulse invariance → preserves frequency, but suffers aliasing.
- ✅ **Stability in z-plane:** All closed-loop poles must satisfy |z| < 1. Use Jury's test or bilinear + Routh-Hurwitz for stability analysis.
- ✅ **Engineering applications:** Digital PID controllers (industrial DCS), digital motor control, embedded robotics, aerospace flight control systems, biomedical instrumentation.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — PEAET633 — Discrete Control Systems**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mapping from s-plane to z-plane
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET633 - DISCRETE CONTROL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Mapping from s-plane to z-plane ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **s-plane to z-plane mapping** is the fundamental mathematical transformation that establishes a one-to-many correspondence between points in the continuous-time complex frequency domain (s-plane) and the discrete-time complex frequency domain (z-plane) of a sampled-data control system. The governing relationship is expressed by the complex exponential function: $$z = e^{sT}$$ where: - **$s = \sigma + j\omega$** is the complex Laplace variable (continuous-time frequency). - **$T$** is the **sampling period** (seconds), with units of **seconds (s)**. - **$\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$** is the **sampling frequency** in **radians/second (rad/s)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The mapping $z = e^{sT}$ is **not a one-to-one** (bijective) transformation. A single point in the z-plane corresponds to **infinitely many points** in the s-plane, differing by integer multiples of the sampling frequency $\pm jk\omega_s$. This is the central conceptual hurdle students must master. --- ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing at the top of a long spiral staircase (the s-plane) and looking down at the floor below. Each full turn of the staircase places you directly above a point on the floor (the z-plane). From above, all those staircase landings overlap onto the **same** point on the floor. - The **height (σ)** of each staircase landing above the floor controls the distance from the center of the floor pattern (radius). - The **angular rotation (ωT)** around the spiral controls where you land on the floor pattern (angle). This is precisely the mapping: - The s-plane is an **infinite vertical strip** pattern; the z-plane is the **unit circle floor** that gets repeatedly stamped by the strip from above. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Summary:** The s-plane is a continuous infinite domain; the z-plane "compresses" this infinite domain into the finite unit disk via the exponential map. Stability, dynamics, and frequency response all get translated from a familiar continuous framework (s) to a discrete framework (z) using this elegant transformation. --- ### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights) | Symbol | Meaning | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | $T$ | Sampling period | **seconds (s)** | | $f_s = 1/T$ | Sampling frequency | **Hertz (Hz)** | | $\omega_s = 2\pi/T$ | Sampling angular frequency | **rad/s** | | $\omega_N = \omega_s/2 = \pi/T$ | Nyquist frequency | **rad/s** | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown **Step 1: Substitute the complex Laplace variable** Let $s = \sigma + j\omega$. The mapping becomes: $$z = e^{(\sigma + j\omega)T}$$ **Step 2: Apply Euler's exponential identity** Using $e^{a+b} = e^a \cdot e^b$, we split the real and imaginary parts: $$z = e^{\sigma T} \cdot e^{j\omega T}$$ **Step 3: Identify magnitude and phase of z** Recognizing that $e^{j\omega T} = \cos(\omega T) + j\sin(\omega T)$: $$|z| = e^{\sigma T}, \quad \angle z = \omega T = \theta$$ **Step 4: Interpret geometrically** - The **magnitude** $|z|$ depends *only* on the real part $\sigma$ of $s$. - The **angle** $\theta$ depends *only* on the imaginary part $\omega$ of $s$. **Step 5: Stability mapping inference** - If $\sigma < 0$ (Left Half Plane — LHP of s-plane) → $|z| = e^{\sigma T} < 1$ → **Inside** the unit circle. - If $\sigma = 0$ (Imaginary axis) → $|z| = 1$ → **On** the unit circle. - If $\sigma > 0$ (Right Half Plane — RHP of s-plane) → $|z| > 1$ → **Outside** the unit circle. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Insight:** The imaginary axis of the s-plane maps to the unit circle of the z-plane. The LHP of s maps to the **interior** of the unit circle. **Stability in z-domain = poles must lie strictly inside the unit circle.** --- ### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / Application | |---|---|---| | 1 | $z = e^{sT}$ | Fundamental mapping equation | | 2 | $s = \dfrac{1}{T}\ln(z)$ | Inverse mapping (multi-valued) | | 3 | $\vert z \vert = e^{\sigma T}$ | Magnitude relation | | 4 | $\angle z = \omega T = \theta$ | Phase (angle) relation | | 5 | $\omega = \dfrac{\theta}{T}$ | Discrete frequency from z-plane angle | | 6 | $s = \sigma + j\omega \rightarrow z = e^{\sigma T}(\cos\omega T + j\sin\omega T)$ | Rectangular form of z | | 7 | Primary strip: $-\dfrac{\omega_s}{2} < \omega < +\dfrac{\omega_s}{2}$ | Fundamental frequency range | | 8 | $\sigma < 0 \Rightarrow \vert z \vert < 1$ | Stability condition (LHP → interior) | | 9 | $\sigma = 0 \Rightarrow \vert z \vert = 1$ | Imaginary axis → unit circle | | 10 | $\sigma > 0 \Rightarrow \vert z \vert > 1$ | RHP → exterior of unit circle | | 11 | $s = 0 \Rightarrow z = 1$ | Origin maps to $z = 1$ | | 12 | $s = \pm j\omega_N = \pm j\dfrac{\omega_s}{2} \Rightarrow z = -1$ | Nyquist boundary points | | 13 | $\omega_d = \dfrac{\theta_d}{T}$ | Discrete-time frequency conversion | | 14 | $\Omega = \omega T$ | Normalized digital frequency (radians/sample) | --- ### Real-World Engineering Utility The s-to-z mapping is the **cornerstone of digital redesign** of analog controllers. Practically, every digital controller (in DSP chips, microcontrollers, PLCs) is designed by: 1. Synthesizing the controller in the s-domain (where classical control theory applies). 2. **Discretizing it via the mapping $z = e^{sT}$** to obtain a realizable difference equation. This mapping is also critical in: - **Digital filter design** (Bilinear transform is a refined version of this concept). - **Sampled-data aerospace systems** (flight control computers sampling sensor data). - **Process control industries** (DCS/PLC-based PID implementation). - **Biomedical signal processing** (ECG/EEG digital filtering). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### Derivation 1: Magnitude and Angle of z in terms of s **Given:** $$s = \sigma + j\omega, \quad z = e^{sT}$$ **Step 1.** Substitute $s$ into the mapping equation: $$z = e^{(\sigma + j\omega)T}$$ **Step 2.** Use the property $e^{a+b} = e^a \cdot e^b$: $$z = e^{\sigma T} \cdot e^{j\omega T}$$ **Step 3.** Apply Euler's identity $e^{j\phi} = \cos\phi + j\sin\phi$: $$z = e^{\sigma T}\left[\cos(\omega T) + j\sin(\omega T)\right]$$ **Step 4.** Identify the polar form $z = |z|e^{j\theta}$: $$\begin{aligned} |z| &= e^{\sigma T} \\ \theta &= \omega T \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5.** Inverse — express $s$ in terms of $z$: $$\begin{aligned} |z| = e^{\sigma T} &\Rightarrow \ln|z| = \sigma T \Rightarrow \sigma = \frac{\ln|z|}{T} \\ \theta = \omega T &\Rightarrow \omega = \frac{\theta}{T} \end{aligned}$$ --- ### Derivation 2: Mapping of Key s-plane Points to z-plane **Step 1.** Map $s = 0$ (origin): $$z = e^{0 \cdot T} = e^{0} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s=0 \to z=1$$ **Step 2.** Map $s = \pm j\omega_s/2 = \pm j\pi/T$ (Nyquist points): $$z = e^{\pm j(\pi/T) \cdot T} = e^{\pm j\pi} = \cos(\pi) \pm j\sin(\pi) = -1$$ So the Nyquist frequency points collapse to the single point $z = -1$. **Step 3.** Map $s = -a$ (real negative, stable pole): $$z = e^{-aT} \Rightarrow |z| = e^{-aT} < 1$$ The point lies strictly **inside** the unit circle on the positive real axis. **Step 4.** Map $s = a$ (real positive, unstable pole): $$z = e^{aT} \Rightarrow |z| = e^{aT} > 1$$ The point lies **outside** the unit circle. **Step 5.** Map a complex conjugate pair $s = -a \pm j\omega_d$: $$z = e^{(-a \pm j\omega_d)T} = e^{-aT} e^{\pm j\omega_d T}$$ So the z-plane complex pair has magnitude $e^{-aT}$ and angle $\pm\omega_d T$, which lies **inside** the unit circle (since $a > 0$). --- ### Derivation 3: Mapping of Constant Damping Lines A line of constant damping ratio $\zeta$ in the s-plane has the equation: $$s = -\zeta\omega_n + j\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$$ In terms of the angle from negative real axis: $\beta = \cos^{-1}(\zeta)$. Substituting into the mapping: $$z = e^{-\zeta\omega_n T} \cdot e^{j\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}\,T}$$ The **locus** of $z$ as $\omega_n$ varies from $0$ to $\infty$ is a **logarithmic spiral** in the z-plane, originating from $z = 1$ (when $\omega_n = 0$) and spiraling inward (for $\zeta > 0$). --- ### Analytical Matrix: Complete s-Plane to z-Plane Mapping | s-Plane Region | Condition | z-Plane Region | Engineering Interpretation | |---|---|---|---| | Origin | $s = 0$ | $z = 1$ | DC / steady-state reference | | Imaginary axis | $\sigma = 0$ | Unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ | Sustained oscillation boundary | | Left Half Plane (LHP) | $\sigma < 0$ | Inside unit circle $\vert z \vert < 1$ | **Stable** discrete poles | | Right Half Plane (RHP) | $\sigma > 0$ | Outside unit circle $\vert z \vert > 1$ | **Unstable** discrete poles | | Primary strip (top) | $\omega = \omega_s/2$ | $z = -1$ (negative real axis) | Nyquist upper bound | | Primary strip (bottom) | $\omega = -\omega_s/2$ | $z = -1$ (negative real axis) | Nyquist lower bound | | Constant $\sigma$ line | $\sigma = \text{const}$ | Circle of radius $e^{\sigma T}$ | Concentric circles in z | | Constant $\omega$ line | $\omega = \text{const}$ | Ray at angle $\omega T$ | Radial lines from origin | | $s = -\infty$ to $0$ along real axis | — | $z = 0$ to $z = 1$ | Real segment [0, 1) | | $s = 0$ to $+\infty$ along real axis | — | $z = 1$ to $z = +\infty$ | Real segment $(1, \infty)$ | --- ### Numerical Example: Mapping a Real Pole **Problem:** A continuous-time system has a pole at $s = -2$. The sampling period is $T = 0.1$ s. Find the corresponding z-plane pole. **Solution:** $$z = e^{sT} = e^{(-2)(0.1)} = e^{-0.2}$$ $$z = 0.8187$$ **Verification using $\ln|z| = \sigma T$:** $$\sigma = \frac{\ln(0.8187)}{0.1} = \frac{-0.2}{0.1} = -2 \quad \checkmark$$ **Conclusion:** The discrete-time pole lies at $z = 0.8187$, which is **inside** the unit circle, confirming stability. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### Diagram 1: s-Plane to z-Plane Mapping Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Continuous-time s-plane
s = sigma + j omega"] --> B["Exponential Mapping
z = e to the power sT"] B --> C["Discrete-time z-plane
z = x + jy"] A1["LHP: sigma less than 0"] --> B A2["Imaginary axis: sigma = 0"] --> B A3["RHP: sigma greater than 0"] --> B B --> C1["Inside unit circle: mod z less than 1
STABLE REGION"] B --> C2["On unit circle: mod z = 1
MARGINALLY STABLE"] B --> C3["Outside unit circle: mod z greater than 1
UNSTABLE REGION"] D["Primary Strip
omega between minus omega_s by 2 and omega_s by 2"] --> B style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc style C fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600 style B fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32 style C1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style C2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style C3 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c style D fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c ``` --- ### Diagram 2: Stability Region Correspondence ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Splane["S-PLANE (Continuous Domain)"] S1["LHP - Stable Region"] S2["Imaginary Axis - Marginally Stable"] S3["RHP - Unstable Region"] end subgraph Zplane["Z-PLANE (Discrete Domain)"] Z1["Interior of Unit Circle - Stable"] Z2["Unit Circle - Marginally Stable"] Z3["Exterior of Unit Circle - Unstable"] end S1 -- "z = e to power sT
sT less than 0" --> Z1 S2 -- "z = e to power j omega T
mod z = 1" --> Z2 S3 -- "sT greater than 0" --> Z3 style S1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style S2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style S3 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c style Z1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style Z2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style Z3 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c ``` --- ### Diagram 3: Mapping of Primary Strip and Aliasing Folds ```mermaid flowchart TD P0["Primary Strip
omega in minus omega_s by 2 to omega_s by 2"] --> MAP["z = e to power sT"] P1["Strip 1: omega in omega_s by 2 to 3 omega_s by 2"] --> MAP Pn1["Strip minus 1: omega in minus 3 omega_s by 2 to minus omega_s by 2"] --> MAP Pn["Strip n: General kth strip"] --> MAP MAP --> Z1["Unit Disk in z-plane
mapped INFINITELY MANY TIMES"] N1["s = 0 maps to z = 1"] -.-> Z1 N2["s = plus minus j omega_s by 2 maps to z = minus 1"] -.-> Z1 style P0 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 style P1 fill:#c5cae9,stroke:#1a237e style Pn1 fill:#c5cae9,stroke:#1a237e style Pn fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92 style MAP fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style Z1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style N1 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style N2 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c ``` > [!NOTE] > **Visual Takeaway:** The s-plane consists of infinite horizontal strips of width $\omega_s$, all of which **fold** onto the same unit disk in the z-plane. Only the **primary strip** ($-\omega_s/2 \le \omega \le \omega_s/2$) is uniquely mapped; all other strips produce **aliases** (overlapping shadows) of the primary strip's mapping. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** State the mathematical relationship that maps points from the s-plane to the z-plane. Explain the role of the sampling period in this mapping. **Model Answer:** The s-plane to z-plane mapping is defined by the complex exponential: $$z = e^{sT}$$ where $s = \sigma + j\omega$ is the Laplace variable and $T$ is the **sampling period** in seconds. **Role of sampling period T:** - It acts as the **scaling factor** that determines how the continuous-time frequency $\omega$ maps onto the discrete angular position $\theta = \omega T$ in the z-plane. - A **larger T** causes the same $\omega$ to be mapped to a **larger angle**, potentially causing frequency aliasing. - A **smaller T** preserves the original frequency information with finer resolution. The mapping equation can be decomposed as: $$z = e^{\sigma T} \cdot e^{j\omega T}$$ The magnitude $|z| = e^{\sigma T}$ depends on damping, while the angle $\theta = \omega T$ depends on frequency. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Explain why the mapping from s-plane to z-plane is not one-to-one. What is the significance of the primary strip? **Model Answer:** The mapping $z = e^{sT}$ is **not one-to-one** because of the periodicity of the complex exponential. Consider two points in the s-plane: $$s_1 = \sigma + j\omega, \quad s_2 = \sigma + j(\omega + 2\pi k/T) = \sigma + j(\omega + k\omega_s)$$ Both map to the **same point** in the z-plane: $$z_1 = e^{s_1 T} = e^{\sigma T} e^{j\omega T}$$ $$z_2 = e^{s_2 T} = e^{\sigma T} e^{j(\omega + k\omega_s)T} = e^{\sigma T} e^{j\omega T} e^{j 2\pi k} = z_1$$ This shows that **infinitely many** s-plane points differing by integer multiples of $j\omega_s$ map to a single z-plane point. **Significance of the primary strip:** The primary strip is defined as: $$-\frac{\omega_s}{2} \le \omega \le \frac{\omega_s}{2}$$ It is the **unique** horizontal strip in the s-plane of width equal to the sampling frequency, such that every point in the entire s-plane has exactly **one alias** lying within this strip. Hence, the primary strip provides a **bijective (one-to-one)** representation of the z-plane and is used as the standard reference for discrete-time analysis. --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]** **(a)** Derive the relationship between s-plane poles and z-plane poles. Show explicitly that: - (i) The imaginary axis of the s-plane maps onto the unit circle in the z-plane. - (ii) A pole in the left half of the s-plane maps to a pole inside the unit circle in the z-plane. **(b)** A continuous-time system has poles at $s_1 = -1$ and $s_2 = -2 + j4$. If the sampling period is $T = 0.5$ s, determine the corresponding z-plane pole locations. Comment on the stability of the discrete-time system. --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Derivation:** Given the mapping $z = e^{sT}$ and $s = \sigma + j\omega$: **Step 1.** Substitute: $$z = e^{(\sigma + j\omega)T} = e^{\sigma T} \cdot e^{j\omega T}$$ **Step 2.** Identify the polar form: $$|z| = e^{\sigma T}, \quad \angle z = \omega T$$ **(i) Imaginary axis mapping:** When the point lies on the imaginary axis, $\sigma = 0$: $$|z| = e^{0 \cdot T} = e^0 = 1$$ Hence every point on the $j\omega$-axis of the s-plane maps to a point on the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ in the z-plane. The angle varies as $\theta = \omega T$. **(ii) LHP mapping:** When the point lies in the LHP, $\sigma < 0$. Let $\sigma = -a$ where $a > 0$: $$|z| = e^{-aT} = \frac{1}{e^{aT}} < 1$$ Since $a > 0$ and $T > 0$, $e^{aT} > 1$, so $\vert z \vert < 1$. Hence every LHP point maps to a point **strictly inside** the unit circle. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Step 1.** For pole $s_1 = -1$, $T = 0.5$ s: $$z_1 = e^{s_1 T} = e^{(-1)(0.5)} = e^{-0.5}$$ $$z_1 = 0.6065$$ **Step 2.** For pole $s_2 = -2 + j4$, $T = 0.5$ s: $$z_2 = e^{(-2 + j4)(0.5)} = e^{-1} \cdot e^{j2}$$ $$|z_2| = e^{-1} = 0.3679$$ $$\angle z_2 = 2 \text{ rad} = 114.59°$$ In rectangular form: $$z_2 = 0.3679[\cos(2) + j\sin(2)]$$ $$z_2 = 0.3679[-0.4161 + j\,0.9093]$$ $$z_2 = -0.1531 + j\,0.3346$$ **Step 3.** The conjugate pole $s_3 = -2 - j4$ maps to: $$z_3 = z_2^* = -0.1531 - j\,0.3346$$ **Stability Comment:** - $\vert z_1 \vert = 0.6065 < 1$ → Stable - $\vert z_2 \vert = 0.3679 < 1$ → Stable - $\vert z_3 \vert = 0.3679 < 1$ → Stable **All three discrete-time poles lie strictly inside the unit circle, hence the sampled system is STABLE.** ✓ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the mapping of the constant-damping ratio line and constant-natural-frequency locus from the s-plane to the z-plane. **(b)** A digital control system has characteristic equation poles in the z-plane at $z_1 = 0.5$ and $z_2 = 0.3 + j0.4$. If the sampling period is $T = 0.2$ s, find the corresponding s-plane pole locations and verify stability in the s-domain. --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation:** **Constant damping ratio line ($\zeta$ = const):** In the s-plane, a line of constant $\zeta$ makes angle $\beta = \cos^{-1}(\zeta)$ with the negative real axis. Parametrically: $$s = -\zeta\omega_n + j\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$$ The mapped z-plane point is: $$z = e^{sT} = e^{-\zeta\omega_n T} \cdot e^{j\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}\,T}$$ As $\omega_n$ varies from $0$ to $\infty$, this traces a **logarithmic spiral** starting at $z = 1$ and spiraling toward the origin (for $\zeta > 0$). **Constant natural frequency locus ($\omega_n$ = const):** Points satisfying $\vert s \vert = \omega_n$ form a **circle of radius $\omega_n$** centered at origin in the s-plane. In the z-plane, this maps to a **closed curve** (approximately cardioid-shaped near the unit circle). **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $z_1 = 0.5$, $z_2 = 0.3 + j0.4$, $T = 0.2$ s **Step 1.** Use inverse mapping: $s = \dfrac{1}{T}\ln(z)$. **For $z_1 = 0.5$:** $$\ln(z_1) = \ln(0.5) = -0.6931$$ $$s_1 = \frac{-0.6931}{0.2} = -3.4657$$ **For $z_2 = 0.3 + j0.4$:** First, find the polar form of $z_2$: $$|z_2| = \sqrt{0.3^2 + 0.4^2} = \sqrt{0.09 + 0.16} = \sqrt{0.25} = 0.5$$ $$\angle z_2 = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.4}{0.3}\right) = 53.13° = 0.9273 \text{ rad}$$ Now: $$\ln(z_2) = \ln|z_2| + j\angle z_2 = \ln(0.5) + j(0.9273)$$ $$\ln(z_2) = -0.6931 + j\,0.9273$$ Dividing by $T$: $$s_2 = \frac{-0.6931 + j\,0.9273}{0.2} = -3.4657 + j\,4.6365$$ The conjugate pole is $s_3 = -3.4657 - j\,4.6365$. **Step 2.** Stability verification in s-domain: All three poles have $\sigma < 0$: - $s_1 = -3.4657$ (real, negative) - $s_2 = -3.4657 + j\,4.6365$ (real part negative) - $s_3 = -3.4657 - j\,4.6365$ (real part negative) **All poles lie in the LEFT HALF PLANE → the continuous-time system is STABLE.** ✓ **Cross-check with z-domain:** $\vert z_1 \vert = 0.5 < 1$, $\vert z_2 \vert = 0.5 < 1$ — all inside the unit circle → also stable. Consistent! ✓ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Sign error in $\sigma$ extraction:** Students often compute $\sigma = \ln|z|$ (forgetting to divide by $T$). Always remember: $\sigma = \dfrac{\ln|z|}{T}$. > 2. **Angle ambiguity:** The angle $\theta = \omega T$ must be expressed in **radians**, not degrees, when computing $s = \sigma + j\omega$. > 3. **Forgetting conjugate poles:** When mapping complex s-plane poles, you **must** map the entire conjugate pair. Forgetting the conjugate loses 50% of the marks in stability questions. > 4. **Confusing stability regions:** Students sometimes say "LHP → unit circle" (wrong). Correct mapping: **LHP → inside unit circle**. > 5. **Multi-valuedness of inverse:** $\ln(z)$ has infinitely many values differing by $j2\pi k$. Always report only the value in the primary strip ($-\pi \le \omega T \le \pi$). --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] The fundamental mapping equation is **$z = e^{sT}$**, where $T$ is the sampling period. - [x] Decompose: **$z = e^{\sigma T} \cdot e^{j\omega T}$** — magnitude and angle. - [x] **Magnitude:** $\vert z \vert = e^{\sigma T}$ — depends only on damping. - [x] **Angle:** $\angle z = \omega T = \theta$ — depends only on frequency. - [x] **Inverse mapping:** $s = \sigma + j\omega = \dfrac{\ln|z|}{T} + j\dfrac{\theta}{T}$. - [x] **Stability rule:** LHP of s-plane → **Inside** unit circle in z-plane. - [x] **Marginal stability:** Imaginary axis of s → **On** unit circle in z. - [x] **Instability:** RHP of s → **Outside** unit circle in z. - [x] **Origin:** $s = 0 \Rightarrow z = 1$. - [x] **Nyquist points:** $s = \pm j\omega_s/2 \Rightarrow z = -1$. - [x] **Primary strip:** $-\omega_s/2 \le \omega \le \omega_s/2$ gives bijective mapping. - [x] The mapping is **many-to-one** — infinitely many s-points map to one z-point. - [x] Constant $\zeta$ lines in s-plane map to **logarithmic spirals** in z-plane. - [x] Constant $\sigma$ lines in s-plane map to **concentric circles** of radius $e^{\sigma T}$ in z-plane. - [x] Constant $\omega$ lines in s-plane map to **radial rays** at angle $\omega T$ in z-plane. - [x] **Discrete frequency:** $\omega_d = \theta/T$ (rad/s); **normalized digital frequency:** $\Omega = \omega T$ (rad/sample). - [x] Sampling frequency: $\omega_s = 2\pi/T$; Nyquist frequency: $\omega_N = \pi/T$.

topics.title: Analysis of discrete time control systems, stability analysis of linear digital control systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET633 - DISCRETE CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Analysis of discrete time control systems, stability stability analysis of linear digital control systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Analysis of Discrete Time Control Systems

### Formal KTU Definition

A **discrete-time control system** is a system in which one or more signals are defined only at discrete instants of time (typically as sampled data). The analysis of such systems is performed in the **z-domain**, analogous to the s-domain analysis of continuous systems, using the z-transform as the mathematical tool.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The pulse transfer function $G(z)$ completely characterizes a linear time-invariant (LTI) discrete system. The output is given by $C(z) = G(z) \cdot R(z)$.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Staircase vs. Ramp):** Imagine walking up a slope. A continuous system is like a smooth ramp — your position changes continuously with time. A discrete-time system is like a staircase — your position is only updated at specific instants (the steps). Even though you only "check" the height at each step, the staircase must still be designed so it doesn't collapse (unstable) or sink into the ground (over-damped). The z-plane is our mathematical "blueprint" of the staircase.

### Key Performance Specifications in Discrete Domain

| Parameter | Definition | Standard Notation |
|-----------|-----------|------------------|
| Sampling period | Time between two consecutive samples | **T** (seconds) |
| Sampling frequency | $f_s = 1/T$ | **Hz** |
| Damping ratio | Ratio of actual to critical damping | $\zeta$ |
| Settling time | Time to reach ±2% of final value | $t_s = 4T/(\zeta \omega_n)$ |
| Natural frequency | Undamped oscillation frequency | $\omega_n$ (rad/sec) |

### Classification of Stability for Discrete Systems

For a **linear shift-invariant** discrete system with characteristic equation:

$$\Delta(z) = a_0 z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + a_2 z^{n-2} + \cdots + a_n = 0$$

The system is **stable**, **marginally stable**, or **unstable** based on the location of roots (poles) in the z-plane:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Criteria (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output — BIBO):**
> 1. **Asymptotically Stable:** All roots lie **strictly inside** the unit circle ($\vert z_i \vert < 1$).
> 2. **Marginally Stable:** Roots lie **on** the unit circle ($\vert z_i \vert = 1$) with no repeated roots.
> 3. **Unstable:** Any root lies **outside** the unit circle ($\vert z_i \vert > 1$) OR any root on the unit circle is repeated.

The **unit circle** in the z-plane plays the role of the imaginary axis in the s-plane.

---

## 1.2 Stability Analysis of Linear Digital Control Systems

### Formal Definition

**Stability analysis** of a linear digital control system refers to the determination of conditions under which the system's response to any bounded input remains bounded, and any disturbance-induced transients decay to zero. In digital systems, this translates to ensuring that all closed-loop poles of the pulse transfer function lie **inside the unit circle** in the z-plane.

### Conceptual Analogy (The Ball on a Hill)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Hill Analogy:** In a continuous system (s-plane), a ball is stable if the hill has a valley below the imaginary axis. In a discrete system (z-plane), stability means the ball always lands **inside a unit-diameter circular bowl** centered at the origin. If the ball ever lands on the rim (unit circle) repeatedly, the ball rolls forever on the edge — marginally stable. If it lands outside — runaway failure.

### Why Stability Analysis is Critical

- A digital controller (e.g., a PID implemented on a microcontroller) computes control actions at discrete instants. If the system is unstable, the controller output can grow unbounded, saturate the actuator, and damage the plant.
- For the **closed-loop pulse transfer function** $\dfrac{C(z)}{R(z)} = \dfrac{G(z)}{1 + GH(z)}$, the denominator $1 + GH(z) = 0$ gives the **characteristic equation**. All its roots must lie inside the unit circle for stability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A common error by students is to apply the **Routh-Hurwitz criterion directly** on the characteristic equation in z. This is **incorrect** because Routh-Hurwitz applies to polynomials in $s$ (left half-plane test), not $z$ (inside unit circle test). Use **Bilinear Transformation** first, then apply Routh-Hurwitz.

### The Three Major Stability Tests Covered in KTU Module 3

| Test | Best Suited For | Effort Level |
|------|-----------------|--------------|
| **Jury's Stability Test** | Direct z-domain polynomials, no transformation needed | Moderate |
| **Bilinear Transformation + Routh-Hurwitz** | Mapping z to w-plane; reusing continuous methods | Easy if transformation mastered |
| **Root Locus in z-plane** | Designing controllers; observing pole movement as gain varies | Graphical |
| **Nyquist Criterion (Discrete)** | Frequency response using $z = e^{j\omega T}$ | Advanced |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mapping Between s-plane and z-plane

The fundamental relationship for sampled systems is:

$$z = e^{sT}$$

This transcendental mapping transforms the left half of the s-plane (stable region) **into the interior of the unit circle** in the z-plane.

| s-plane location | z-plane location |
|------------------|------------------|
| Origin ($s = 0$) | $z = 1$ |
| Imaginary axis ($s = j\omega$) | Unit circle ($\vert z \vert = 1$) |
| Left half-plane (Re $s < 0$) | Inside unit circle ($\vert z \vert < 1$) |
| Right half-plane (Re $s > 0$) | Outside unit circle ($\vert z \vert > 1$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The entire left half of the s-plane ($\omega$ from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$) maps onto the unit circle **only once**, meaning the z-plane is a multi-valued mapping. This is why we restrict $\omega$ to the principal range $-\pi/T < \omega < \pi/T$ (Nyquist frequency range).

## 2.2 Jury's Stability Test

Jury's test is the discrete-time analog of the Routh-Hurwitz test. It checks whether all roots of the characteristic polynomial lie inside the unit circle.

### Procedure (for an $n$-th order polynomial)

Given:

$$F(z) = a_0 z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + a_2 z^{n-2} + \cdots + a_{n-1} z + a_n = 0$$

with $a_0 > 0$. The system is stable **if and only if** the following Jury's conditions are satisfied:

### Jury's Table Construction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield — Jury's Conditions for Stability:**

| Condition | Formula | Meaning |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| **Condition 1** | $F(1) > 0$ | Sum of all coefficients is positive |
| **Condition 2** | $(-1)^n F(-1) > 0$ | Alternating sum has the correct sign |
| **Condition 3** | $\vert a_n \vert < a_0$ | Constant term smaller than leading coefficient |
| **Condition 4** | All determinants in Jury's array are positive | Equivalent to Schur-Cohn conditions |

### Jury's Array (Tabular Method)

Construct an array with rows alternating between the polynomial coefficients and their reversed forms:

| Row | $z^0$ | $z^1$ | $z^2$ | $\cdots$ | $z^n$ |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|----------|-------|
| Row 1 | $a_n$ | $a_{n-1}$ | $a_{n-2}$ | $\cdots$ | $a_0$ |
| Row 2 | $a_0$ | $a_1$ | $a_2$ | $\cdots$ | $a_n$ |
| Row 3 | $b_0$ | $b_1$ | $b_2$ | $\cdots$ | - |
| Row 4 | $b_{n-1}$ | $b_{n-2}$ | $b_{n-3}$ | $\cdots$ | - |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | | |

Where:

$$b_k = \begin{vmatrix} a_n & a_{n-1-k} \\ a_0 & a_{k+1} \end{vmatrix}$$

Continuing this process generates rows of decreasing length, and the system is stable iff **all first elements of each row are positive**.

## 2.3 Bilinear Transformation (W-Plane / w-Plane Mapping)

The bilinear transformation maps the **interior of the unit circle** to the **left half of a new w-plane**, allowing the use of the classical Routh-Hurwitz criterion.

### Standard Bilinear Transformation

$$z = \frac{1 + w}{1 - w} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad w = \frac{z - 1}{z + 1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** This is sometimes called the **Tustin's transformation** when used in controller design. It maps $z = 1 \to w = \infty$, $z = -1 \to w = 0$, and the unit circle ($\vert z \vert = 1$) $\to$ the entire imaginary axis ($j\omega_w$) of the w-plane.

### Mapping Properties

| Feature | z-plane | w-plane |
|---------|---------|---------|
| Stability region | $\vert z \vert < 1$ | Re $w < 0$ |
| Boundary | Unit circle | Imaginary axis |
| Mapping of $z = 1$ | Point $(1, 0)$ | $w = \infty$ |
| Mapping of $z = -1$ | Point $(-1, 0)$ | $w = 0$ |

### Frequency Relationship Between w-plane and Real Frequency

When $z = e^{j\omega T}$ is on the unit circle, the corresponding w-plane point is:

$$w = j\omega_w, \quad \omega_w = \tan\left(\frac{\omega T}{2}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Warping Effect:** The relationship $\omega_w = \tan(\omega T/2)$ is **nonlinear** (it warps frequencies). This is why Tustin's approximation uses a prewarping frequency for accurate design.

## 2.4 Steady-State Error Analysis in Discrete Systems

For a **unity feedback** discrete system with open-loop pulse transfer function $G(z)$, the steady-state error for a step input is:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1) R(z)}{1 + G(z)}$$

### Static Error Constants

| Input | $R(z)$ | $e_{ss}$ formula | Error constant |
|-------|--------|------------------|----------------|
| Unit step | $\dfrac{z}{z-1}$ | $\dfrac{1}{1 + K_p}$ | $K_p = \lim_{z \to 1} G(z)$ |
| Unit ramp | $\dfrac{Tz}{(z-1)^2}$ | $\dfrac{1}{K_v}$ | $K_v = \lim_{z \to 1} \dfrac{(z-1) G(z)}{T}$ |
| Unit parabolic | $\dfrac{T^2 z(z+1)}{2(z-1)^3}$ | $\dfrac{1}{K_a}$ | $K_a = \lim_{z \to 1} \dfrac{(z-1)^2 G(z)}{T^2}$ |

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description |
|---|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $z = e^{sT}$ | Fundamental s-z mapping |
| 2 | $z = (1+w)/(1-w)$ | Bilinear transformation (Tustin) |
| 3 | $w = (z-1)/(z+1)$ | Inverse bilinear transformation |
| 4 | $\omega_w = \tan(\omega T/2)$ | Frequency warping relation |
| 5 | $F(1) > 0$, $(-1)^n F(-1) > 0$ | Jury's first two conditions |
| 6 | $\vert a_n \vert < a_0$ | Jury's third condition |
| 7 | $K_p = \lim_{z \to 1} G(z)$ | Position error constant |
| 8 | $K_v = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1)G(z)/T$ | Velocity error constant |
| 9 | $K_a = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1)^2 G(z)/T^2$ | Acceleration error constant |
| 10 | $C(z)/R(z) = G(z)/[1+GH(z)]$ | Closed-loop pulse transfer function |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Stability analysis via Jury's test is computationally efficient for low-order digital controllers in industrial PLCs, while bilinear + Routh-Hurwitz is the standard in aerospace digital flight-control system certification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bilinear Transformation Frequency Warping

We start with the relationship between the real frequency $\omega$ and the w-plane frequency $\omega_w$.

### Step 1: Substitute $z = e^{j\omega T}$ on the Unit Circle

When the z-plane variable lies on the unit circle, $z = e^{j\omega T}$.

### Step 2: Apply the Bilinear Transformation

$$
\begin{aligned}
w &= \frac{z - 1}{z + 1} \\
w &= \frac{e^{j\omega T} - 1}{e^{j\omega T} + 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Express in Terms of Half-Angle

Using the Euler identity:

$$e^{j\omega T} - 1 = e^{j\omega T/2} \left(e^{j\omega T/2} - e^{-j\omega T/2}\right) = 2j \, e^{j\omega T/2} \sin\left(\frac{\omega T}{2}\right)$$

$$e^{j\omega T} + 1 = e^{j\omega T/2} \left(e^{j\omega T/2} + e^{-j\omega T/2}\right) = 2 \, e^{j\omega T/2} \cos\left(\frac{\omega T}{2}\right)$$

### Step 4: Simplify

$$
\begin{aligned}
w &= \frac{2j \, e^{j\omega T/2} \sin(\omega T/2)}{2 \, e^{j\omega T/2} \cos(\omega T/2)} \\
w &= j \tan\left(\frac{\omega T}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Identify the w-plane Frequency

Since $w = j\omega_w$, we conclude:

$$\boxed{\omega_w = \tan\left(\frac{\omega T}{2}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This proves that the w-plane "frequency" $\omega_w$ is a **nonlinear, tangent function** of the actual physical frequency $\omega$. At low frequencies ($\omega T \ll 1$), $\omega_w \approx \omega T/2$ (linear). At high frequencies near the Nyquist limit ($\omega T \to \pi$), $\omega_w \to \infty$ — the w-plane "stretches" high frequencies to infinity. This is why w-plane Routh-Hurwitz is conservative at high frequencies.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Jury's Stability Test

**Problem:** Determine the stability range of $K$ for the discrete system whose characteristic equation is:

$$F(z) = z^3 + 0.5 z^2 + (K - 0.2) z + 0.1 = 0$$

### Step 1: Apply Condition 1 — $F(1) > 0$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(1) &= 1 + 0.5 + (K - 0.2) + 0.1 \\
F(1) &= 1.4 + K
\end{aligned}
$$

For stability: $1.4 + K > 0 \Rightarrow K > -1.4$

### Step 2: Apply Condition 2 — $(-1)^n F(-1) > 0$

Here $n = 3$, so $(-1)^3 = -1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(-1) &= (-1)^3 + 0.5(-1)^2 + (K-0.2)(-1) + 0.1 \\
F(-1) &= -1 + 0.5 - K + 0.2 + 0.1 \\
F(-1) &= -0.2 - K
\end{aligned}
$$

Condition: $-F(-1) > 0 \Rightarrow -(-0.2 - K) > 0 \Rightarrow 0.2 + K > 0 \Rightarrow K > -0.2$

### Step 3: Apply Condition 3 — $\vert a_n \vert < a_0$

Here $a_0 = 1$, $a_3 = 0.1$.

$$\vert 0.1 \vert < 1 \quad \text{(automatically satisfied, independent of } K\text{)}$$

### Step 4: Apply Condition 4 — Construct Jury's Array

| Row | $z^0$ | $z^1$ | $z^2$ | $z^3$ |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| 1 | $0.1$ | $K-0.2$ | $0.5$ | $1$ |
| 2 | $1$ | $0.5$ | $K-0.2$ | $0.1$ |
| 3 | $b_0$ | $b_1$ | $b_2$ | - |

Compute $b_k$ using:

$$b_k = \begin{vmatrix} a_n & a_{n-1-k} \\ a_0 & a_{k+1} \end{vmatrix}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_0 &= \begin{vmatrix} 0.1 & K-0.2 \\ 1 & 0.5 \end{vmatrix} = (0.1)(0.5) - (K-0.2)(1) = 0.05 - K + 0.2 = 0.25 - K \\
b_1 &= \begin{vmatrix} 0.1 & 0.5 \\ 1 & K-0.2 \end{vmatrix} = (0.1)(K-0.2) - (0.5)(1) = 0.1K - 0.02 - 0.5 = 0.1K - 0.52 \\
b_2 &= \begin{vmatrix} 0.1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0.1 \end{vmatrix} = (0.1)(0.1) - (1)(1) = 0.01 - 1 = -0.99
\end{aligned}
$$

The next row (Row 4) is the reverse of Row 3:
Row 4: $-0.99$, $0.1K - 0.52$, $0.25 - K$

For Row 5, compute $c_0$:

$$c_0 = \begin{vmatrix} 0.25 - K & -0.99 \\ -0.99 & 0.25 - K \end{vmatrix} = (0.25 - K)^2 - (-0.99)^2$$

For stability: $c_0 > 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.25 - K)^2 &> 0.9801 \\
\vert 0.25 - K \vert &> 0.99 \\
K - 0.25 &> 0.99 \quad \text{or} \quad 0.25 - K > 0.99 \\
K &> 1.24 \quad \text{or} \quad K < -0.74
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Combine All Conditions

| Condition | Result |
|-----------|--------|
| $F(1) > 0$ | $K > -1.4$ |
| $(-1)^3 F(-1) > 0$ | $K > -0.2$ |
| $\vert a_3 \vert < a_0$ | Always true |
| $c_0 > 0$ | $K > 1.24$ or $K < -0.74$ |

**Intersection:** $K > 1.24$ (since the first three demand $K > -0.2$, the binding constraint is the last: $K > 1.24$).

> [!TIP]
> **Verification using Bilinear Transformation (Alternative):** Substituting $z = (1+w)/(1-w)$ and forming a Routh array gives the same result. The w-plane Routh-Hurwitz method is faster for second and third-order systems.

---

## 3.3 Steady-State Error Derivation for a Type-1 System

Consider a sampled unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function:

$$G(z) = \frac{(z - z_1)(z - z_2)\cdots}{(z - p_1)(z - p_2)\cdots(z - 1)^N}$$

where $N$ is the **system type** (number of poles at $z = 1$, equivalent to integrators).

### Step 1: Velocity Error Constant

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_v &= \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1) G(z)}{T} \\
&= \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1) (z - z_1)(z - z_2)\cdots}{T (z - p_1)(z - p_2)\cdots (z - 1)^N} \\
&= \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z - z_1)(z - z_2)\cdots}{T (z - p_1)(z - p_2)\cdots (z - 1)^{N-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For Type-1 ($N = 1$):

$$K_v = \frac{1}{T} \cdot \frac{\prod (1 - z_i)}{\prod (1 - p_i)}$$

### Step 2: Ramp Steady-State Error

$$e_{ss,\text{ramp}} = \frac{1}{K_v}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The velocity error constant $K_v$ for a discrete system has units of $\text{sec}^{-1}$ (just like the continuous case), and the steady-state error for a unit ramp is simply $1/K_v$ — a finite value for a Type-1 system, infinite for a Type-0 system, and zero for Type-2 or higher.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary: Discrete vs. Continuous Stability Tests

| Feature | Continuous (s-plane) | Discrete (z-plane) |
|---------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Stability region | Re $s < 0$ (left half-plane) | $\vert z \vert < 1$ (interior of unit circle) |
| Boundary | Imaginary axis | Unit circle |
| Standard test | Routh-Hurwitz | Jury's Test |
| Frequency transformation | None | $\omega_w = \tan(\omega T/2)$ |
| Nyquist contour | Encloses right half-plane | Encloses outside of unit circle |
| Steady-state error constants | $K_p, K_v, K_a$ at $s \to 0$ | $K_p, K_v, K_a$ at $z \to 1$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** The mapping $s = \sigma + j\omega$ does not have a one-to-one correspondence with z. Multiple s-plane points map to the same z-plane point due to aliasing. This is the **aliasing phenomenon** unique to discrete systems.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Discrete Control System Stability Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Plant Gp s and Digital Controller D z"] --> B["Identify Closed Loop Pulse Transfer Function"]
    B --> C["Form Characteristic Equation 1 plus G open z equals 0"]
    C --> D["Choose Stability Test Method"]
    D --> E1["Jury Test Direct z domain"]
    D --> E2["Bilinear w plane then Routh Hurwitz"]
    D --> E3["Root Locus in z plane"]
    D --> E4["Nyquist in z plane"]
    E1 --> F["Apply Jury Conditions F1 greater than 0 and F minus 1 sign and determinant test"]
    E2 --> G["Substitute z equals 1 plus w over 1 minus w"]
    E3 --> H["Find Open Loop Poles and Zeros plot in z plane"]
    E4 --> I["Map unit circle to j omega axis use z equals e to j omega T"]
    F --> J["Check all roots inside unit circle"]
    G --> K["Apply Routh Hurwitz to polynomial in w"]
    H --> L["Determine stability range from root locus"]
    I --> M["Apply Nyquist criterion in z domain"]
    J --> N["Final Result STABLE or UNSTABLE"]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

