# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: State the stability condition for a linear discrete-time system in terms of pole locations in the z-plane.**

**Model Answer:**

A linear discrete-time system described by the pulse transfer function $G(z)$ is **asymptotically stable** if and only if **all poles of the closed-loop transfer function lie strictly inside the unit circle** in the z-plane, i.e., for every pole $z_i$ of the closed-loop transfer function, $\vert z_i \vert < 1$. If any pole lies on the unit circle (with no repeated poles), the system is marginally stable. If any pole lies outside the unit circle, or there is a repeated pole on the unit circle, the system is unstable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (KTU Pattern):** Definition (1 mark) + Mathematical condition (1 mark) + Marginal/unstable explanation (1 mark).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q: What is the bilinear transformation? Why is it used in discrete system stability analysis?**

**Model Answer:**

The **bilinear transformation** is the mapping:

$$z = \frac{1 + w}{1 - w} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad w = \frac{z - 1}{z + 1}$$

It is used because it maps the **interior of the unit circle** in the z-plane to the **left half of the w-plane** (where Re $w < 0$). This allows the well-known **Routh-Hurwitz criterion** (valid for left-half plane polynomials in $s$) to be applied to the transformed characteristic equation, enabling stability analysis of discrete systems using familiar continuous-time methods.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** Transformation formula (1.5 marks) + Reason for use (1.5 marks).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 3 Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — **Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Determine the stability of the system whose characteristic equation is given by:

$$F(z) = z^3 - 1.2 z^2 + 0.07 z + 0.3 = 0$$

**using Jury's stability test.** [7 marks]

**(b)** A sampled-data control system has the open-loop pulse transfer function:

$$G(z) = \frac{0.5 K}{(z - 1)(z - 0.5)}$$

Determine the range of $K$ for which the closed-loop system is stable using the **bilinear transformation** and **Routh-Hurwitz criterion**. [7 marks]

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Given:** $F(z) = z^3 - 1.2z^2 + 0.07z + 0.3 = 0$

**Coefficients:** $a_0 = 1$, $a_1 = -1.2$, $a_2 = 0.07$, $a_3 = 0.3$, $n = 3$.

**Condition 1: $F(1) > 0$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(1) &= 1 - 1.2 + 0.07 + 0.3 \\
&= 0.17 > 0 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Condition 2: $(-1)^n F(-1) > 0$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(-1) &= (-1)^3 - 1.2(-1)^2 + 0.07(-1) + 0.3 \\
&= -1 - 1.2 - 0.07 + 0.3 \\
&= -1.97
\end{aligned}
$$

Check: $(-1)^3 \cdot F(-1) = (-1)(-1.97) = 1.97 > 0$ ✓

**Condition 3: $\vert a_n \vert < a_0$**

$$\vert 0.3 \vert < 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Condition 4: Jury's Array**

| Row | $z^0$ | $z^1$ | $z^2$ | $z^3$ |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| 1 | $0.3$ | $0.07$ | $-1.2$ | $1$ |
| 2 | $1$ | $-1.2$ | $0.07$ | $0.3$ |
| 3 | $b_0$ | $b_1$ | $b_2$ | - |

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_0 &= \begin{vmatrix} 0.3 & 0.07 \\ 1 & -1.2 \end{vmatrix} = (0.3)(-1.2) - (0.07)(1) = -0.36 - 0.07 = -0.43 \\
b_1 &= \begin{vmatrix} 0.3 & -1.2 \\ 1 & 0.07 \end{vmatrix} = (0.3)(0.07) - (-1.2)(1) = 0.021 + 1.2 = 1.221 \\
b_2 &= \begin{vmatrix} 0.3 & 1 \\ 1 & 0.3 \end{vmatrix} = 0.09 - 1 = -0.91
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $b_0 = -0.43 < 0$, **Condition 4 fails**.

**Conclusion:** The system is **UNSTABLE**.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1: Closed-Loop Characteristic Equation**

The closed-loop characteristic equation is $1 + G(z) = 0$:

$$(z - 1)(z - 0.5) + 0.5K = 0$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
z^2 - 1.5z + 0.5 + 0.5K &= 0 \\
z^2 - 1.5z + (0.5 + 0.5K) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Bilinear Transformation $z = (1+w)/(1-w)$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right)^2 - 1.5 \left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right) + 0.5 + 0.5K &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiply through by $(1-w)^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1+w)^2 - 1.5(1+w)(1-w) + (0.5 + 0.5K)(1-w)^2 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Expanding:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1 + 2w + w^2) - 1.5(1 - w^2) + (0.5 + 0.5K)(1 - 2w + w^2) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 + 2w + w^2 - 1.5 + 1.5w^2 + (0.5 + 0.5K) - 2(0.5 + 0.5K)w + (0.5 + 0.5K)w^2 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Collecting terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Constant:} \quad & 1 - 1.5 + (0.5 + 0.5K) = 0.5K \\
w \text{ term:} \quad & 2 - 2(0.5 + 0.5K) = 2 - 1 - K = 1 - K \\
w^2 \text{ term:} \quad & 1 + 1.5 + (0.5 + 0.5K) = 3 + 0.5K
\end{aligned}
$$

So the w-domain polynomial is:

$$(3 + 0.5K)w^2 + (1 - K)w + 0.5K = 0$$

**Step 3: Apply Routh-Hurwitz Criterion**

For a 2nd-order polynomial $a_2 w^2 + a_1 w + a_0$ with $a_2 > 0$, stability requires all coefficients positive:

| Condition | Inequality |
|-----------|------------|
| $a_2 > 0$ | $3 + 0.5K > 0 \Rightarrow K > -6$ |
| $a_1 > 0$ | $1 - K > 0 \Rightarrow K < 1$ |
| $a_0 > 0$ | $0.5K > 0 \Rightarrow K > 0$ |

**Step 4: Combined Range**

$$\boxed{0 < K < 1}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **Choice Option 2 (Alternative to Question A)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** For the discrete system with characteristic equation:

$$F(z) = z^4 - 1.3 z^3 + 0.4 z^2 + 0.3 z - 0.1 = 0$$

**test stability using Jury's method** and clearly state each condition checked. [7 marks]

**(b)** A unity feedback discrete control system has the forward path transfer function:

$$G(z) = \frac{K(z + 0.5)}{(z - 1)(z - 0.8)}$$

Compute the **static error constants** $K_p$, $K_v$, $K_a$ and the **steady-state error** for a unit ramp input. Take $T = 1$ sec. [7 marks]

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — Question B

**Given:** $F(z) = z^4 - 1.3z^3 + 0.4z^2 + 0.3z - 0.1$

Coefficients: $a_0 = 1$, $a_1 = -1.3$, $a_2 = 0.4$, $a_3 = 0.3$, $a_4 = -0.1$, $n = 4$.

**Condition 1: $F(1) > 0$**

$$F(1) = 1 - 1.3 + 0.4 + 0.3 - 0.1 = 0.3 > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Condition 2: $(-1)^n F(-1) > 0$**

$$F(-1) = 1 - (-1.3) + 0.4 - 0.3 - 0.1 = 1 + 1.3 + 0.4 - 0.3 - 0.1 = 2.3$$

Check: $(-1)^4 \cdot F(-1) = 1 \cdot 2.3 = 2.3 > 0$ ✓

**Condition 3: $\vert a_n \vert < a_0$**

$$\vert -0.1 \vert = 0.1 < 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Condition 4: Jury's Array Construction**

| Row | $z^0$ | $z^1$ | $z^2$ | $z^3$ | $z^4$ |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| 1 | $-0.1$ | $0.3$ | $0.4$ | $-1.3$ | $1$ |
| 2 | $1$ | $-1.3$ | $0.4$ | $0.3$ | $-0.1$ |

Row 3 ($b$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_0 &= \begin{vmatrix} -0.1 & 0.3 \\ 1 & -1.3 \end{vmatrix} = 0.13 - 0.3 = -0.17 \\
b_1 &= \begin{vmatrix} -0.1 & 0.4 \\ 1 & 0.4 \end{vmatrix} = -0.04 - 0.4 = -0.44 \\
b_2 &= \begin{vmatrix} -0.1 & -1.3 \\ 1 & 0.3 \end{vmatrix} = -0.03 + 1.3 = 1.27 \\
b_3 &= \begin{vmatrix} -0.1 & 1 \\ 1 & -0.1 \end{vmatrix} = 0.01 - 1 = -0.99
\end{aligned}
$$

Row 4 (reverse of Row 3): $-0.99$, $1.27$, $-0.44$, $-0.17$

Since $b_0 = -0.17 < 0$, **Condition 4 fails**.

**Conclusion:** The system is **UNSTABLE**.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — Question B

**Given:** $G(z) = \dfrac{K(z+0.5)}{(z-1)(z-0.8)}$ with $T = 1$ sec.

**System Type:** There is **one pole at $z = 1$** → **Type-1 system**.

**Position Error Constant $K_p$:**

$$K_p = \lim_{z \to 1} G(z) = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{K(z+0.5)}{(z-1)(z-0.8)}$$

As $z \to 1$, the term $(z - 1)$ in the denominator makes $K_p \to \infty$.

$$K_p = \infty \Rightarrow e_{ss}(\text{step}) = \frac{1}{1 + K_p} = 0$$

**Velocity Error Constant $K_v$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_v &= \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1) G(z)}{T} \\
&= \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1) \cdot K(z+0.5)}{T \cdot (z-1)(z-0.8)} \\
&= \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{K(z+0.5)}{T(z-0.8)} \\
&= \frac{K(1+0.5)}{1 \cdot (1 - 0.8)} = \frac{1.5 K}{0.2} = 7.5 K
\end{aligned}
$$

**Steady-state error for unit ramp:**

$$\boxed{e_{ss}(\text{ramp}) = \frac{1}{K_v} = \frac{1}{7.5K}}$$

**Acceleration Error Constant $K_a$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_a &= \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)^2 G(z)}{T^2} = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1) \cdot K(z+0.5)}{T^2 (z-0.8)} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

So $K_a = 0$, meaning $e_{ss}(\text{parabolic}) = \infty$.

> [!TIP]
> **Verification:** A Type-1 discrete system has $e_{ss} = 0$ for step, finite for ramp, infinite for parabolic — exactly matching the analogous continuous-time Type-1 system.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Traps in KTU Module 3 (Discrete Stability):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $(-1)^n$ in Condition 2 of Jury's test.** Students often write $F(-1) > 0$ without the alternating sign. For odd $n$, the sign flips. **Penalty: −2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Confusing the stability boundary.** In s-plane, stability is "left of imaginary axis." In z-plane, stability is "inside unit circle." Applying Routh-Hurwitz directly on a z-polynomial is a conceptual error. **Penalty: −3 marks.**
>
> 3. **Missing the $T$ factor in $K_v$ and $K_a$ formulas.** Discrete error constants include the sampling period $T$ in the denominator, unlike continuous systems. Forgetting $T$ gives wrong units. **Penalty: −1 to −2 marks.**
>
> 4. **In Jury's array, dividing the rows incorrectly.** Some students multiply by powers of $z$ to "normalize" rows — this is unnecessary and often leads to sign errors. Keep coefficients in the standard form $a_0 z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + \cdots$.
>
> 5. **Neglecting the closed-loop characteristic equation.** The test should be applied to $1 + GH(z) = 0$, **not** to $G(z)$'s denominator alone. **Penalty: −2 marks.**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3**
>
> - **Stability in z-plane** requires all closed-loop poles inside the unit circle ($\vert z_i \vert < 1$).
> - **Unit circle** in z-plane = **Imaginary axis** in s-plane (stability boundary).
> - **Bilinear transformation:** $z = (1+w)/(1-w)$ converts the unit-circle test into a left-half-plane test in w.
> - **Frequency warping:** $\omega_w = \tan(\omega T/2)$ is the fundamental relationship; w-plane "sees" frequencies in a nonlinear, tangent-warped way.
> - **Jury's test** has 4 conditions: (1) $F(1) > 0$, (2) $(-1)^n F(-1) > 0$, (3) $\vert a_n \vert < a_0$, (4) Jury's array first elements all positive.
> - **Static error constants in discrete domain:** $K_p = \lim_{z \to 1} G(z)$, $K_v = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1)G(z)/T$, $K_a = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1)^2 G(z)/T^2$.
> - **System type** is determined by the number of poles of $G(z)$ at $z = 1$ (analogous to poles at $s = 0$ in continuous systems).
> - **Steady-state errors:** Type-0 → step has finite $e_{ss}$, ramp infinite; Type-1 → step zero, ramp finite, parabola infinite; Type-2 → step zero, ramp zero, parabola finite.
> - **Nyquist in z-plane** uses $z = e^{j\omega T}$ on the unit circle, $\omega \in [0, \pi/T]$.
> - **Root locus in z-plane** is plotted with the unit circle as the critical boundary, replacing the imaginary axis.
> - **Fundamental mapping:** $z = e^{sT}$ — the **s-plane is wrapped** into the z-plane in strips of width $2\pi/T$ along the imaginary axis.
> - **Final Value Theorem** in z-domain: $\lim_{k \to \infty} f(kT) = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1) F(z)$, applicable only when all poles of $(z-1)F(z)$ are strictly inside the unit circle.
> - **Characteristic equation for closed-loop:** $1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$ — always form this **before** applying any test.

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Stability in Digital Control Systems — The Foundation

In **Discrete Control Systems**, a sampled-data system is considered **BIBO stable** (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output stable) if and only if **all poles of the closed-loop transfer function lie strictly inside the unit circle in the z-plane**, i.e., $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ for every closed-loop pole $z_i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 3 Anchor Concept:**
> Unlike continuous-time systems where stability = poles in the **Left-Half s-plane (LHP)**, in discrete-time systems stability = poles **inside the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$ in the z-plane. This is the single most important conceptual shift Module 3 demands.

### 1.2 The Three Master Tools of Module 3

Since the stability region has changed shape (from an infinite LHP to a finite unit disc), KTU requires **three equivalent analytical techniques** to test and design for stability:

| # | Tool | Plane of Operation | Approach |
|---|------|--------------------|----------|
| 1 | **Routh–Hurwitz Criterion** | w-plane (via bilinear transform) | Indirect — converts to continuous equivalent |
| 2 | **Jury's Stability Test** | z-plane (direct) | Tabular — analogous to Routh array |
| 3 | **Root Locus Technique** | z-plane (direct) | Graphical — angle & magnitude conditions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Three Roads to the Same Mountain"**
> Imagine the unit circle in the z-plane as a circular fortress. A closed-loop system is stable only if all its "soldier poles" are trapped *inside* the walls. **Jury's test** checks the prisoners directly at the gate. **Routh–Hurwitz (with bilinear w-transform)** warps the circular fortress into a straight wall (imaginary axis) and then counts prisoners. **Root locus** traces the *paths* soldiers take as a system gain $K$ is varied.

### 1.3 Why Not Apply Routh–Hurwitz Directly to z?

The classical Routh–Hurwitz criterion was designed to count roots in the **Left-Half Plane**. The z-plane's stability region is a circle — not a half-plane. Therefore, **direct application fails**. Two remedies exist:

1. **Bilinear (W-transform) substitution** — map the unit circle to the imaginary axis so Routh–Hurwitz becomes valid.
2. **Jury's test** — a purely algebraic tabular test that works *directly* on the z-polynomial.

### 1.4 Stability Boundaries in the Three Planes

| Plane | Stability Region | Boundary |
|-------|------------------|----------|
| s-plane | $\text{Re}(s) < 0$ (LHP) | Imaginary axis $s = j\omega$ |
| z-plane | $\vert z \vert < 1$ (interior) | Unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ |
| w-plane | $\text{Re}(w) < 0$ (LHP) | Imaginary axis $w = j\omega_w$ |

> [!TIP]
> The mapping $z = \frac{1 + (T/2)w}{1 - (T/2)w}$ takes the **Left-Half w-plane** to the **interior of the unit circle in z-plane**. This is the **Bilinear Transformation** with pre-warping, and it is the cornerstone of the Module 3 toolkit.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bilinear (W) Transformation — Detailed Mechanics

The **Bilinear Transformation** (also called the **Tustin's transformation** or **W-transform**) is defined as:

$$w = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{z - 1}{z + 1} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad z = \frac{1 + (T/2)w}{1 - (T/2)w}$$

where $T$ is the sampling period. This is a **conformal (one-to-one) mapping** that:

- Maps the **unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$ to the **imaginary axis** of the w-plane.
- Maps the **interior** $\vert z \vert < 1$ to the **LHP** of the w-plane.
- Maps the **exterior** $\vert z \vert > 1$ to the **RHP** of the w-plane.

### Procedure to Apply Routh–Hurwitz to a Discrete System

1. **Step A** — Obtain the closed-loop characteristic equation in z:
$$F(z) = 1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$$
2. **Step B** — Substitute $z = \dfrac{1 + (T/2)w}{1 - (T/2)w}$ into $F(z)$.
3. **Step C** — Cross-multiply to get a polynomial in $w$: $F(w) = 0$.
4. **Step D** — Apply the standard Routh array on $F(w)$.
5. **Step E** — All Routh array first-column entries $> 0$ ⇒ system stable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Examiner's Note:** The sampling period $T$ is *always* specified in the problem. If $T$ is not given, students commonly assume $T = 1$ s or sometimes $T = 2$ s. **Always read the question carefully** — KTU will deduct marks if $T$ is mishandled.

---

## 2.2 Routh–Hurwitz Array Construction (Quick Recap)

Given a polynomial $a_0 w^n + a_1 w^{n-1} + a_2 w^{n-2} + \cdots + a_n = 0$, the **Routh array** is built as follows:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 | ... |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|-----|
| $w^n$ | $a_0$ | $a_2$ | $a_4$ | ... |
| $w^{n-1}$ | $a_1$ | $a_3$ | $a_5$ | ... |
| $w^{n-2}$ | $b_1$ | $b_2$ | $b_3$ | ... |
| $w^{n-3}$ | $c_1$ | $c_2$ | ... | |
| $\vdots$ | ... | | | |
| $w^0$ | ... | | | |

where
$$b_1 = \frac{a_1 a_2 - a_0 a_3}{a_1}, \quad b_2 = \frac{a_1 a_4 - a_0 a_5}{a_1}, \quad \text{etc.}$$

**Stability condition:** *All elements of the first column must be strictly positive* (assuming $a_0 > 0$).

**Number of sign changes** in the first column = **number of roots in the RHP** (in w-plane, this translates to poles *outside* the unit circle in z-plane).

---

## 2.3 Jury's Stability Test — Direct Z-Plane Test

Jury's test determines whether **all roots of a polynomial lie inside the unit circle** without any transformation. Given the characteristic polynomial in z:

$$F(z) = a_0 z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + a_2 z^{n-2} + \cdots + a_{n-1} z + a_n = 0 \quad (a_0 > 0)$$

### Necessary Conditions (First 3 Conditions — F(z) must satisfy):

| Condition # | Expression | Meaning |
|-------------|------------|---------|
| **1** | $F(1) > 0$ | Sum of all coefficients positive |
| **2** | $(-1)^n F(-1) > 0$ | Alternating sum of coefficients positive |
| **3** | $\vert a_n \vert < a_0$ | Magnitude of constant term less than leading coefficient |

### Jury's Tabular Array (Remaining Conditions)

Construct the **Jury array** with $2n - 3$ rows. Denote the rows as $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_{2n-2}$ (alternating with the *reverse* of each row):

$$R_1 = (a_0, \; a_1, \; a_2, \; \ldots, \; a_{n-1}, \; a_n)$$
$$R_2 = (a_n, \; a_{n-1}, \; \ldots, \; a_1, \; a_0)$$

The next row entries are computed as:
$$R_3(k) = \begin{vmatrix} a_0 & a_{n-k} \\ a_n & a_k \end{vmatrix} = a_0 \cdot a_{n-k} - a_n \cdot a_k$$

Subsequent rows are formed by *reversing* the previous row and repeating the determinant operation.

**Jury's Stability Theorem:** All roots lie inside the unit circle **if and only if** the *first elements* of every odd-numbered row ($R_1, R_3, R_5, \ldots$) are **strictly positive**:
$$a_0 > 0, \quad b_0 > 0, \quad c_0 > 0, \quad d_0 > 0, \quad \ldots$$

The number of sign violations = number of roots *outside* the unit circle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Jury's test is the discrete analog of Routh–Hurwitz.** Routh needs $T$ to perform the bilinear mapping; Jury needs *no transformation* and is *exact* for all $T$. For exam speed, Jury is preferred when $T$ is complex or unspecified.

---

## 2.4 Root Loci of Digital Control Systems

The **root locus** is the locus of closed-loop poles in the z-plane as the gain $K$ varies from $0$ to $\infty$, satisfying:

$$1 + G(z)H(z) = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad G(z)H(z) = -1$$

### Magnitude and Angle Conditions

| Condition | Equation | Use |
|-----------|----------|-----|
| **Magnitude** | $\vert G(z)H(z) \vert = 1$ | Find $K$ for a given $z$ on locus |
| **Angle** | $\angle G(z)H(z) = (2k+1)180°, \; k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ | Locate points on the locus |

### Construction Rules for the Z-Plane Root Locus

| Rule | Formula / Description |
|------|------------------------|
| **Number of branches** | Equal to order $n$ of $G(z)H(z)$ |
| **Starting points** ($K=0$) | Open-loop poles (×) of $G(z)H(z)$ |
| **Ending points** ($K \to \infty$) | Open-loop zeros (○) of $G(z)H(z)$ |
| **Symmetry** | About the **real axis** of the z-plane |
| **Real-axis segments** | Count real-axis poles + zeros to the **right** of the test point; if **odd**, that segment is on the locus |
| **Asymptotes** | Centroid: $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sum p_i - \sum z_j}{n - m}$; Angles: $\dfrac{(2k+1)180°}{n - m}$ |
| **Breakaway / Break-in points** | $\dfrac{dK}{dz} = 0$ from $K = -\dfrac{1}{G(z)H(z)}$ |
| **Departure / Arrival angles** | $\phi_d = 180° + \sum \angle z_j - \sum \angle p_i$ (for departure from a pole) |
| **Crossing of unit circle** | Use Jury's test to find the critical $K$ value |
| **jω-axis analog** | The **unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$ is the stability boundary |

### KTU Formula Sheet — Master Table

| Concept | Formula | Condition |
|---------|---------|-----------|
| Bilinear transform | $w = \dfrac{2(z-1)}{T(z+1)}$ | Always |
| Jury C1 | $F(1) > 0$ | Necessary |
| Jury C2 | $(-1)^n F(-1) > 0$ | Necessary |
| Jury C3 | $\vert a_n \vert < a_0$ | Necessary |
| Jury array | $b_k = a_0 a_{n-k} - a_n a_k$ | $b_0 > 0$ |
| Routh $b_1$ | $b_1 = \dfrac{a_1 a_2 - a_0 a_3}{a_1}$ | First col > 0 |
| Magnitude condition | $K = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(z)H(z) \vert}$ | At any $z$ on locus |
| Angle condition | $\angle G(z)H(z) = \pm 180°(2k+1)$ | $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ |
| Asymptote centroid | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sum p_i - \sum z_j}{n - m}$ | $n > m$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Jury's test is widely used in **digital filter design** (DSP chips, audio codecs, biomedical signal processors) to verify filter stability before hardware implementation. Root loci in z-plane are crucial in **servo-system digital redesign** and **motion control** for robotics and CNC machines.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Routh–Hurwitz via Bilinear Transform

**Problem:** The characteristic equation of a sampled-data system is $F(z) = z^2 - 0.6z + 0.05 - 0.04K = 0$, with $T = 0.5$ s. Determine the range of $K$ for stability.

### Step 1 — Write the characteristic equation in z

$$z^2 - 0.6z + (0.05 - 0.04K) = 0$$

### Step 2 — Apply bilinear substitution

Using $z = \dfrac{1 + (T/2)w}{1 - (T/2)w} = \dfrac{1 + 0.25w}{1 - 0.25w}$ (since $T/2 = 0.25$).

**Substitution into the polynomial:**

$$\left(\frac{1 + 0.25w}{1 - 0.25w}\right)^2 - 0.6\left(\frac{1 + 0.25w}{1 - 0.25w}\right) + (0.05 - 0.04K) = 0$$

### Step 3 — Multiply through by $(1 - 0.25w)^2$

$$(1 + 0.25w)^2 - 0.6(1 + 0.25w)(1 - 0.25w) + (0.05 - 0.04K)(1 - 0.25w)^2 = 0$$

### Step 4 — Expand each term

**Term 1:** $(1 + 0.25w)^2 = 1 + 0.5w + 0.0625w^2$

**Term 2:** $0.6(1 + 0.25w)(1 - 0.25w) = 0.6(1 - 0.0625w^2) = 0.6 - 0.0375w^2$

**Term 3:** $(0.05 - 0.04K)(1 - 0.5w + 0.0625w^2)$

Let $\alpha = 0.05 - 0.04K$. Then Term 3 $= \alpha - 0.5\alpha w + 0.0625\alpha w^2$.

### Step 5 — Collect coefficients by powers of w

**Coefficient of $w^2$:** $0.0625 - 0.0375 + 0.0625\alpha = 0.025 + 0.0625\alpha$

**Coefficient of $w^1$:** $0.5 - 0.5\alpha$

**Coefficient of $w^0$:** $1 - 0.6 + \alpha = 0.4 + \alpha$

So the polynomial in w becomes:

$$(0.025 + 0.0625\alpha)w^2 + (0.5 - 0.5\alpha)w + (0.4 + \alpha) = 0$$

### Step 6 — Substitute back $\alpha = 0.05 - 0.04K$

**Coefficient of $w^2$:** $0.025 + 0.0625(0.05 - 0.04K) = 0.025 + 0.003125 - 0.0025K = 0.028125 - 0.0025K$

**Coefficient of $w$:** $0.5 - 0.5(0.05 - 0.04K) = 0.5 - 0.025 + 0.02K = 0.475 + 0.02K$

**Coefficient of $w^0$:** $0.4 + 0.05 - 0.04K = 0.45 - 0.04K$

### Step 7 — Construct the Routh array

$$\begin{array}{c|cc}
w^2 & 0.028125 - 0.0025K & 0.45 - 0.04K \\
w^1 & 0.475 + 0.02K & 0 \\
w^0 & 0.45 - 0.04K & \\
\end{array}$$

### Step 8 — Compute $b_1$ (the $w^0$ row entry)

$$b_1 = \frac{(0.475 + 0.02K)(0.45 - 0.04K) - 0}{0.475 + 0.02K} = 0.45 - 0.04K$$

### Step 9 — Apply stability conditions (all first-column entries > 0)

**Condition A:** $0.028125 - 0.0025K > 0 \Rightarrow K < 11.25$

**Condition B:** $0.475 + 0.02K > 0 \Rightarrow K > -23.75$ (always true for $K \geq 0$)

**Condition C:** $0.45 - 0.04K > 0 \Rightarrow K < 11.25$

### Step 10 — Result

$$\boxed{0 < K < 11.25}$$

The system is stable for $0 \leq K < 11.25$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Jury's Stability Test

**Problem:** Determine the stability of the discrete system whose characteristic equation is

$$F(z) = z^4 + 0.5z^3 - 0.3z^2 + 0.1z + 0.08 = 0$$

### Step 1 — Identify coefficients and order

$$a_0 = 1, \quad a_1 = 0.5, \quad a_2 = -0.3, \quad a_3 = 0.1, \quad a_4 = 0.08, \quad n = 4$$

### Step 2 — Check the three necessary conditions

**Condition 1:** $F(1) > 0$
$$F(1) = 1 + 0.5 - 0.3 + 0.1 + 0.08 = 1.38 > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Condition 2:** $(-1)^4 F(-1) > 0 \Rightarrow F(-1) > 0$
$$F(-1) = 1 - 0.5 - 0.3 - 0.1 + 0.08 = 0.18 > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Condition 3:** $\vert a_4 \vert < a_0$
$$\vert 0.08 \vert = 0.08 < 1 \quad \checkmark$$

All three necessary conditions are satisfied. Proceed to Jury array.

### Step 3 — Build the Jury array

**Row 1** ($R_1$): $(1, \; 0.5, \; -0.3, \; 0.1, \; 0.08)$

**Row 2** ($R_2$): reverse of $R_1$ = $(0.08, \; 0.1, \; -0.3, \; 0.5, \; 1)$

**Row 3** entries — apply $b_k = a_0 a_{n-k} - a_n a_k$ (with $a_0 = 1, a_n = 0.08$, $n = 4$):

$$b_0 = 1 \cdot 0.08 - 0.08 \cdot 1 = 0$$
$$b_1 = 1 \cdot 0.1 - 0.08 \cdot 0.5 = 0.06$$
$$b_2 = 1 \cdot (-0.3) - 0.08 \cdot (-0.3) = -0.276$$
$$b_3 = 1 \cdot 0.5 - 0.08 \cdot 0.1 = 0.492$$

**Row 3** ($R_3$): $(0, \; 0.06, \; -0.276, \; 0.492)$

### Step 4 — Stability criterion on first element of $R_3$

$$b_0 = 0 \quad \text{— NOT strictly positive!}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Observation:** The first element of Row 3 is **exactly zero**, which is a boundary case. A pole lies **on the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$. The system is **marginally stable** (oscillatory), not asymptotically stable. To break the tie, we use the **auxiliary polynomial** from the previous even row.

**Auxiliary polynomial** from $R_2$: $P(z) = 0.08 z^4 + 0.1 z^3 - 0.3 z^2 + 0.5 z + 1$

Its derivative: $P'(z) = 0.32 z^3 + 0.3 z^2 - 0.6 z + 0.5$

Replace $R_3$ by coefficients of $P'(z)$:

$$R_3 \to (0.32, \; 0.3, \; -0.6, \; 0.5)$$

Now $b_0 = 0.32 > 0 \checkmark$, but the test continues with this modified array for rows 4 and beyond.

### Step 5 — Result

The system is **marginally stable** — a closed-loop pole sits *exactly* on the unit circle. KTU would mark this as *"system on the verge of instability"*.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Root Locus Angle & Magnitude Calculation

**Problem:** Given $G(z)H(z) = \dfrac{K(z - 0.5)}{(z - 0.2)(z - 0.8)}$, find the points on the root locus in the z-plane and the corresponding $K$.

### Step 1 — Angle condition

Let a test point be $z = x + jy$. Compute the angle sum:

$$\angle(z - 0.5) - \angle(z - 0.2) - \angle(z - 0.8) = 180°(2k+1)$$

### Step 2 — Real-axis locus

Both poles ($0.2, 0.8$) and the zero ($0.5$) are real. **To the right of the test point**, count poles + zeros.

- For $z > 0.8$: 2 poles + 1 zero = 3 (odd) → **on the locus**
- For $0.5 < z < 0.8$: 1 pole + 0 zeros to the right = 1 (odd) → **on the locus**
- For $0.2 < z < 0.5$: 1 pole to the right = 1 (odd) → **on the locus**
- For $z < 0.2$: 0 poles/zeros to the right = 0 (even) → **NOT on locus**

So the real-axis segments on the locus are: $(-\infty, 0.2)$, $(0.5, 0.8)$, and $(0.8, +\infty)$. The breakaway/break-in points occur between $0.2$–$0.5$ and around $0.8$.

### Step 3 — Find breakaway point

From $K = -\dfrac{1}{G(z)H(z)} = -\dfrac{(z - 0.2)(z - 0.8)}{z - 0.5}$, set $\dfrac{dK}{dz} = 0$:

$$K = -\frac{z^2 - z + 0.16}{z - 0.5}$$

$$\frac{dK}{dz} = -\frac{(2z - 1)(z - 0.5) - (z^2 - z + 0.16)(1)}{(z - 0.5)^2} = 0$$

Numerator: $(2z - 1)(z - 0.5) - (z^2 - z + 0.16)$

$$= 2z^2 - z - z + 0.5 - z^2 + z - 0.16$$

$$= z^2 - z + 0.34$$

Setting $z^2 - z + 0.34 = 0$:
$$z = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 1.36}}{2} = \frac{1 \pm j\sqrt{0.36}}{2} = 0.5 \pm j0.3$$

These are complex conjugates — the **breakaway points** are off the real axis at $z = 0.5 \pm j0.3$.

### Step 4 — Compute $K$ at breakaway

$$K = -\frac{(0.5 + j0.3)^2 - (0.5 + j0.3) + 0.16}{(0.5 + j0.3) - 0.5} = -\frac{(0.25 + j0.3 - 0.09) - 0.5 - j0.3 + 0.16}{j0.3}$$

$$= -\frac{0.16 - j0.3 - 0.5 - j0.3 + 0.16}{j0.3} = -\frac{-0.18 - j0.6}{j0.3}$$

$$= \frac{0.18 + j0.6}{j0.3} = \frac{0.18}{j0.3} + \frac{j0.6}{j0.3} = -j0.6 + 2$$

For a physical (real, positive) $K$, we need $\vert K \vert$ — confirming $K = \sqrt{(-0.6)^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{0.36 + 4} = \sqrt{4.36} \approx 2.088$.

### Step 5 — Magnitude condition for a point on unit circle

Set $z = e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta$ and solve $\vert 1 + G(z)H(z) \vert = 0$. The intersection with $\vert z \vert = 1$ gives the **critical gain** $K_{\max}$ for stability.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Stability Testing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Closed-Loop Char. Eq. F of z = 0] --> B{Choose Method}
    B -->|Method 1: Routh-Hurwitz| C[Apply Bilinear Transform]
    C --> D[Obtain F of w = 0]
    D --> E[Build Routh Array]
    E --> F{All First Col > 0?}
    F -->|Yes| G[STABLE in z-plane]
    F -->|No| H[UNSTABLE]
    F -->|Boundary| I[Marginally Stable]

    B -->|Method 2: Jury's Test| J[Check F of 1 > 0]
    J --> K[Check F of -1 sign]
    K --> L[Check vert a_n vert < a_0]
    L --> M[Build Jury Array]
    M --> N{All First Elements > 0?}
    N -->|Yes| G
    N -->|No| H
    N -->|Boundary| I

    B -->|Method 3: Root Locus| O[Apply Angle Condition]
    O --> P[Plot Poles and Zeros]
    P --> Q[Identify Real-Axis Segments]
    Q --> R[Find Asymptotes and Breakaway]
    R --> S[Find K at Unit Circle Crossing]
    S --> G
```

## 4.2 Jury's Array Construction Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Polynomial F of z of order n] --> Step1[Row 1: Original Coefficients a0, a1, ..., an]
    Step1 --> Step2[Row 2: Reverse an, a n-1, ..., a0]
    Step2 --> Step3[Row 3: Determinant Operation]
    Step3 --> Step4[Row 4: Reverse of Row 3]
    Step4 --> Step5[Row 5: Determinant Operation]
    Step5 --> Step6[Continue until 2n minus 3 rows]
    Step6 --> Check{First Element of Every Odd Row > 0?}
    Check -->|Yes| Stable[All Roots inside Unit Circle - STABLE]
    Check -->|No| Unstable[Some Roots outside Unit Circle - UNSTABLE]
```

## 4.3 Mapping Visualization — s → z → w Plane Equivalence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Splane[s-plane]
        S1[Left Half Plane] -.->|Stable Region| S2[Imaginary Axis s = j omega]
    end
    subgraph Zplane[z-plane]
        Z1[Inside Unit Circle] -.->|Stable Region| Z2[Unit Circle vert z vert = 1]
    end
    subgraph Wplane[w-plane]
        W1[Left Half Plane] -.->|Stable Region| W2[Imaginary Axis w = j omega w]
    end
    S1 <-->|z = e to the sT| Z1
    Z1 <-->|w = 2 z minus 1 over T z plus 1| W1
    S2 <-->|Maps to| Z2
    Z2 <-->|Maps to| W2
```

## 4.4 Root Locus Skeleton in the Z-Plane

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Pole p1 = 0.2 on real axis] -->|K = 0| P1
    P2[Pole p2 = 0.8 on real axis] -->|K = 0| P2
    Z1[Zero z1 = 0.5 on real axis] -->|K approaches infinity| Z1
    P1 -.->|Branch moves| BP1[Breakaway Point at 0.5 minus j 0.3]
    P2 -.->|Branch moves| BP2[Breakaway Point at 0.5 plus j 0.3]
    BP1 -->|Moves toward| Z1
    BP2 -->|Moves toward| Z1
    UC[Unit Circle Boundary] -->|Critical K| CritK[Kmax for stability]
    CritK -->|System on edge| Margin[Marginally Stable]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the Jury's stability criterion for a second-order discrete system $F(z) = a_0 z^2 + a_1 z + a_2 = 0$ with $a_0 > 0$. List all necessary and sufficient conditions for stability.**

**Model Answer:**

For a second-order polynomial $F(z) = a_0 z^2 + a_1 z + a_2$ with $a_0 > 0$, the necessary and sufficient conditions for all roots to lie inside the unit circle are:

**Condition 1:** $F(1) = a_0 + a_1 + a_2 > 0$

**Condition 2:** $(-1)^2 F(-1) = F(-1) = a_0 - a_1 + a_2 > 0$

**Condition 3:** $\vert a_2 \vert < a_0$, i.e., $-a_0 < a_2 < a_0$

For a second-order system, these three conditions alone are **both necessary and sufficient** (no Jury array is required). All three must be satisfied simultaneously for stability.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the necessity of the bilinear transformation when applying Routh–Hurwitz criterion to discrete systems.**

**Model Answer:**

The classical **Routh–Hurwitz criterion** tests whether all polynomial roots lie in the **Left-Half Plane (LHP)** of the s-plane. However, in discrete systems the stability region is **inside the unit circle** of the z-plane — not a half-plane. Direct application of Routh–Hurwitz on $F(z)$ is therefore invalid.

The **bilinear transformation** $w = \dfrac{2}{T} \cdot \dfrac{z-1}{z+1}$ is a **conformal mapping** that:

- Transforms the interior of the unit circle (z-plane) into the **LHP** of the w-plane.
- Transforms the unit circle (stability boundary) into the **imaginary axis** of the w-plane.

After substitution, the characteristic equation becomes $F(w) = 0$, which is now in a form suitable for the standard Routh–Hurwitz analysis. The transformation thus bridges the **continuous-time testing tool** (Routh) and the **discrete-time stability region** (unit circle).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**The characteristic equation of a sampled-data control system is given by:**

$$z^2 + (0.2K - 1.5)z + 0.5 + 0.1K = 0$$

**with sampling period $T = 1$ s.**

**(a)** [7 marks] Find the range of $K$ for stability using the **Jury's stability test**.

**(b)** [7 marks] Verify the result using the **Bilinear transformation and Routh–Hurwitz criterion**.

---

#### Part (a) — Jury's Stability Test [7 marks]

The characteristic polynomial is $F(z) = z^2 + (0.2K - 1.5)z + (0.5 + 0.1K)$ with $a_0 = 1$, $a_1 = 0.2K - 1.5$, $a_2 = 0.5 + 0.1K$, $n = 2$.

**Apply the three Jury conditions:**

**Condition 1:** $F(1) > 0$
$$1 + (0.2K - 1.5) + (0.5 + 0.1K) > 0$$
$$1 - 1.5 + 0.5 + 0.2K + 0.1K > 0$$
$$0 + 0.3K > 0 \Rightarrow K > 0$$

**Condition 2:** $F(-1) > 0$ (since $n=2$, $(-1)^n = 1$)
$$1 - (0.2K - 1.5) + (0.5 + 0.1K) > 0$$
$$1 - 0.2K + 1.5 + 0.5 + 0.1K > 0$$
$$3 - 0.1K > 0 \Rightarrow K < 30$$

**Condition 3:** $\vert a_2 \vert < a_0$
$$\vert 0.5 + 0.1K \vert < 1$$
$$-1 < 0.5 + 0.1K < 1$$
$$-1.5 < 0.1K < 0.5$$
$$-15 < K < 5$$

**Combined range:** $0 < K < 5$.

**Answer for (a):** $\boxed{0 < K < 5}$

---

#### Part (b) — Routh–Hurwitz via Bilinear Transform [7 marks]

**Substitute** $z = \dfrac{1 + 0.5w}{1 - 0.5w}$ (since $T/2 = 0.5$).

$$\left(\frac{1 + 0.5w}{1 - 0.5w}\right)^2 + (0.2K - 1.5)\left(\frac{1 + 0.5w}{1 - 0.5w}\right) + (0.5 + 0.1K) = 0$$

Multiply by $(1 - 0.5w)^2$:

$$(1 + 0.5w)^2 + (0.2K - 1.5)(1 + 0.5w)(1 - 0.5w) + (0.5 + 0.1K)(1 - 0.5w)^2 = 0$$

**Expand each term:**

- $(1 + 0.5w)^2 = 1 + w + 0.25w^2$
- $(1 + 0.5w)(1 - 0.5w) = 1 - 0.25w^2$
- $(1 - 0.5w)^2 = 1 - w + 0.25w^2$

**Coefficient of $w^2$:** $0.25 + (0.2K - 1.5)(-0.25) + (0.5 + 0.1K)(0.25) = 0.25 - 0.05K + 0.375 + 0.125 + 0.025K = 0.75 - 0.025K$

**Coefficient of $w$:** $1 + (0.2K - 1.5)(0) + (0.5 + 0.1K)(-1) = 1 - 0.5 - 0.1K = 0.5 - 0.1K$

**Coefficient of $w^0$:** $1 + (0.2K - 1.5)(1) + (0.5 + 0.1K)(1) = 1 + 0.2K - 1.5 + 0.5 + 0.1K = 0.3K$

**Polynomial in w:**
$$(0.75 - 0.025K)w^2 + (0.5 - 0.1K)w + 0.3K = 0$$

**Routh array:**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|-----|-------|-------|
| $w^2$ | $0.75 - 0.025K$ | $0.3K$ |
| $w^1$ | $0.5 - 0.1K$ | $0$ |
| $w^0$ | $0.3K$ | |

**Stability conditions:**

1. $0.75 - 0.025K > 0 \Rightarrow K < 30$
2. $0.5 - 0.1K > 0 \Rightarrow K < 5$
3. $0.3K > 0 \Rightarrow K > 0$

**Combined:** $\boxed{0 < K < 5}$ ✓ (matches Jury's result)

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A unity-feedback discrete control system has an open-loop pulse transfer function:**

$$G(z) = \frac{0.5K(z + 0.6)}{(z - 1)(z - 0.4)}$$

**(a)** [7 marks] Sketch the root locus of the system in the z-plane, identifying all critical features (real-axis segments, asymptotes, breakaway points).

**(b)** [7 marks] Find the **maximum value of $K$** for which the closed-loop system remains stable, using the **Jury's test** on the characteristic equation.

---

#### Part (a) — Root Locus Sketch [7 marks]

**Poles and zeros:**
- Poles: $p_1 = 1$, $p_2 = 0.4$ (both real, on positive real axis)
- Zero: $z_1 = -0.6$ (real, on negative real axis)
- Number of poles $n = 2$, number of zeros $m = 1$, $n - m = 1$ asymptote.

**Feature 1 — Real-axis segments:**

For a point on the real axis, count poles + zeros to its **right**:

- $z > 1$: 2 poles + 1 zero to right = 3 (odd) → **on locus**
- $0.4 < z < 1$: 1 pole + 1 zero to right = 2 (even) → **NOT on locus**
- $-0.6 < z < 0.4$: 1 pole + 0 zeros to right = 1 (odd) → **on locus**
- $z < -0.6$: 0 poles/zeros to right = 0 (even) → **NOT on locus**

So real-axis segments on the locus: $(-0.6, \; 0.4)$ and $(1, \; +\infty)$.

**Feature 2 — Breakaway point** (in the segment $0.4 < z < 1$):

From $1 + G(z) = 0$:
$$K = -\frac{(z-1)(z-0.4)}{0.5(z+0.6)}$$

Set $\dfrac{dK}{dz} = 0$. Equivalently, from $K = -\dfrac{N(z)}{D(z)}$, $\dfrac{dK}{dz} = 0 \Rightarrow N'(z) D(z) = N(z) D'(z)$.

Here $N(z) = (z-1)(z-0.4) = z^2 - 1.4z + 0.4$, $D(z) = 0.5(z+0.6) = 0.5z + 0.3$.

$N'(z) = 2z - 1.4$, $D'(z) = 0.5$.

$(2z - 1.4)(0.5z + 0.3) = (z^2 - 1.4z + 0.4)(0.5)$

Expand LHS: $z^2 + 0.6z - 0.7z - 0.42 = z^2 - 0.1z - 0.42$

Expand RHS: $0.5z^2 - 0.7z + 0.2$

Equating: $z^2 - 0.1z - 0.42 = 0.5z^2 - 0.7z + 0.2$
$0.5z^2 - 0.6z - 0.62 = 0$
$z^2 - 1.2z - 1.24 = 0$
$z = \dfrac{1.2 \pm \sqrt{1.44 + 4.96}}{2} = \dfrac{1.2 \pm 2.53}{2}$

$z_1 = 1.865$ (break-in, on segment $(1, \infty)$) and $z_2 = -0.665$ (close to zero, on segment $(-0.6, 0.4)$ — actually slightly off, so reject).

So **break-in point** is at $z \approx 1.865$ where the locus re-enters the real axis from the complex plane.

**Feature 3 — Asymptote:** $n - m = 1$, so one asymptote along the negative real axis at angle $180°$.

**Feature 4 — Behavior:** Branches start at poles ($K=0$) and move along the real axis — one toward the zero at $-0.6$, the other looping into the complex plane and crossing the unit circle at some $K = K_{\max}$.

#### Part (b) — Maximum K for Stability [7 marks]

**Closed-loop characteristic equation:**
$$1 + G(z) = 0 \Rightarrow (z-1)(z-0.4) + 0.5K(z+0.6) = 0$$
$$z^2 - 1.4z + 0.4 + 0.5Kz + 0.3K = 0$$
$$z^2 + (0.5K - 1.4)z + (0.4 + 0.3K) = 0$$

$a_0 = 1$, $a_1 = 0.5K - 1.4$, $a_2 = 0.4 + 0.3K$, $n = 2$.

**Jury's conditions:**

**C1:** $F(1) = 1 + 0.5K - 1.4 + 0.4 + 0.3K > 0 \Rightarrow 0.8K > 0 \Rightarrow K > 0$

**C2:** $F(-1) = 1 - 0.5K + 1.4 + 0.4 + 0.3K > 0 \Rightarrow 2.8 - 0.2K > 0 \Rightarrow K < 14$

**C3:** $\vert 0.4 + 0.3K \vert < 1$
$$-1 < 0.4 + 0.3K < 1$$
$$-1.4 < 0.3K < 0.6$$
$$-4.67 < K < 2$$

**Combined stability range:** $0 < K < 2$

$$\boxed{K_{\max} = 2}$$

At $K = 2$, the closed-loop pole lies *on* the unit circle. The system is marginally stable.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in PEAET633 Module 3:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $\frac{2}{T}$ in the bilinear transform.** The full transformation is $w = \frac{2(z-1)}{T(z+1)}$, not $w = \frac{z-1}{z+1}$. Using the latter gives the *wrong* polynomial and incorrect stability boundaries. **Loss: up to 3 marks.**
>
> 2. **Sign error in Jury's Condition 2.** The condition is $(-1)^n F(-1) > 0$. Students often drop the $(-1)^n$ factor. For odd $n$, $F(-1)$ must be **negative**, not positive. **Loss: 2 marks.**
>
> 3. **Mixing Routh array entries for Jury.** Jury's array uses a *determinant* operation with $a_0$ and $a_n$, **not** the Routh recurrence. Copying Routh formulas into Jury is a fatal error. **Loss: full 7 marks for the part.**
>
> 4. **Real-axis locus rule reversed.** On the z-plane real axis, the rule is identical to s-plane: count poles + zeros to the **right**. Some students count to the left. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
>
> 5. **Confusion between $\angle G(z)H(z) = 180°$ and $0°$.** The standard z-plane root locus is the **$180°$ locus** (negative feedback). A $0°$ locus applies only to positive feedback systems. **Loss: 2 marks.**
>
> 6. **Not handling the auxiliary polynomial case.** When a row begins with 0, examiners expect students to use the **auxiliary polynomial derivative** to continue the Jury/Routh test. Skipping this loses 3–4 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Stability in z-plane** $\Leftrightarrow$ all closed-loop poles satisfy $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ (strictly inside the unit circle).
- **Bilinear transformation** $w = \dfrac{2(z-1)}{T(z+1)}$ is required to apply Routh–Hurwitz to discrete systems. Sampling period $T$ must be **explicitly** accounted for.
- **Routh–Hurwitz (in w-plane)**: All first-column entries of the Routh array must be **strictly positive**. Number of sign changes = number of unstable w-plane roots = number of poles outside z-plane unit circle.
- **Jury's Test** works **directly** in z-plane, without any transformation. Three necessary conditions: $F(1) > 0$, $(-1)^n F(-1) > 0$, $\vert a_n \vert < a_0$. Followed by Jury array whose first elements of odd rows must all be positive.
- **Jury is the discrete analog of Routh** — both are tabular frequency-independent algebraic tests.
- **Root locus** in z-plane follows the same angle/magnitude framework as s-plane, but the stability boundary is the **unit circle**, not the imaginary axis.
- **Real-axis root locus rule** in z-plane: a real-axis segment belongs to the locus if the total number of real poles and real zeros to its **right** is **odd**.
- **Asymptote centroid in z-plane** (for $n > m$): $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sum p_i - \sum z_j}{n - m}$; **angles**: $\dfrac{(2k+1)180°}{n - m}$ for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, n-m-1$.
- **Breakaway / break-in points** in z-plane are found from $\dfrac{dK}{dz} = 0$ where $K = -\dfrac{1}{G(z)H(z)}$.
- **Critical $K$** (where locus crosses $\vert z \vert = 1$) is best found using **Jury's test** rather than angle condition — this is the **highest KTU-yield trick** for Module 3.
- **Marginal stability** occurs when a closed-loop pole sits *exactly* on the unit circle — corresponding to a **Routh array row beginning with 0** or a **Jury array first element equal to 0**.
- **Choice of method**: Use **Jury** for direct, $T$-independent analysis; use **Routh–Hurwitz (bilinear)** when the question explicitly requires a continuous-time equivalent; use **root locus** for graphical insight into pole trajectories as $K$ varies.
- **Real-world uses**: Jury's test is foundational in **DSP filter design** and **digital audio codecs**; z-plane root locus is used in **sampled-data servo systems**, **digital PID tuning**, and **robotics motion control**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rules for construction of root locus, frequency domain analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET633 - DISCRETE CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rules for construction of root locus, frequency domain analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Root Locus in Discrete Systems?

In a sampled-data (discrete) control system, the open-loop transfer function is expressed in the **z-domain** rather than the s-domain. The closed-loop characteristic equation that governs stability is:

$$1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$$

The **root locus** is the locus (path) traced by the closed-loop poles of the system in the **z-plane** as the system gain $K$ is varied from $0$ to $\infty$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** For discrete systems, the root locus is plotted in the **z-plane**, NOT the s-plane. The stability boundary is the **unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$, in contrast to the $j\omega$-axis of the s-plane.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Boat and Harbour" Intuition

Imagine a sailboat (closed-loop pole) trying to return to a safe harbour (origin, $z = 0$). The harbour represents stability. As you change the sail size (gain $K$), the boat's path shifts. The root locus is the **map of all possible boat trajectories** as you adjust the sail. Your engineering job is to choose a sail size such that the boat stays safely inside the harbour (inside the unit circle).

## 1.2 Frequency Domain Analysis — Definition

**Frequency domain analysis** of discrete systems studies the steady-state sinusoidal response using the **Bilinear (W-Transform)** technique. The continuous-time frequency methods (Bode plots, Nyquist criterion, gain/phase margins) cannot be directly applied to discrete systems because the unit circle of the z-plane is non-linear in frequency.

The solution is to map the z-plane to a new **w-plane** using the bilinear transformation:

$$z = \frac{1 + w}{1 - w} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad w = \frac{z - 1}{z + 1}$$

After this mapping, the unit circle of z-plane maps to the **entire imaginary axis** of the w-plane ($w = j\omega_w$). The variable $\omega_w$ is called the **pseudo-frequency** (or warped frequency).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Pseudo-frequency?** The relationship $\omega_w = \tan(\omega T/2)$ warps (compresses) the actual frequency. This is why we call it "pseudo" — it preserves the shape of the response but distorts the frequency scale.

### Real-World Application

In **digital motor controllers**, **DSP-based audio systems**, and **aerospace flight control computers**, engineers use w-plane frequency response to design digital filters and ensure that the closed-loop system has adequate **phase margin (PM)** and **gain margin (GM)** before physical hardware is fabricated.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eleven Rules for Constructing the Root Locus in the z-Plane

These rules are **modified adaptations** of the continuous-time s-plane rules, tailored to the discrete z-domain. The magnitudes and angles of $G(z)H(z)$ must satisfy:

$$\vert G(z)H(z) \vert = 1 \quad \text{(Magnitude Criterion)}$$

$$\angle G(z)H(z) = \pm 180°(2k+1), \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots \quad \text{(Angle Criterion)}$$

### Rule-by-Rule Breakdown

**Rule 1 — Number of Branches**
The root locus has $P$ branches, where $P$ is the number of open-loop poles of $G(z)H(z)$. Each branch starts at an open-loop pole ($K=0$) and terminates at either an open-loop zero ($K=\infty$) or at infinity.

**Rule 2 — Symmetry**
The root locus is always **symmetric about the real axis** of the z-plane, because complex roots always appear in conjugate pairs.

**Rule 3 — Starting and Ending Points**
- **Start** ($K = 0$): at the $P$ open-loop poles of $G(z)H(z)$
- **End** ($K \to \infty$): at the $Z$ open-loop zeros, and $P-Z$ branches go to infinity

**Rule 4 — Asymptotes of the Root Locus**
For $P - Z > 0$, there are $P - Z$ asymptotes. Their **angles** are:

$$\phi_A = \frac{(2k+1)180°}{P - Z}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, (P-Z-1)$$

The **centroid** (intersection of asymptotes) is:

$$\sigma_A = \frac{\sum \text{poles} - \sum \text{zeros}}{P - Z}$$

**Rule 5 — Real Axis Segments**
A point on the real axis belongs to the root locus if the **total number of real poles and real zeros to its right is odd**.

**Rule 6 — Breakaway and Break-In Points**
These occur where multiple loci converge or diverge. They are found by solving:

$$\frac{dK}{dz} = 0 \quad \text{where} \quad K = -\frac{1}{G(z)H(z)}$$

A **breakaway** is where the locus leaves the real axis; a **break-in** is where it returns.

**Rule 7 — Angle of Departure from a Complex Pole**
For a complex pole $p_m$:

$$\phi_{d,m} = 180° + \sum \angle (p_m - z_i) - \sum_{n \ne m} \angle (p_m - p_n)$$

**Rule 8 — Angle of Arrival at a Complex Zero**
For a complex zero $z_m$:

$$\phi_{a,m} = 180° - \sum_{n \ne m} \angle (z_m - z_n) + \sum \angle (z_m - p_i)$$

**Rule 9 — Intersection with the Unit Circle**
This is the most critical rule for discrete systems. The points where the locus crosses $\vert z \vert = 1$ give the **critical gain $K_{max}$** for stability. Found using **Routh-Hurwitz** applied to the characteristic polynomial after substituting $z = (s+1)/(s-1)$, or via the **Schur-Cohn** criterion.

**Rule 10 — Intersection with the Positive Real Axis ($z = +1$)**
The locus crosses $z = 1$ (DC point) when the steady-state error condition is met:

$$1 + G(1)H(1) = 0$$

**Rule 11 — Value of K at any Point $z_1$ on the Locus**

$$K = \frac{\prod \vert z_1 - p_i \vert}{\prod \vert z_1 - z_i \vert}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these are different from s-plane rules:** In s-plane, stability boundary is $j\omega$-axis. In z-plane, it's the **unit circle**. The centroid formula is the same mathematically, but the angles are measured from a different origin and the geometry of the plane differs.

## 2.2 Frequency Domain Analysis: W-Transform Procedure

The bilinear transformation converts $G(z)$ to an equivalent $G(w)$:

$$G(w) = G(z)\bigg|_{z = \frac{1+w}{1-w}}$$

Substitute $w = j\omega_w$ to obtain the frequency response function $G(j\omega_w)$. Then standard Bode/Nyquist techniques apply, but with **pseudo-frequency**:

$$\omega_w = \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega T}{2}\right)$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Unit |
|---|---------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Characteristic equation | $1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Magnitude condition | $\vert G(z)H(z) \vert = 1$ | Pure number |
| 3 | Angle condition | $\angle G(z)H(z) = \pm 180°(2k+1)$ | Degrees |
| 4 | Number of asymptotes | $n_A = P - Z$ | Count |
| 5 | Asymptote angle | $\phi_A = (2k+1)180°/(P-Z)$ | Degrees |
| 6 | Centroid | $\sigma_A = (\sum p_i - \sum z_i)/(P-Z)$ | Pure z-plane |
| 7 | Breakaway equation | $dK/dz = 0$ | Algebraic root |
| 8 | Departure angle | $\phi_d = 180° + \sum \phi_z - \sum \phi_p$ | Degrees |
| 9 | K at point $z_1$ | $K = \prod \vert z_1 - p_i \vert / \prod \vert z_1 - z_i \vert$ | Pure number |
| 10 | Bilinear transform | $z = (1+w)/(1-w)$ | Algebraic map |
| 11 | Inverse bilinear | $w = (z-1)/(z+1)$ | Algebraic map |
| 12 | Pseudo-frequency | $\omega_w = \tan(\omega T/2)$ | rad/sec |
| 13 | Actual frequency | $\omega = (2/T)\arctan(\omega_w)$ | rad/sec |
| 14 | Phase margin (w-plane) | $PM = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$ | Degrees |
| 15 | Gain margin (w-plane) | $GM = 1/\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$ | dB |
| 16 | Stability boundary | $\vert z \vert = 1$ (unit circle) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All angles in the z-plane are measured from the **positive real axis of z**, similar to the s-plane, but the locus branches curve around the unit circle differently due to the non-linear warping between the two planes.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Digital filter design**: Root locus guides placement of digital controller poles/zeros to achieve desired transient response.
- **Stability margins**: Frequency-domain analysis yields PM and GM, which directly quantify robustness against modelling errors.
- **Predicting oscillations**: The intersection of the root locus with the unit circle gives the **oscillation frequency** of marginally stable sampled systems — critical for digital audio, ADC/DAC anti-aliasing, and high-speed digital communication.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Angle Criterion from the Characteristic Equation

Starting from the closed-loop characteristic equation:

$$1 + G(z)H(z) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad G(z)H(z) = -1$$

Taking the argument (angle) of both sides:

$$\angle G(z)H(z) = \angle(-1) = \pm 180°(2k+1), \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots$$

This is the **Angle Criterion**. For the magnitude:

$$\vert G(z)H(z) \vert = \vert -1 \vert = 1$$

This is the **Magnitude Criterion**. Together, they define the root locus.

## 3.2 Derivation: Asymptote Angles for Discrete Systems

Consider the characteristic equation as $K \to \infty$:

$$1 + K \frac{\prod_{i=1}^{Z}(z - z_i)}{\prod_{i=1}^{P}(z - p_i)} = 0$$

For very large $z$, this approaches $K \cdot z^{Z-P} \to 0$. The behaviour at infinity is governed by the polynomial $z^{P-Z} + K = 0$ asymptotically. The roots at infinity satisfy:

$$z^{P-Z} = -K = K e^{j(2k+1)\pi}$$

Taking the $(P-Z)$-th root:

$$z = K^{1/(P-Z)} \cdot e^{j(2k+1)\pi/(P-Z)}$$

Therefore the **asymptotic directions** are:

$$\phi_A = \frac{(2k+1)180°}{P - Z}, \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, P-Z-1$$

## 3.3 Derivation: The Breakaway Point Equation

Starting from the magnitude condition:

$$K = -\frac{1}{G(z)H(z)} = -\frac{\prod_{i=1}^{P}(z - p_i)}{\prod_{i=1}^{Z}(z - z_i)}$$

A breakaway or break-in point is a point on the locus where the branches merge or split — geometrically, a point where the tangent to the locus is vertical (in the complex plane) and where the **multiplicity of the root changes**. This corresponds to a stationary point of $K$ with respect to $z$:

$$\frac{dK}{dz} = 0$$

Expanding using the quotient rule:

$$\frac{dK}{dz} = -\frac{\left[\sum_{j=1}^{P}\prod_{i \ne j}(z - p_i)\right]\prod_{i=1}^{Z}(z-z_i) - \prod_{i=1}^{P}(z-p_i)\left[\sum_{j=1}^{Z}\prod_{i \ne j}(z-z_i)\right]}{\left[\prod_{i=1}^{Z}(z-z_i)\right]^2} = 0$$

Equivalently, in a cleaner form:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{P} \frac{1}{z - p_i} = \sum_{i=1}^{Z} \frac{1}{z - z_i}$$

## 3.4 Detailed Worked Example: Root Locus Construction in z-Plane

**Problem:** For the sampled-data system with open-loop pulse transfer function:

$$G(z)H(z) = \frac{K}{(z-0.5)(z-1)}$$

Construct the root locus and find the **range of K for stability**.

**Solution — Step 1: Identify poles and zeros**
- Open-loop poles: $p_1 = 0.5$, $p_2 = 1$  →  $P = 2$
- Open-loop zeros: $Z = 0$
- Number of asymptotes: $P - Z = 2$

**Solution — Step 2: Asymptote angles**

$$\phi_A = \frac{(2k+1)180°}{2} = 90°, 270° \quad (k = 0, 1)$$

**Solution — Step 3: Centroid**

$$\sigma_A = \frac{(0.5 + 1) - 0}{2} = \frac{1.5}{2} = 0.75$$

**Solution — Step 4: Real axis segments**
- To the left of $z = 0.5$: 0 poles to the right (even) → NOT on locus
- Between $0.5$ and $1$: 1 pole to the right (odd) → **ON locus**
- To the right of $1$: 2 poles to the right (even) → NOT on locus

**Solution — Step 5: Breakaway point**
Since both poles are real and the locus lies between them, there must be a breakaway point in the segment $[0.5, 1]$.

$$K = -(z-0.5)(z-1) = -(z^2 - 1.5z + 0.5)$$

$$\frac{dK}{dz} = -(2z - 1.5) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad z = 0.75$$

This matches the centroid — which is expected for two real poles and no zeros.

**Solution — Step 6: Intersection with unit circle**
Substitute $z = e^{j\theta}$ or use $z = (1+s)/(1-s)$ for s-plane Routh analysis.

Characteristic equation: $z^2 - 1.5z + 0.5 + K = 0$

Substitute $z = (1+s)/(1-s)$:

$$\left(\frac{1+s}{1-s}\right)^2 - 1.5\left(\frac{1+s}{1-s}\right) + 0.5 + K = 0$$

Multiply through by $(1-s)^2$:

$$(1+s)^2 - 1.5(1+s)(1-s) + (0.5+K)(1-s)^2 = 0$$

Expand each term:

$$1 + 2s + s^2 - 1.5(1 - s^2) + (0.5+K)(1 - 2s + s^2) = 0$$

$$1 + 2s + s^2 - 1.5 + 1.5s^2 + (0.5+K) - 2(0.5+K)s + (0.5+K)s^2 = 0$$

Group by powers of $s$:

$$\begin{aligned}
s^2: &\quad 1 + 1.5 + (0.5 + K) = 3 + K \\
s^1: &\quad 2 - 2(0.5 + K) = 2 - 1 - 2K = 1 - 2K \\
s^0: &\quad 1 - 1.5 + (0.5 + K) = K
\end{aligned}$$

Routh array for $a s^2 + b s + c = 0$ where $a = 3+K$, $b = 1-2K$, $c = K$:

| $s^2$ | $3 + K$ | $K$ |
|-------|---------|-----|
| $s^1$ | $1 - 2K$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $K$ | |

For stability: all first-column entries $> 0$:

$$3 + K > 0 \Rightarrow K > -3 \quad \text{(always true for } K \ge 0\text{)}$$

$$1 - 2K > 0 \Rightarrow K < 0.5$$

$$K > 0$$

**Range of K for stability:** $\boxed{0 < K < 0.5}$

## 3.5 Derivation: Pseudo-Frequency Relationship

Start with $w = (z-1)/(z+1)$. On the unit circle, $z = e^{j\omega T}$. Substituting:

$$w = \frac{e^{j\omega T} - 1}{e^{j\omega T} + 1}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $e^{-j\omega T/2}$:

$$w = \frac{e^{j\omega T/2} - e^{-j\omega T/2}}{e^{j\omega T/2} + e^{-j\omega T/2}}$$

Using Euler's identity $e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta} = 2j\sin\theta$ and $e^{j\theta} + e^{-j\theta} = 2\cos\theta$:

$$w = \frac{2j\sin(\omega T/2)}{2\cos(\omega T/2)} = j\tan(\omega T/2)$$

Therefore $\omega_w = \tan(\omega T/2)$. Inverting:

$$\omega = \frac{2}{T} \arctan(\omega_w)$$

This proves that $\omega \to \infty$ as $\omega_w \to \pi/2$, and $\omega_w$ is a **non-linear warping** of the true frequency.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Root Locus Construction Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B --> C["Compute P minus Z and Centroid"]
    C --> D["Compute Asymptote Angles using 2k+1 times 180 over P minus Z"]
    D --> E["Mark Real Axis Segments by Odd Count Rule"]
    E --> F["Find Breakaway Points by solving dK by dz equals 0"]
    F --> G["Compute Departure and Arrival Angles for Complex Poles"]
    G --> H["Apply Routh Hurwitz to find Intersection with Unit Circle"]
    H --> I["Plot Complete Root Locus in z Plane"]
    I --> J["Determine Critical K and Stability Range"]
    J --> K["End: Validated Discrete System Design"]
```

```mermaid
    Freq --> Bode["Standard Bode Plot Analysis"]
```
