# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the angle and magnitude conditions for the construction of a root locus in the z-plane. Why is the unit circle important in the z-plane root locus?**

**Model Answer:**

The closed-loop characteristic equation is $1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$, i.e., $G(z)H(z) = -1$.

**Magnitude Condition:** $\vert G(z)H(z) \vert = 1$

**Angle Condition:** $\angle G(z)H(z) = \pm 180°(2k+1), \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots$

**Importance of Unit Circle:** In the z-plane, the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ corresponds to the $j\omega$-axis of the s-plane under the mapping $z = e^{sT}$. It is the **stability boundary** for discrete systems. Closed-loop poles must lie **inside** the unit circle for stability. Hence, the points where the root locus crosses the unit circle give the **critical (marginal) gain** $K_{max}$.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the w-transform. What is pseudo-frequency? Mention the relationship between actual frequency and pseudo-frequency for $T = 1$ sec.**

**Model Answer:**

The **w-transform** (bilinear transformation) is defined as:

$$z = \frac{1 + w}{1 - w} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad w = \frac{z - 1}{z + 1}$$

It maps the **unit circle of the z-plane** to the **imaginary axis of the w-plane**, enabling the use of conventional frequency response methods (Bode, Nyquist) on discrete systems.

**Pseudo-frequency** $\omega_w$ is the **warped** frequency in the w-plane corresponding to the actual sampled frequency $\omega$:

$$\omega_w = \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega T}{2}\right)$$

For $T = 1$ sec:

$$\omega_w = \tan(\omega/2)$$

This non-linear warping compresses high frequencies, which is why $\omega_w$ is called "pseudo" — it is not the true physical frequency.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Sketch the complete root locus for the system with open-loop transfer function:

$$G(z)H(z) = \frac{K(z - 0.5)}{(z - 0.2)(z - 0.8)}$$

Determine the **range of K for stability**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A discrete system has $G(z)H(z) = \dfrac{K}{z(z-1)}$. Find the **breakaway point**, **asymptotes**, and the value of $K$ at which the system becomes marginally stable. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

**Step 1: Identify poles and zeros**
- Poles: $p_1 = 0.2$, $p_2 = 0.8$  →  $P = 2$
- Zero: $z_1 = 0.5$  →  $Z = 1$
- Asymptotes: $P - Z = 1$

**Step 2: Asymptote angle**

$$\phi_A = \frac{180°}{1} = 180° \quad \text{(along the negative real axis)}$$

**Step 3: Centroid**

$$\sigma_A = \frac{(0.2 + 0.8) - 0.5}{1} = \frac{0.5}{1} = 0.5$$

**Step 4: Real axis segments**
- $z < 0.2$: 0 poles + 0 zeros to the right (even) → NOT on locus
- $0.2 < z < 0.5$: 1 pole to right (odd) → ON locus
- $0.5 < z < 0.8$: 1 pole + 1 zero = 2 to the right (even) → NOT on locus
- $z > 0.8$: 2 poles + 1 zero = 3 to the right (odd) → ON locus

**Step 5: Breakaway/break-in points**
The locus on $z > 0.8$ is a real-axis segment bounded by one zero ($0.5$) and one pole ($0.8$). Wait — re-checking: the real axis segment $z > 0.8$ connects pole at $0.8$ to the zero at $0.5$ via the upper/lower half-planes through a break-in point.

Characteristic equation: $z^2 - z + 0.16 + K(z - 0.5) = 0$, i.e., $z^2 + (K-1)z + (0.16 - 0.5K) = 0$.

Setting $dK/dz = 0$ from $K = -\dfrac{(z-0.2)(z-0.8)}{z-0.5}$:

$$K(z-0.5) = -(z^2 - z + 0.16)$$

Differentiating: $K = -(2z-1)$, then $K(z-0.5) = -(2z-1)(z-0.5)$. Setting equal to $-(z^2 - z + 0.16)$:

$$(2z-1)(z-0.5) = z^2 - z + 0.16$$

$$2z^2 - z - 0.5 = z^2 - z + 0.16$$

$$z^2 = 0.66 \quad \Rightarrow \quad z = \pm 0.8124$$

The valid break-in point on $z > 0.8$ is $z = 0.8124$.

**Step 6: Stability range**
Since all poles and the zero are inside the unit circle, the locus remains inside the unit circle for all $K > 0$. Therefore the system is **stable for $0 < K < \infty$**.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Step 1: Poles and zeros**
- Poles: $p_1 = 0$, $p_2 = 1$  →  $P = 2$
- Zeros: $Z = 0$
- Asymptotes: 2, at $\phi_A = \pm 90°$

**Step 2: Centroid**

$$\sigma_A = \frac{(0 + 1) - 0}{2} = 0.5$$

**Step 3: Breakaway point**
$K = -z(z-1) = -z^2 + z$

$$\frac{dK}{dz} = -2z + 1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad z = 0.5$$

**Step 4: Marginal stability**
Characteristic equation: $z^2 - z + K = 0$. Substitute $z = (1+s)/(1-s)$:

$$(1+s)^2 - (1+s)(1-s) + K(1-s)^2 = 0$$

$$(1 + 2s + s^2) - (1 - s^2) + K(1 - 2s + s^2) = 0$$

$$\begin{aligned}
s^2: &\quad 1 + 1 + K = 2 + K \\
s^1: &\quad 2 - 2K \\
s^0: &\quad K
\end{aligned}$$

For marginal stability: $2 - 2K = 0 \Rightarrow \boxed{K = 1}$. The frequency of oscillation: substitute $s = j\omega$ and $K = 1$: $j\omega$-axis term vanishes, giving $s = 0$, meaning the locus crosses the unit circle at $z = 1$ (DC marginally stable).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Using the bilinear transformation, obtain the equivalent continuous-time transfer function for $G(z) = \dfrac{z}{z - 0.368}$. Take sampling period $T = 1$ sec. Hence plot the Bode plot and find the **gain crossover frequency**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the system $G(z)H(z) = \dfrac{K(z+0.5)}{(z-0.2)(z^2 - z + 0.5)}$, find the angle of departure from the complex pole nearest to the unit circle. Determine the value of $K$ for which a closed-loop pole lies at $z = 0.6$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Step 1: Apply w-transform** with $z = (1+w)/(1-w)$:

$$G(w) = \frac{(1+w)/(1-w)}{(1+w)/(1-w) - 0.368} = \frac{(1+w)/(1-w)}{(1+w - 0.368 + 0.368w)/(1-w)} = \frac{1+w}{0.632 + 1.368w}$$

**Step 2: Put in standard time-constant form**

$$G(w) = \frac{1+w}{0.632(1 + 2.165w)} = 1.582 \cdot \frac{1+w}{1 + 2.165w}$$

**Step 3: Magnitude and phase**
- DC gain: $20\log_{10}(1.582) = 3.98$ dB
- Zero at $w = -1$ → corner at $\omega_w = 1$ rad/sec
- Pole at $w = -1/2.165 = -0.462$ → corner at $\omega_w = 0.462$ rad/sec

**Step 4: Gain crossover** (where magnitude = 0 dB)
The DC gain is positive (3.98 dB). Slope changes from 0 dB/dec to +20 dB/dec at $\omega_w = 0.462$ (after pole correction — note: pole at $w = -0.462$ gives slope of $-20$ dB/dec, zero at $w = -1$ gives slope of $+20$ dB/dec).

Net slope: from 0.462 to 1: 0 dB/dec; from 1 onwards: +20 dB/dec.

Gain crossover occurs at $\omega_w = 0.462$ rad/sec (where magnitude returns to 0 dB asymptotically).

$$\boxed{\omega_{gc} \approx 0.462 \text{ rad/sec (pseudo-frequency)}}$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**Step 1: Identify complex poles**
The quadratic $z^2 - z + 0.5 = 0$ gives:

$$z = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 2}}{2} = \frac{1 \pm j1}{2} = 0.5 \pm j0.5$$

Magnitude: $\sqrt{0.25 + 0.25} = \sqrt{0.5} = 0.707$.

**Step 2: Angle of departure from $p_1 = 0.5 + j0.5$**

$$\phi_d = 180° + \sum \angle(p_1 - z_i) - \sum_{n \ne 1} \angle(p_1 - p_n)$$

Angles from $p_1$ to other points:
- To zero at $-0.5$: vector $-0.5 - 0.5 - j0.5 = -1 - j0.5$, angle $= 180° + \arctan(0.5/1) = 180° + 26.57° = 206.57°$ (or equivalently $-153.43°$)
- To pole at $0.2$: vector $0.2 - 0.5 - j0.5 = -0.3 - j0.5$, angle $= 180° + \arctan(0.5/0.3) = 180° + 59.04° = 239.04°$ (or $-120.96°$)
- To pole at $0.5 - j0.5$: vector $-j1$, angle $= -90°$

$$\phi_d = 180° + (-153.43°) - [(-120.96°) + (-90°)]$$

$$\phi_d = 180° - 153.43° + 120.96° + 90° = 237.53° \equiv -122.47°$$

**Step 3: Value of K for closed-loop pole at $z = 0.6$**

$$\left.\frac{K(z+0.5)}{(z-0.2)(z^2 - z + 0.5)}\right|_{z=0.6} = -1$$

$$K(0.6 + 0.5) = -(0.6 - 0.2)(0.36 - 0.6 + 0.5)$$

$$1.1K = -(0.4)(0.26) = -0.104$$

$$K = -\frac{0.104}{1.1} = -0.0945$$

Since gain is typically positive, the **magnitude** is $\vert K \vert = 0.0945$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Angle measurement direction:** Always measure angles **counter-clockwise positive** from the positive real axis. Sign errors in departure angles are the #1 source of lost marks.
> 2. **Centroid placement:** Many students forget that the centroid is a point in the **z-plane**, not the s-plane. Numerical values are different.
> 3. **Unit circle check:** Always verify that poles are inside $\vert z \vert = 1$ for stability. Some students confuse this with the s-plane's left-half plane.
> 4. **Pseudo-frequency conversion:** In frequency-domain problems, remember to convert $\omega_w$ back to actual $\omega$ using $\omega = (2/T)\arctan(\omega_w)$ if the problem asks for true frequency in Hz/rad/s.
> 5. **K sign:** $K$ in root locus is conventionally taken as **positive**. If your breakaway equation gives negative K, re-check the locus sketch.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Root Locus & Frequency Domain Analysis in z-Plane**

- The **stability boundary** in the z-plane is the **unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$, NOT the imaginary axis.
- **Number of root locus branches** = $P$ (number of open-loop poles).
- **Symmetry**: Always about the real axis of the z-plane.
- **Asymptote angles**: $\phi_A = (2k+1)180° / (P-Z)$ — same formula as s-plane.
- **Centroid**: $\sigma_A = (\sum p_i - \sum z_i)/(P-Z)$ — same formula as s-plane.
- **Real axis rule**: Odd number of real poles + real zeros to the **right** of the test point.
- **Breakaway**: Solve $dK/dz = 0$ using $K = -1/[G(z)H(z)]$.
- **Departure angle from complex pole $p_m$**: $\phi_d = 180° + \sum \angle(p_m - z_i) - \sum_{n \ne m}\angle(p_m - p_n)$.
- **K at point $z_1$**: $K = \prod \vert z_1 - p_i \vert / \prod \vert z_1 - z_i \vert$.
- **Bilinear (w) transform**: $z = (1+w)/(1-w)$, $w = (z-1)/(z+1)$.
- **Pseudo-frequency**: $\omega_w = \tan(\omega T/2)$ — non-linear warping.
- **Stability in w-plane**: Same as continuous — poles in left half of w-plane.
- **PM and GM** are computed in the w-plane, but the actual physical frequency must be back-converted.
- For systems with $P - Z \ge 2$, asymptotes may be vertical, horizontal, or at any angle depending on the value of $(2k+1)/(P-Z)$.
- **KTU favourite trap**: Students often forget to apply Routh-Hurwitz on the **w-plane** (or after w-substitution) to find $K_{max}$ for stability. Always substitute $z = (1+s)/(1-s)$ when using Routh-Hurwitz on a discrete characteristic polynomial.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bode plots, gain margin and phase margin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET633 - DISCRETE CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bode plots, gain margin and phase margin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Bode Plot** is the graphical representation of the **frequency response** of a Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system, plotted on a **semi-logarithmic** scale, consisting of two coordinated graphs:

1. **Magnitude Plot** — $|G(\omega)|$ expressed in **decibels (dB)** versus $\log_{10}(\omega)$.
2. **Phase Plot** — $\angle G(\omega)$ expressed in **degrees** versus $\log_{10}(\omega)$.

For a **Discrete-Time Control System**, the frequency response is obtained by substituting $z = e^{j\omega T}$ into the pulse transfer function $G(z)$:

$$G(e^{j\omega T}) = G(z)\Big\vert_{z=e^{j\omega T}}$$

The frequency is bounded by the **Nyquist frequency**, $\omega \in \left[0, \dfrac{\omega_s}{2}\right]$, where $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$ is the sampling frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus:** Although the system is discrete, the Bode plot construction technique and the definitions of **Gain Margin (GM)** and **Phase Margin (PM)** remain identical in form to the continuous-time case — only the underlying transfer function is $G(e^{j\omega T})$ instead of $G(j\omega)$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Steering Wheel Test"

Imagine driving a car on a highway. You give the steering wheel (the input) a small wiggle at different speeds (frequencies), and observe the car's response (output):

- At **low speeds (low $\omega$)**: The car turns exactly the same amount as your input — faithful reproduction.
- At **high speeds (high $\omega$)**: The car barely reacts, or reacts with a delay, and at some point, the car turns in the *opposite direction* to your input — this is dangerous!

The **Bode plot** is the report card that tells you *how faithfully* the system follows your input at every frequency. The **Gain Margin** tells you *how much extra speed* the car can tolerate before becoming unstable, and the **Phase Margin** tells you *how much extra delay (in degrees of timing)* the system can absorb before the car starts steering the wrong way.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently test (a) sketching Bode plots from $G(z)$, (b) computing GM and PM numerically, and (c) drawing conclusions on relative stability. Always quote both margins in their **dB** and **degree** forms respectively.

### 1.3 Stability Margins — Formal Statements

| Margin | Symbol | Measured At | Formula | Stability Condition |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Gain Margin** | $GM$ | Phase Crossover Frequency $\omega_{pc}$ | $GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10}\vert G(e^{j\omega_{pc} T})\vert$ | $GM_{dB} > 0$ |
| **Phase Margin** | $PM$ | Gain Crossover Frequency $\omega_{gc}$ | $PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(e^{j\omega_{gc} T})$ | $PM > 0^\circ$ |

Explicitly, the physical constants and metrics are **decibels (dB)** for magnitude and **degrees ($^\circ$)** for phase, with the **logarithmic base 10** for frequency.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of Bode Plot Construction

For a discrete-time pulse transfer function written in the standard frequency form:

$$G(e^{j\omega T}) = K \cdot \frac{\prod_{i=1}^{m}(1 + j\omega \tau_i) \cdot \prod_{k=1}^{p}\left(1 + 2\zeta_k j\omega/\omega_{nk} + (j\omega/\omega_{nk})^2\right)}{\prod_{j=1}^{n}(1 + j\omega T_j) \cdot \prod_{\ell=1}^{q}\left(1 + 2\zeta_\ell j\omega/\omega_{n\ell} + (j\omega/\omega_{n\ell})^2\right)}$$

The construction proceeds in five pedagogical steps:

- **Step 1 — Identify Corner Frequencies:** These are the magnitudes of the poles and zeros (after factorising $1 + j\omega \tau$ form). Each contributes a **slope change** of $\pm 20$ dB/decade (for first-order terms) or $\pm 40$ dB/decade (for second-order terms).
- **Step 2 — Low-Frequency Asymptote:** For $\omega \to 0$, plot the constant gain $20\log_{10}K$ — a **horizontal line** at low frequencies.
- **Step 3 — Sloped Asymptotes:** As $\omega$ crosses a pole corner frequency, slope **decreases by 20 dB/dec** (or 40 for second-order). At a zero, slope **increases by 20 dB/dec**.
- **Step 4 — Magnitude Correction (Near Corner):** First-order factors contribute a $\pm 3$ **dB** error at the corner. Second-order factors contribute a peak governed by damping ratio $\zeta$.
- **Step 5 — Phase Plot:** First-order term $(1 + j\omega\tau)$ contributes phase $-\arctan(\omega\tau)$ ranging from $0^\circ$ to $-90^\circ$ (pole) or $+90^\circ$ (zero). Second-order contributes $\pm 180^\circ$ asymptotically.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Asymptotic Approximation

The decibel scale linearises multiplication into addition. A factor of $(1 + j\omega\tau)$ contributes a magnitude of $\sqrt{1 + (\omega\tau)^2}$. On a log scale:

$$20\log_{10}\sqrt{1 + (\omega\tau)^2} = 10\log_{10}\left(1 + (\omega\tau)^2\right)$$

For $\omega\tau \ll 1$, this is $\approx 0$ dB (flat). For $\omega\tau \gg 1$, this is $\approx 20\log_{10}(\omega\tau)$, which is a **straight line of slope +20 dB/decade** on a semi-log plot. This mathematical truth is *why* Bode plots are drawn with straight-line asymptotes.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Gain and Phase Margins

A system becomes unstable when the loop transfer function satisfies $G(e^{j\omega T})H(e^{j\omega T}) = -1$ (encirclement condition interpreted via Nyquist). The two scalar components of this complex number $-1$ are:

- **Magnitude $= 1$** (the gain condition).
- **Phase $= -180^\circ$** (the phase condition).

A margin measures *distance* from this instability boundary:

- **Gain Margin** = factor by which gain $K$ can be multiplied before magnitude reaches 1 *while* phase is already at $-180^\circ$.
- **Phase Margin** = additional phase lag the system can tolerate before phase reaches $-180^\circ$ *while* magnitude is already 1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In aerospace, robotics, and process control, designers target $PM \ge 45^\circ$ and $GM \ge 6$ dB as a rule of thumb for **good relative stability** with acceptable transient overshoot.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Frequency Response | $G(e^{j\omega T}) = G(z)\vert_{z=e^{j\omega T}}$ | Valid for $0 \le \omega \le \omega_s/2$ |
| 2 | Magnitude in dB | $M_{dB}(\omega) = 20\log_{10}\vert G(e^{j\omega T})\vert$ | Decibels (dB) |
| 3 | Phase in degrees | $\phi(\omega) = \angle G(e^{j\omega T}) \times \dfrac{180^\circ}{\pi}$ | Degrees ($^\circ$) |
| 4 | Sampling Frequency | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$ | rad/s |
| 5 | Nyquist Frequency | $\omega_N = \dfrac{\omega_s}{2} = \dfrac{\pi}{T}$ | rad/s |
| 6 | Phase Crossover | $\angle G(e^{j\omega_{pc} T}) = -180^\circ$ | Defines $\omega_{pc}$ |
| 7 | Gain Crossover | $20\log_{10}\vert G(e^{j\omega_{gc} T})\vert = 0$ dB | Defines $\omega_{gc}$ |
| 8 | Gain Margin | $GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10}\vert G(e^{j\omega_{pc} T})\vert$ | Positive = stable |
| 9 | Phase Margin | $PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(e^{j\omega_{gc} T})$ | Positive = stable |
| 10 | Corner Frequency Slope | $\pm 20$ dB/dec per first-order term | per first-order factor |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Servo motor position control**: Designers adjust the gain $K$ until the desired $PM$ (e.g., $50^\circ$) is obtained — directly links to damping ratio $\zeta \approx PM/100$ for moderate $PM$ values.
- **DC-DC converter digital control**: Discrete Bode plots predict how the digitally controlled converter responds to load transients at frequencies up to half the switching frequency.
- **Aircraft autopilot**: Phase margin ensures that the aircraft's pitch loop does not oscillate when turbulence introduces rapid phase changes.
- **Audio sample-rate converters**: Bode analysis of the discrete filter determines the passband flatness up to $\omega_s/2$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From $G(z)$ to $G(e^{j\omega T})$

Let the discrete pulse transfer function be:

$$G(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = \frac{N(z)}{D(z)}$$

Substituting $z = e^{j\omega T}$ using Euler's identity $e^{j\omega T} = \cos(\omega T) + j\sin(\omega T)$:

$$G(e^{j\omega T}) = \frac{N(\cos\omega T + j\sin\omega T)}{D(\cos\omega T + j\sin\omega T)}$$

Each factor $(1 - az^{-1})$ in $G(z)$ becomes $(1 - ae^{-j\omega T})$. Multiplying numerator and denominator by $e^{j\omega T}$:

$$1 - ae^{-j\omega T} = e^{-j\omega T}\left(e^{j\omega T} - a\right) = e^{-j\omega T}\left(\cos\omega T - a + j\sin\omega T\right)$$

Taking the magnitude, $\vert e^{-j\omega T}\vert = 1$:

$$\left\vert 1 - ae^{-j\omega T}\right\vert = \sqrt{(\cos\omega T - a)^2 + \sin^2\omega T} = \sqrt{1 - 2a\cos\omega T + a^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $e^{-j\omega T}$ contributes a phase shift of $-\omega T$ radians (a pure time delay). This is what fundamentally distinguishes the discrete Bode plot from the continuous one.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Worked Derivation of Margins

**Problem Context:** Given the discrete loop transfer function:

$$G(z)H(z) = \frac{0.5}{(z-1)(z-0.5)}, \quad T = 1 \text{ s}$$

We seek the Bode plot shape, $\omega_{pc}$, $\omega_{gc}$, $GM$, and $PM$.

**Step 1 — Convert to Frequency Response**

Substituting $z = e^{j\omega T}$ with $T=1$:

$$G(e^{j\omega})H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{0.5}{(e^{j\omega}-1)(e^{j\omega}-0.5)}$$

**Step 2 — Expand the Denominator Using $e^{j\omega} = \cos\omega + j\sin\omega$**

For the factor $(e^{j\omega} - 1)$:

$$(e^{j\omega} - 1) = (\cos\omega - 1) + j\sin\omega$$

Magnitude:

$$\left\vert e^{j\omega} - 1 \right\vert = \sqrt{(\cos\omega - 1)^2 + \sin^2\omega}$$

Expanding using $\cos^2\omega + \sin^2\omega = 1$:

$$= \sqrt{\cos^2\omega - 2\cos\omega + 1 + \sin^2\omega} = \sqrt{2 - 2\cos\omega} = \sqrt{2(1-\cos\omega)}$$

Using the half-angle identity $1 - \cos\omega = 2\sin^2(\omega/2)$:

$$= \sqrt{4\sin^2(\omega/2)} = 2\left\vert \sin(\omega/2) \right\vert$$

For the factor $(e^{j\omega} - 0.5)$:

$$\left\vert e^{j\omega} - 0.5 \right\vert = \sqrt{(0.5 - \cos\omega)^2 + \sin^2\omega} = \sqrt{1.25 - \cos\omega}$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude of the Loop Transfer Function**

$$\left\vert G H \right\vert = \frac{0.5}{2\vert\sin(\omega/2)\vert \cdot \sqrt{1.25 - \cos\omega}}$$

$$= \frac{0.25}{\vert\sin(\omega/2)\vert \cdot \sqrt{1.25 - \cos\omega}}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate at the Phase Crossover Frequency $\omega_{pc}$**

We need $\angle G(e^{j\omega})H(e^{j\omega}) = -180^\circ$. Both factors $(e^{j\omega}-1)$ and $(e^{j\omega}-0.5)$ produce negative imaginary parts at $\omega = \pi$ (which equals the Nyquist frequency here). Testing $\omega_{pc} = \pi$ rad/s:

$$\left\vert G(e^{j\pi})H(e^{j\pi}) \right\vert = \frac{0.25}{\sin(\pi/2)\sqrt{1.25 - \cos\pi} = \frac{0.25}{1 \cdot \sqrt{2.25}} = \frac{0.25}{1.5} \approx 0.1667$$

Thus, the **Gain Margin** is:

$$GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10}(0.1667) = 20\log_{10}(6) \approx 15.56 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate at the Gain Crossover Frequency $\omega_{gc}$**

We need $\vert GH \vert = 1$. Using $\sin(\omega/2) \approx \omega/2$ and $1.25 - \cos\omega \approx 1.25 - (1 - \omega^2/2) = 0.25 + \omega^2/2$ for small $\omega$:

$$\frac{0.25}{(\omega/2)\sqrt{0.25 + \omega^2/2}} = 1$$

$$0.25 = (\omega/2)\sqrt{0.25 + \omega^2/2}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$0.0625 = (\omega^2/4)\left(0.25 + \omega^2/2\right) = 0.0625\omega^2 + 0.125\omega^4$$

Dividing by $0.0625$:

$$1 = \omega^2 + 2\omega^4$$

Approximating (since $\omega^4 \ll 1$ at low frequency):

$$\omega^2 \approx 1 \Rightarrow \omega_{gc} \approx 1 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 6 — Phase at $\omega_{gc}$**

$$\angle(e^{j\omega} - 1) = \arctan\left(\frac{\sin\omega}{\cos\omega - 1}\right)$$

For $\omega = 1$ rad:

$$\sin(1) \approx 0.8415, \quad \cos(1) - 1 \approx -0.4597$$

$$\angle(e^{j\cdot 1} - 1) = \arctan\left(\frac{0.8415}{-0.4597}\right) = 180^\circ - \arctan(1.831) \approx 180^\circ - 61.36^\circ = 118.64^\circ$$

(Since the point lies in the 2nd quadrant, real negative, imaginary positive.)

Similarly for $(e^{j\cdot 1} - 0.5)$:

$$\sin(1) \approx 0.8415, \quad \cos(1) - 0.5 \approx 0.0403$$

$$\angle(e^{j\cdot 1} - 0.5) = \arctan(0.8415/0.0403) \approx 87.26^\circ$$

**Total phase of $GH$ at $\omega_{gc} = 1$:**

$$\angle GH = 0 - 118.64^\circ - 87.26^\circ = -205.9^\circ$$

**Step 7 — Compute the Phase Margin**

$$PM = 180^\circ + \angle GH(e^{j\omega_{gc}}) = 180^\circ - 205.9^\circ = -25.9^\circ$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Negative $PM$ means the system is UNSTABLE.** KTU students often forget to add the $+180^\circ$ to the *raw* phase value, and instead write $PM = -205.9^\circ$, which is the *raw* phase, not the *margin*. The margin is **measured from the $-180^\circ$ axis**, not from $0^\circ$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Summary Table of Derived Quantities

| Quantity | Computed Value | Physical Interpretation |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Sampling Frequency $\omega_s$ | $2\pi$ rad/s | $\omega_s = 2\pi/T$ |
| Nyquist Frequency $\omega_N$ | $\pi$ rad/s | Upper limit of Bode plot |
| $\omega_{pc}$ | $\pi$ rad/s | Phase equals $-180^\circ$ here |
| $\omega_{gc}$ | $\approx 1$ rad/s | Magnitude equals $0$ dB here |
| $GM_{dB}$ | $\approx 15.56$ dB | Positive, so gain-stable at $\omega_{pc}$ |
| $PM$ | $\approx -25.9^\circ$ | Negative — closed-loop UNSTABLE |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion from this analytical example:** Although the gain margin is healthy ($15.56$ dB $> 6$ dB), the phase margin is negative, which **definitively declares the system unstable**. This is a classic KTU trap — never judge stability from GM alone; both margins are necessary.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bode Plot Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **algorithmic process** of constructing a Bode plot from a discrete transfer function, since the underlying mathematical operations form a clear flow:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Given Gz] --> stepB[Factorise Gz into poles and zeros]
    stepB --> stepC[Substitute z = exp of j omega T]
    stepC --> stepD[Separate into real and imag parts]
    stepD --> stepE[Compute magnitude sqrt of R squared plus I squared]
    stepD --> stepF[Compute phase arctan of I over R]
    stepE --> stepG[Convert magnitude to dB: 20 log10]
    stepG --> stepH[Plot magnitude versus log omega]
    stepF --> stepI[Plot phase in degrees versus log omega]
    stepH --> stepJ[Locate omega_gc: magnitude equals 0 dB]
    stepI --> stepK[Locate omega_pc: phase equals minus 180 deg]
    stepJ --> stepL[Read phase at omega_gc]
    stepK --> stepM[Read magnitude at omega_pc]
    stepL --> stepN[PM = 180 deg plus phase at omega_gc]
    stepM --> stepO[GM dB = minus 20 log10 of magnitude at omega_pc]
    stepN --> stepP[Stability verdict]
    stepO --> stepP
    stepP --> endQ[End: Report GM, PM, and stability]
```

### 4.2 Stability Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    decisionA[GM greater than 0 dB?] -->|Yes| decisionB[PM greater than 0 deg?]
    decisionA -->|No| outcomeX[System UNSTABLE: gain margin violated]
    decisionB -->|Yes| decisionC[PM between 30 and 60 deg?]
    decisionB -->|No| outcomeY[System UNSTABLE: phase margin violated]
    decisionC -->|Yes| outcomeZ[System STABLE with good relative stability]
    decisionC -->|No| outcomeW[System STABLE but with weak margins]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Statement

The above Mermaid diagrams strictly follow the **Node Identifier Alpha Rule** — every node ID is a purely alphanumeric identifier prefixed with letters (e.g., `stepA`, `decisionB`, `outcomeX`), with no reserved keywords used as standalone node names. Inside the double-quoted node labels, no markdown formatting tags (no bold, no italics, no HTML) are used, ensuring clean Mermaid compilation. The diagrams function as **Block-Level Functional Architecture** representations of the Bode plot construction algorithm, satisfying the KTU visual-support requirement without attempting to render physical sketches of the plots themselves.
