## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define **Gain Margin (GM)** and **Phase Margin (PM)** for a discrete control system. State the conditions on $GM$ and $PM$ for the closed-loop system to be stable.

**Model Answer:**

> The **Gain Margin (GM)** is defined as the reciprocal of the magnitude of the open-loop frequency response, measured at the **phase crossover frequency** $\omega_{pc}$ where the phase angle equals $-180^\circ$. Expressed in decibels, $GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10}\vert G(e^{j\omega_{pc} T})\vert$.
>
> The **Phase Margin (PM)** is defined as $180^\circ$ plus the phase angle of the open-loop frequency response, measured at the **gain crossover frequency** $\omega_{gc}$ where the magnitude is unity ($0$ dB): $PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(e^{j\omega_{gc} T})$.
>
> **Stability Conditions:** $GM_{dB} > 0$ **and** $PM > 0^\circ$. Both conditions must be satisfied simultaneously for the closed-loop discrete system to be stable.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Why is the magnitude plot of a Bode diagram expressed in **decibels** rather than in linear scale? What is the significance of the **$-20$ dB/decade** slope?

**Model Answer:**

> Expressing magnitude in decibels ($M_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\vert G \vert$) **linearises multiplication into addition**. A transfer function $G(j\omega) = K \cdot \dfrac{(1+j\omega\tau_1)}{(1+j\omega\tau_2)}$ becomes a sum of Bode plots of individual factors, which is mathematically and graphically far easier to construct.
>
> Additionally, the decibel scale **compresses a wide dynamic range** of magnitudes (e.g., from $0.001$ to $1000$) into a manageable visual range ($-60$ dB to $+60$ dB), which is essential when examining low-frequency gain and high-frequency attenuation in the same plot.
>
> The **$-20$ dB/decade** slope arises from a simple pole factor $\dfrac{1}{1 + j\omega\tau}$: for $\omega \tau \gg 1$, $\vert G \vert \approx \dfrac{1}{\omega\tau}$, and $20\log_{10}(1/\omega\tau) = -20\log_{10}\omega - 20\log_{10}\tau$, which is a straight line of slope $-20$ dB per decade of $\omega$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** For the discrete-time system with pulse transfer function:

$$G(z)H(z) = \frac{0.368(1 + 0.718z^{-1})}{(1 - 0.368z^{-1})(1 - 0.1z^{-1})}, \quad T = 0.5 \text{ s}$$

Sketch the Bode plot. Compute the **Gain Margin** and **Phase Margin**. Comment on the stability of the closed-loop system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A discrete unity-feedback system has the open-loop pulse transfer function:

$$G(z) = \frac{0.2(z + 0.8)}{(z - 1)(z - 0.6)}, \quad T = 0.2 \text{ s}$$

Determine the phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$, the gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$, the gain margin, and the phase margin. Is the system stable? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A(a):**

**Step 1 — Rewrite in the $(1 + j\omega\tau)$ form** (substitute $z = e^{j\omega T}$):

Using $1 - ae^{-j\omega T}$, the magnitude-squared is $1 - 2a\cos\omega T + a^2$, and the phase is $\arctan\left(\dfrac{-a\sin\omega T}{1 - a\cos\omega T}\right)$.

**Step 2 — Low-frequency magnitude** ($\omega \to 0$):

$$\left\vert GH \right\vert_{\omega \to 0} = \frac{0.368(1 + 0.718)}{(1 - 0.368)(1 - 0.1)} = \frac{0.368 \times 1.718}{0.632 \times 0.9} = \frac{0.6323}{0.5688} = 1.1117$$

$$M_{dB}(0) = 20\log_{10}(1.1117) = 0.92 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Low-frequency phase** ($\omega \to 0$):

All factors have $\sin\omega T \to 0$, so phase $\to 0^\circ$.

**Step 4 — Locate $\omega_{gc}$:** Solve $20\log_{10}\vert GH\vert = 0$, i.e., $\vert GH \vert = 1$. Using iterative trial at $\omega = 2$ rad/s, $\omega T = 1$ rad:

$$\left\vert e^{-j\cdot 1} - 0.368 \right\vert = \sqrt{1 - 0.736\cos(1) + 0.368^2} = \sqrt{1 - 0.736(0.5403) + 0.1354}$$
$$= \sqrt{1 - 0.3976 + 0.1354} = \sqrt{0.7378} = 0.859$$

$$\left\vert e^{-j\cdot 1} - 0.1 \right\vert = \sqrt{1 - 0.2\cos(1) + 0.01} = \sqrt{1 - 0.1081 + 0.01} = \sqrt{0.9019} = 0.9497$$

$$\left\vert 1 + 0.718 e^{-j\cdot 1} \right\vert = \sqrt{1 + 1.436\cos(1) + 0.718^2} = \sqrt{1 + 0.7759 + 0.5155} = \sqrt{2.2914} = 1.5137$$

$$\left\vert GH \right\vert = \frac{0.368 \times 1.5137}{0.859 \times 0.9497} = \frac{0.5570}{0.8159} = 0.6827$$

Magnitude is $<1$, so $\omega_{gc} > 2$ rad/s. Try $\omega = 4$ rad/s, $\omega T = 2$ rad:

$$\left\vert e^{-j 2} - 0.368 \right\vert = \sqrt{1 - 0.736\cos(2) + 0.1354}$$
$\cos(2) = -0.4161$, so $= \sqrt{1 + 0.3063 + 0.1354} = \sqrt{1.4417} = 1.2007$

$$\left\vert e^{-j 2} - 0.1 \right\vert = \sqrt{1 - 0.2(-0.4161) + 0.01} = \sqrt{1 + 0.0832 + 0.01} = \sqrt{1.0932} = 1.0455$$

$$\left\vert 1 + 0.718 e^{-j 2} \right\vert = \sqrt{1 + 1.436(-0.4161) + 0.5155} = \sqrt{1 - 0.5975 + 0.5155} = \sqrt{0.918} = 0.958$$

$$\left\vert GH \right\vert = \frac{0.368 \times 0.958}{1.2007 \times 1.0455} = \frac{0.3525}{1.2553} = 0.2808$$

Now $<1$ again? Wait — checking: at $\omega = 2$, magnitude was $0.68$, at $\omega = 4$, magnitude is $0.28$. Both $< 1$. The magnitude is **monotonically decreasing**, so $\omega_{gc}$ is below $2$ rad/s. Try $\omega = 1$ rad/s, $\omega T = 0.5$:

$$\left\vert e^{-j 0.5} - 0.368 \right\vert = \sqrt{1 - 0.736\cos(0.5) + 0.1354} = \sqrt{1 - 0.736(0.8776) + 0.1354}$$
$$= \sqrt{1 - 0.6459 + 0.1354} = \sqrt{0.4895} = 0.6997$$

$$\left\vert e^{-j 0.5} - 0.1 \right\vert = \sqrt{1 - 0.2(0.8776) + 0.01} = \sqrt{1 - 0.1755 + 0.01} = \sqrt{0.8345} = 0.9135$$

$$\left\vert 1 + 0.718 e^{-j 0.5} \right\vert = \sqrt{1 + 1.436(0.8776) + 0.5155} = \sqrt{1 + 1.2602 + 0.5155} = \sqrt{2.7757} = 1.6660$$

$$\left\vert GH \right\vert = \frac{0.368 \times 1.666}{0.6997 \times 0.9135} = \frac{0.6131}{0.6392} = 0.9591$$

Very close to $1$. Try $\omega = 0.9$, $\omega T = 0.45$:

Numerically iterating, $\omega_{gc} \approx 0.95$ rad/s.

**Step 5 — Phase at $\omega_{gc}$:** At $\omega = 0.95$, $\omega T = 0.475$:

- $\angle(1 - 0.368 e^{-j 0.475})$: numerator imaginary $= -0.368\sin(0.475) = -0.368(0.4576) = -0.1684$; real part $= 1 - 0.368\cos(0.475) = 1 - 0.368(0.8891) = 1 - 0.3272 = 0.6728$. So angle $= \arctan(-0.1684/0.6728) = -14.05^\circ$.
- $\angle(1 - 0.1 e^{-j 0.475})$: imaginary $= -0.1(0.4576) = -0.04576$; real $= 1 - 0.1(0.8891) = 0.9111$. Angle $= \arctan(-0.04576/0.9111) = -2.876^\circ$.
- $\angle(1 + 0.718 e^{-j 0.475})$: imaginary $= +0.718\sin(0.475)\cdot(\text{wait, this factor is }1 + 0.718 e^{-j\omega T}, \text{ so imag} = -0.718\sin(0.475) = -0.3287)$; real $= 1 + 0.718\cos(0.475) = 1 + 0.718(0.8891) = 1.6384$. Angle $= \arctan(-0.3287/1.6384) = -11.36^\circ$.

Total phase $= 0 + (-11.36^\circ) - (-14.05^\circ) - (-2.876^\circ) = -11.36 + 14.05 + 2.876 = 5.57^\circ$

Wait, careful: $\angle GH = \angle(0.368) + \angle(1 + 0.718e^{-j\omega T}) - \angle(1 - 0.368e^{-j\omega T}) - \angle(1 - 0.1e^{-j\omega T})$
$= 0 + (-11.36) - (-14.05) - (-2.876) = -11.36 + 14.05 + 2.876 = 5.57^\circ$

**Phase Margin** = $180^\circ + 5.57^\circ = 185.57^\circ$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $PM > 180^\circ$ is unphysical in the conventional sense and indicates that the phase is well above $-180^\circ$ at crossover — the system is **very stable**. The gain margin is also computed but is effectively infinite (the phase never reaches $-180^\circ$ in the valid frequency range). The system is **stable** with **excellent relative stability**.

---

**Model Solution for Question A(b):**

**Step 1 — Substitute $z = e^{j\omega T}$ with $T = 0.2$:**

$$G(e^{j\omega T}) = \frac{0.2(e^{j\omega T} + 0.8)}{(e^{j\omega T} - 1)(e^{j\omega T} - 0.6)}$$

**Step 2 — Find $\omega_{pc}$ (where phase $= -180^\circ$):**

The phase is:

$$\phi = \angle(e^{j\omega T} + 0.8) - \angle(e^{j\omega T} - 1) - \angle(e^{j\omega T} - 0.6)$$

For $(e^{j\omega T} - 1)$: real part $\cos\omega T - 1 \le 0$, imag part $\sin\omega T \ge 0$ for $\omega T \in [0, \pi]$. So this factor is in the **2nd quadrant** — phase $= 180^\circ - \arctan\left(\dfrac{\sin\omega T}{\cos\omega T - 1}\right)$ — which is always between $90^\circ$ and $180^\circ$.

For $(e^{j\omega T} - 0.6)$: real $= \cos\omega T - 0.6$ (can be positive or negative), imag $= \sin\omega T \ge 0$.

For $(e^{j\omega T} + 0.8)$: real $= \cos\omega T + 0.8 > 0$ always, imag $= \sin\omega T \ge 0$ — first quadrant.

**At $\omega T = \pi$ (i.e., $\omega = \pi/0.2 = 5\pi$ rad/s, the Nyquist limit):**
- $(e^{j\pi} + 0.8) = -1 + 0.8 + j0 = -0.2$ (pure negative real) $\Rightarrow$ phase $= 180^\circ$
- $(e^{j\pi} - 1) = -2$ (pure negative real) $\Rightarrow$ phase $= 180^\circ$
- $(e^{j\pi} - 0.6) = -1.6$ (pure negative real) $\Rightarrow$ phase $= 180^\circ$

Total phase $= 180^\circ - 180^\circ - 180^\circ = -180^\circ$. ✓

So $\omega_{pc} = 5\pi \approx 15.71$ rad/s.

**Step 3 — Magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$:**

$$\vert e^{j\pi} + 0.8\vert = 0.2, \quad \vert e^{j\pi} - 1\vert = 2, \quad \vert e^{j\pi} - 0.6\vert = 1.6$$

$$\vert G \vert = \frac{0.2 \times 0.2}{2 \times 1.6} = \frac{0.04}{3.2} = 0.0125$$

**Gain Margin** $= -20\log_{10}(0.0125) = 20\log_{10}(80) = 38.06$ dB.

**Step 4 — Find $\omega_{gc}$ (where $\vert G \vert = 1$):**

As $\omega \to 0$:

$$\vert G \vert \to \frac{0.2 \times 1.8}{1 \times 0.6} = \frac{0.36}{0.6} = 0.6 \quad (\text{less than } 1)$$

So magnitude never reaches $1$ at low $\omega$. As $\omega \to \pi/T$ (Nyquist), magnitude is $0.0125 < 1$. The magnitude is **always less than 1**, which means $\omega_{gc}$ does not exist in the conventional sense.

**Step 5 — Phase Margin Assessment:**

Since $\vert G \vert$ never crosses $0$ dB, the conventional $PM$ is undefined by the standard definition. However, in KTU practice, if the loop gain is always less than unity, the loop can never satisfy $G(e^{j\omega T}) = -1$, so the system is **inherently stable** (loop gain insufficient to encircle $-1$).

**Verdict:** The system is **stable** because the loop gain is always less than $1$, with $GM \approx 38.06$ dB.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice — [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the procedure for constructing the Bode plot of a discrete-time system from its pulse transfer function $G(z)$. Discuss how the corner frequencies and slopes are determined. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unity feedback discrete system has the open-loop transfer function:

$$G(z) = \frac{0.5K(z - 0.2)}{(z - 0.8)(z - 0.4)}$$

with $T = 0.1$ s. Find the range of $K$ for which the closed-loop system is stable using the **Bode plot / stability margin** approach. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Question B(a):**

The Bode plot construction procedure for a discrete system is performed in the following structured steps:

1. **Obtain the frequency response** by substituting $z = e^{j\omega T}$ into $G(z)$:

$$G(e^{j\omega T}) = G(z)\vert_{z=e^{j\omega T}}$$

2. **Factorise $G(e^{j\omega T})$** into first-order terms in the form $(1 - ae^{-j\omega T})$ or equivalently $(1 + j\omega\tau)$-like terms using the magnitude-phase conversion:

$$\vert 1 - ae^{-j\omega T}\vert = \sqrt{1 - 2a\cos(\omega T) + a^2}$$
$$\angle(1 - ae^{-j\omega T}) = -\arctan\left(\frac{a\sin\omega T}{1 - a\cos\omega T}\right)$$

3. **Identify corner frequencies:** These are the values of $\omega$ where the magnitude behaviour changes qualitatively. For a first-order factor $(1 - ae^{-j\omega T})$, the corner occurs at $\omega = 0$ (trivial) and the asymptotic behaviour transitions at $\omega T \approx 1$ (i.e., when $\omega T$ is small enough that $\cos\omega T \approx 1$ and $\sin\omega T \approx \omega T$). Each pole contributes a **$-20$ dB/decade** slope at high $\omega T$, and each zero contributes **$+20$ dB/decade**.

4. **Plot the magnitude asymptote:** Start with the low-frequency magnitude $20\log_{10}\vert G(0)\vert$ as a horizontal line. At each corner frequency, change the slope according to the factor type (pole: $-20$ dB/dec, zero: $+20$ dB/dec).

5. **Plot the phase:** Each pole contributes a phase that rolls from $0^\circ$ to $-180^\circ$ as $\omega T$ goes from $0$ to $\pi$. Each zero contributes the mirror image ($0^\circ$ to $+180^\circ$).

6. **Apply $\pm 3$ dB corrections** near the corner frequencies for first-order terms, and resonance peaks for second-order terms governed by damping ratio $\zeta$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical summary:** The construction is essentially identical to the continuous-time case, but with the critical restriction that the frequency axis is bounded by $\omega_s/2$. Beyond this, the magnitude/phase curves are mirror-imaged (due to aliasing), and only the range $\omega \in [0, \omega_s/2]$ is plotted.

---

**Model Answer for Question B(b):**

**Step 1 — Find the frequency response.** Substituting $z = e^{j\omega T}$, $T = 0.1$:

$$G(e^{j\omega T}) = \frac{0.5K(e^{j\omega T} - 0.2)}{(e^{j\omega T} - 0.8)(e^{j\omega T} - 0.4)}$$

**Step 2 — Find $\omega_{pc}$ where $\angle G = -180^\circ$.**

For $\omega T = \pi$ (Nyquist limit, $\omega = 10\pi \approx 31.42$ rad/s):

- $(e^{j\pi} - 0.2) = -1.2$, angle $= 180^\circ$
- $(e^{j\pi} - 0.8) = -1.8$, angle $= 180^\circ$
- $(e^{j\pi} - 0.4) = -1.4$, angle $= 180^\circ$

Total phase $= 0 + 180^\circ - 180^\circ - 180^\circ = -180^\circ$ ✓

So $\omega_{pc} = 10\pi$ rad/s.

**Step 3 — Magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$:**

$$\vert G \vert_{\omega_{pc}} = \frac{0.5K \times 1.2}{1.8 \times 1.4} = \frac{0.6K}{2.52} = 0.2381K$$

**Step 4 — Gain Margin Condition:**

$$GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10}(0.2381K) > 0$$
$$20\log_{10}(0.2381K) < 0$$
$$0.2381K < 1 \Rightarrow K < 4.2$$

**Step 5 — Check the gain crossover for an upper bound on $K$.**

As $\omega \to 0$:

$$\vert G \vert_0 = \frac{0.5K(1 - 0.2)}{(1 - 0.8)(1 - 0.4)} = \frac{0.5K \times 0.8}{0.2 \times 0.6} = \frac{0.4K}{0.12} = 3.333K$$

If $3.333K < 1$, i.e., $K < 0.3$, the magnitude is always $< 0$ dB and the gain crossover does not exist — the system is unconditionally stable.

For $0.3 < K < 4.2$, the magnitude crosses $0$ dB at some $\omega_{gc} < \omega_{pc}$, and we need to verify $PM > 0$.

For $K > 4.2$, the system is unstable by gain margin criterion.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students quote only one bound. The complete stability range is **$0 < K < 4.2$**, verified by the gain margin condition. Do not forget that $K$ must be positive (gain constant of a real system).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Bode Plot Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $+180^\circ$ in PM:** Students often quote the raw phase at $\omega_{gc}$ as the margin. The margin is $180^\circ + \text{raw phase}$. ($-3$ marks)
> 2. **Wrong units for GM:** $GM$ must be expressed in dB in the answer; if computed as a ratio, the formula is $GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(GM_{ratio})$. ($-2$ marks)
> 3. **Plotting beyond Nyquist:** The Bode plot is valid only for $0 \le \omega \le \omega_s/2$. Drawing curves beyond this range invites penalty. ($-2$ marks)
> 4. **Forgetting to convert $z = e^{j\omega T}$:** Some students plot $G(j\omega)$ instead of $G(e^{j\omega T})$ — this is fundamentally wrong for a discrete system. ($-4$ marks)
> 5. **Confusing corner frequencies:** In discrete form, the corner frequencies are at $\omega T = 1$, not at $\omega = 1$. ($-2$ marks)

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bode plot** is **two graphs** — magnitude (dB) and phase (degrees) versus $\log_{10}\omega$.
- **Discrete frequency response** uses $z = e^{j\omega T}$, with the **valid range** $0 \le \omega \le \omega_s/2$.
- **Gain crossover** $\omega_{gc}$ is where the **magnitude = 0 dB**.
- **Phase crossover** $\omega_{pc}$ is where the **phase = $-180^\circ$**.
- **Gain Margin** is the **dB distance from 0 dB** to the magnitude curve at $\omega_{pc}$, *inverted*: $GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10}\vert G \vert$ at $\omega_{pc}$.
- **Phase Margin** is the **degree distance from $-180^\circ$** to the phase curve at $\omega_{gc}$: $PM = 180^\circ + \angle G$ at $\omega_{gc}$.
- **Stability requires both** $GM_{dB} > 0$ **and** $PM > 0^\circ$.
- **Bode plot asymptotes** use straight-line segments with slope $\pm 20$ dB/dec per first-order pole/zero, with **$\pm 3$ dB correction** at the corner.
- **$PM \approx 100\zeta$** for moderate values — a quick cross-check between time-domain and frequency-domain specifications.
- **Sampling period $T$** critically affects the Bode shape — a small $T$ makes the discrete plot converge to the continuous $G(j\omega)$ plot.
- **Target margins in KTU design problems:** $PM \ge 45^\circ$ and $GM \ge 6$ dB for "good" relative stability.
- The **$-20$ dB/dec** slope comes from the $\log_{10}$ derivative of $\log(1/x)$ type expressions.
- **Factorise before substituting** — convert to $(1 - ae^{-j\omega T})$ form for clean magnitude and phase computations.
- **Euler's identity** $e^{j\omega T} = \cos\omega T + j\sin\omega T$ is the bridge from the $z$-plane to the frequency domain.
- **Nyquist frequency** $\omega_N = \pi/T$ is the **upper limit** of the Bode plot — never draw beyond it.
- **Phase is unwrapped** in the Bode plot — students often miss the wrap from $-180^\circ$ to $+180^\circ$ in the plot construction.
- The **GM and PM formulas are identical in form** for continuous and discrete systems — only the underlying $G$ function differs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State space analysis, state space representation of discrete time systems, state space model, various canonical forms from transfer function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET633 - DISCRETE CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** State space analysis, state space representation of discrete time systems, state space model, various canonical forms from transfer function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is State Space Analysis?

**State Space Analysis** is a modern, time-domain method of representing, analyzing, and designing control systems. Unlike the classical transfer function approach that deals only with the input-output relationship, the state space method describes the **internal dynamics** of a system completely.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> The **state** of a dynamic system is the smallest set of variables (called *state variables*) such that the knowledge of these variables at time $t = t_0$, together with the input for $t \ge t_0$, completely determines the behaviour of the system for all $t \ge t_0$.

### 1.2 State Space Model of a Discrete Time System

For a linear, time-invariant, **discrete-time** system, the state space model is given by two matrix equations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{x}(k+1) &= \mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{x}(k) + \mathbf{B}\,u(k) \\
\mathbf{y}(k) &= \mathbf{C}\,\mathbf{x}(k) + \mathbf{D}\,u(k)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{x}(k) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$ is the **state vector** ($n$ = order of the system).
- $u(k)$ is the scalar (or vector) input.
- $\mathbf{y}(k)$ is the scalar (or vector) output.
- $\mathbf{A}$ is the $n \times n$ **system (state) matrix**.
- $\mathbf{B}$ is the $n \times 1$ **input matrix**.
- $\mathbf{C}$ is the $1 \times n$ **output matrix**.
- $\mathbf{D}$ is the $1 \times 1$ **direct transmission (feedforward) matrix**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Explanation (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine you are driving a car on a highway. The *position* of the car alone is not enough to predict its future motion — you also need its *velocity*, *acceleration*, and *steering angle*. These variables together form the **state** of the car. Knowing the state at the current instant (sample) and the driver's input (throttle/brake) tells you everything about the future motion. This is exactly the philosophy of state space analysis: capture all internal "memory" of the system in a vector, then propagate it forward in time using a difference equation.

### 1.4 Why State Space for Discrete Systems?

- It handles **MIMO** (multi-input, multi-output) systems naturally.
- It allows time-varying and non-linear systems.
- It is the foundation for **modern control theory** (pole placement, observers, LQR, Kalman filtering).
- Digital controllers (microcontrollers, DSPs) operate on samples, hence discrete state space is the natural representation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The General Discrete Transfer Function

Consider a single-input single-output (SISO) discrete system with the pulse transfer function:

$$
G(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = \frac{b_0 z^n + b_1 z^{n-1} + b_2 z^{n-2} + \cdots + b_{n-1} z + b_n}{z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + a_2 z^{n-2} + \cdots + a_{n-1} z + a_n}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** The denominator is **monic** (leading coefficient = 1). This is the canonical form used for converting to state space. The numerator and denominator are polynomials in $z^{-1}$ in some textbooks, but for state space we use positive powers of $z$.

### 2.2 The Core Steps to Convert Transfer Function → State Space

The conversion is **not unique**. There are multiple "canonical forms," each giving different $\mathbf{A, B, C, D}$ matrices but mathematically describing the **same system**.

The four most important canonical forms for KTU are:

1. **Controllable Canonical Form (CCF)**
2. **Observable Canonical Form (OCF)**
3. **Diagonal Canonical Form (DCF)**
4. **Jordan Canonical Form (JCF)** — used for repeated roots.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Canonical Form | $\mathbf{A}$ Structure | $\mathbf{B}$ Vector | $\mathbf{C}$ Vector | $\mathbf{D}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Controllable (CCF)** | Companion matrix with negative coefficients $\begin{bmatrix} -a_1 & -a_2 & \cdots & -a_n \\ 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \ddots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & \cdots & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\begin{bmatrix} b_1 - a_1 b_0 & b_2 - a_2 b_0 & \cdots & b_n - a_n b_0 \end{bmatrix}$ | $b_0$ |
| **Observable (OCF)** | Transpose of CCF's $\mathbf{A}$ | $\begin{bmatrix} b_1 - a_1 b_0 \\ b_2 - a_2 b_0 \\ \vdots \\ b_n - a_n b_0 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ | $b_0$ |
| **Diagonal (DCF)** | $\text{diag}(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n)$ | Determined by residues | Determined by zeros | $b_0$ |
| **Jordan (JCF)** | Jordan blocks for repeated roots | Modified by partial fractions | Modified by zeros | $b_0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Utility:** Engineers use CCF for **pole placement** design and OCF for **observer design**. DCF reveals the natural modes (eigenvalues) directly, making stability analysis trivial. JCF is the generalization when DCF fails (repeated poles).

### 2.4 Stability Insight from the State Matrix

The system is **asymptotically stable** if and only if all eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ lie strictly **inside the unit circle** in the $z$-plane:
$$|\lambda_i(\mathbf{A})| < 1 \quad \forall \, i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

This is the discrete-time counterpart of the continuous-time condition $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Satellite attitude control uses discrete state space for onboard digital autopilots.
- **Robotics:** Mobile robots use state space for path tracking with sampled sensor data.
- **Power Electronics:** DC-DC converter digital control (buck/boost) is implemented via discrete state space.
- **Digital Filters:** IIR/FIR filter design uses the same canonical form mathematics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Controllable Canonical Form (CCF)

Consider a general $n$-th order SISO discrete system:
$$
G(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = \frac{b_0 z^n + b_1 z^{n-1} + \cdots + b_n}{z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n}
$$

**Step 1:** Cross-multiply the transfer function:
$$
(z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n)\,Y(z) = (b_0 z^n + b_1 z^{n-1} + \cdots + b_n)\,U(z)
$$

**Step 2:** Divide both sides by $z^n$ to obtain a relation suitable for difference equations:
$$
Y(z) + a_1 z^{-1} Y(z) + \cdots + a_n z^{-n} Y(z) = b_0 U(z) + b_1 z^{-1} U(z) + \cdots + b_n z^{-n} U(z)
$$

**Step 3:** Define an intermediate variable $W(z)$ such that:
$$
W(z) = \frac{U(z)}{z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n}
$$
and
$$
Y(z) = (b_0 z^n + b_1 z^{n-1} + \cdots + b_n)\,W(z)
$$

**Step 4:** From the first equation, in the time domain (taking inverse $z$-transform with the shift property $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{z^{-1} X(z)\} = x(k-1)$):
$$
w(k) = -a_1 w(k-1) - a_2 w(k-2) - \cdots - a_n w(k-n) + u(k)
$$

**Step 5:** Define the state variables in **controllable form**:
$$
x_1(k) = w(k-n), \quad x_2(k) = w(k-n+1), \quad \ldots, \quad x_n(k) = w(k-1)
$$

**Step 6:** Write the state equations. The latest state update gives the first row of $\mathbf{A}$:
$$
x_1(k+1) = w(k-n+1) = x_2(k)
$$
$$
x_2(k+1) = w(k-n+2) = x_3(k)
$$
$$
\vdots
$$
$$
x_{n-1}(k+1) = w(k-1) = x_n(k)
$$
$$
x_n(k+1) = w(k) = -a_n x_1(k) - a_{n-1} x_2(k) - \cdots - a_1 x_n(k) + u(k)
$$

**Step 7:** Express the output $Y(z) = (b_0 z^n + b_1 z^{n-1} + \cdots + b_n)W(z)$. Dividing by $z^n$:
$$
Y(z) = b_0 U(z) + b_1 z^{-1} U(z) + \cdots + b_n z^{-n} U(z)
$$

The output in the time domain:
$$
y(k) = b_0 u(k) + b_1 u(k-1) + \cdots + b_n u(k-n)
$$

But we need to express $y(k)$ in terms of states. Since $W(z)(z^n + \cdots) = U(z)$:
$$
Y(z) = (b_0 z^n + b_1 z^{n-1} + \cdots + b_n)W(z)
$$
$$
= b_0 \bigl[U(z) - a_1 z^{-1} U(z) - \cdots \bigr] + b_1 z^{-1} U(z) + \cdots
$$

After careful algebra, the output becomes:
$$
y(k) = (b_1 - a_1 b_0)\,x_1(k) + (b_2 - a_2 b_0)\,x_2(k) + \cdots + (b_n - a_n b_0)\,x_n(k) + b_0\,u(k)
$$

**Step 8:** The final matrices in Controllable Canonical Form:

$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} -a_1 & -a_2 & -a_3 & \cdots & -a_{n-1} & -a_n \\ 1 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 0 \\ \vdots & \ddots & \ddots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$
$$
\mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} b_1 - a_1 b_0 & b_2 - a_2 b_0 & \cdots & b_n - a_n b_0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{D} = b_0
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Deriving CCF for a 2nd-Order System

**Given:**
$$
G(z) = \frac{2z^2 + 3z + 1}{z^2 + 5z + 6}
$$

**Identify coefficients:** $b_0 = 2, b_1 = 3, b_2 = 1$ and $a_1 = 5, a_2 = 6$.

**Step 1 — Build $\mathbf{A}$ (2×2 companion matrix):**
$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} -5 & -6 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Build $\mathbf{B}$:**
$$
\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Build $\mathbf{C}$:**
$$
\mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} b_1 - a_1 b_0 & b_2 - a_2 b_0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 - 5(2) & 1 - 6(2) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -7 & -11 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Build $\mathbf{D}$:**
$$
\mathbf{D} = b_0 = 2
$$

**Final State Space Model:**
$$
\mathbf{x}(k+1) = \begin{bmatrix} -5 & -6 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{x}(k) + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} u(k)
$$
$$
y(k) = \begin{bmatrix} -7 & -11 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{x}(k) + 2\,u(k)
$$

**Verification by computing $C(zI-A)^{-1}B + D$:** The characteristic polynomial is $\det(zI - A) = z^2 + 5z + 6 = (z+2)(z+3)$, matching the denominator. ✓

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Diagonal Canonical Form (DCF)

This form is obtained by **partial fraction expansion** of $G(z)$ when the poles are distinct.

**Step 1:** Perform partial fraction expansion:
$$
\frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = b_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{r_i}{z - \lambda_i}
$$

where $\lambda_i$ are the (distinct) poles of $G(z)$ and $r_i$ are the residues.

**Step 2:** Define state variables for each first-order block:
$$
X_i(z) = \frac{U(z)}{z - \lambda_i} \quad \implies \quad z X_i(z) = \lambda_i X_i(z) + U(z)
$$

**Step 3:** Take inverse $z$-transform:
$$
x_i(k+1) = \lambda_i x_i(k) + u(k)
$$

**Step 4:** Output:
$$
Y(z) = b_0 U(z) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i X_i(z)
$$
$$
y(k) = b_0\,u(k) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i\,x_i(k)
$$

**Final State Space Model (Diagonal Form):**
$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} \lambda_1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda_2 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & \lambda_n \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ \vdots \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$
$$
\mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} r_1 & r_2 & \cdots & r_n \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{D} = b_0
$$

**Note:** $b_0$ here is the constant term in the partial fraction expansion (the proper part as $z \to \infty$). If the degree of numerator equals that of denominator, $b_0$ is non-zero.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Diagonal Canonical Form

**Given:**
$$
G(z) = \frac{3z^2 - z - 2}{(z-1)(z-2)} = \frac{3z^2 - z - 2}{z^2 - 3z + 2}
$$

**Step 1:** First perform polynomial division (since numerator and denominator have the same degree):
$$
\frac{3z^2 - z - 2}{z^2 - 3z + 2} = 3 + \frac{(3z^2 - z - 2) - 3(z^2 - 3z + 2)}{z^2 - 3z + 2}
$$
$$
= 3 + \frac{8z - 8}{z^2 - 3z + 2} = 3 + \frac{8z - 8}{(z-1)(z-2)}
$$

So $b_0 = 3$.

**Step 2:** Partial fraction of the remainder:
$$
\frac{8z - 8}{(z-1)(z-2)} = \frac{r_1}{z-1} + \frac{r_2}{z-2}
$$
$$
r_1 = \left.\frac{8z - 8}{z-2}\right\vert_{z=1} = \frac{8 - 8}{-1} = 0
$$
$$
r_2 = \left.\frac{8z - 8}{z-1}\right\vert_{z=2} = \frac{16 - 8}{1} = 8
$$

**Step 3:** Therefore:
$$
G(z) = 3 + \frac{0}{z-1} + \frac{8}{z-2}
$$

**Step 4:** Build the matrices. Note: the $r_1 = 0$ mode is decoupled, so we can keep it for completeness:
$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 8 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{D} = 3
$$

---

### 3.5 Observable Canonical Form (OCF) — Quick Derivation

The observable canonical form is the **dual** of the controllable canonical form:

$$
\mathbf{A}_{OCF} = \mathbf{A}_{CCF}^T, \quad \mathbf{B}_{OCF} = \mathbf{C}_{CCF}^T, \quad \mathbf{C}_{OCF} = \mathbf{B}_{CCF}^T, \quad \mathbf{D}_{OCF} = \mathbf{D}_{CCF}
$$

So for the 2nd-order example in Section 3.2:
$$
\mathbf{A}_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} -5 & 1 \\ -6 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B}_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} -7 \\ -11 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{C}_{OCF} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{D} = 2
$$

---

### 3.6 Jordan Canonical Form (JCF) — Repeated Poles

When $G(z)$ has a **repeated root** $\lambda$ of multiplicity $m$, the DCF fails. We use a **Jordan block** of size $m$:

$$
\mathbf{J}(\lambda, m) = \begin{bmatrix} \lambda & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \ddots & \ddots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \lambda & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \lambda \end{bmatrix}_{m \times m}
$$

The corresponding $\mathbf{B}$ block for this mode is:
$$
\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}_{m \times 1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For JCF with a double pole at $\lambda$ and a simple pole at $\mu$:**
> $\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} \lambda & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \mu \end{bmatrix}$, with appropriate $\mathbf{B}$ and $\mathbf{C}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Conversion Workflow from Transfer Function to State Space

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start G z] --> B[Identify order n]
    B --> C{Choose Canonical Form}
    C -->|CCF| D1[Read coefficients a_i and b_i]
    C -->|OCF| D2[Use transpose of CCF]
    C -->|DCF| D3[Partial Fractions]
    C -->|JCF| D4[Jordan Blocks]
    D1 --> E1[Build A as companion matrix]
    D1 --> E2[Build B as standard basis vector]
    D1 --> E3[Build C using formula]
    D1 --> E4[Set D = b0]
    E1 --> F1[State Space Model A B C D]
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    E4 --> F1
    D2 --> F2[Dual of CCF]
    F2 --> F1
    D3 --> E5[Compute residues r_i]
    E5 --> E6[Diagonal A with poles]
    E6 --> F1
    D4 --> E7[Identify multiplicity]
    E7 --> E8[Jordan block construction]
    E8 --> F1
    F1 --> G[Verify char poly matches denom]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Signal Flow of the Discrete State Space Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    u[Input u k] --> B[Matrix B]
    B --> Sum((Sum))
    x[State x k] --> A[Matrix A]
    A --> Sum
    x --> C[Matrix C]
    C --> SumOut((Sum))
    u --> D[Matrix D]
    D --> SumOut
    SumOut --> y[Output y k]
    Sum --> Delay[Unit Delay z to power minus 1]
    Delay --> x
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Structural Comparison of Canonical Forms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Transfer Function G z] --> Form{Select Form}
    Form -->|CCF| CCF[Companion Matrix A]
    Form -->|OCF| OCF[Transpose of Companion]
    Form -->|DCF| DCF[Diagonal A with distinct eigenvalues]
    Form -->|JCF| JCF[Jordan Blocks for repeated poles]
    CCF --> Check1[Used in Pole Placement]
    OCF --> Check2[Used in Observer Design]
    DCF --> Check3[Mode Decoupling]
    JCF --> Check4[Generalized Modes]
```

### 4.4 State Space Structural Summary (Forced Response)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    u0[u k] --> B0[B]
    B0 --> S1((+))
    x0[x k] --> A0[A]
    A0 --> S1
    S1 --> Zinv[z to power minus 1]
    Zinv --> x0
    x0 --> C0[C]
    C0 --> S2((+))
    u0 --> D0[D]
    D0 --> S2
    S2 --> y0[y k]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagram emphasizes the **recursive** nature of the discrete state space update: $\mathbf{x}(k+1)$ is computed from the current state $\mathbf{x}(k)$ and the current input $u(k)$, then passed through a unit delay (one sample period $T_s$) to become the next state.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define state space representation of a discrete time control system. Write the standard form of state and output equations.*

**Model Answer:**
State space representation describes a linear discrete-time system using two matrix equations:
$$
\mathbf{x}(k+1) = \mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{x}(k) + \mathbf{B}\,u(k), \qquad y(k) = \mathbf{C}\,\mathbf{x}(k) + \mathbf{D}\,u(k)
$$
where $\mathbf{x}(k)$ is the $n \times 1$ state vector, $u(k)$ is the input, $y(k)$ is the output, and $\mathbf{A, B, C, D}$ are constant matrices of dimensions $n \times n$, $n \times 1$, $1 \times n$, $1 \times 1$ respectively.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
*List any four canonical forms used in state space representation of discrete systems.*

**Model Answer:**
The four major canonical forms are:
1. **Controllable Canonical Form (CCF)**
2. **Observable Canonical Form (OCF)**
3. **Diagonal Canonical Form (DCF)**
4. **Jordan Canonical Form (JCF)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

**Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the state space representation in **controllable canonical form** for a discrete-time system with the pulse transfer function:
$$
G(z) = \frac{2z^2 + 5z + 1}{z^2 + 4z + 3}
$$
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** From the derived $\mathbf{A}$ matrix in part (a), determine the **stability** of the system. Comment on the location of eigenvalues. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution for Question A (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify coefficients:**
$b_0 = 2, b_1 = 5, b_2 = 1, a_1 = 4, a_2 = 3$, and $n = 2$.

**Step 2 — Construct $\mathbf{A}$ (companion matrix):**
$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} -4 & -3 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Construct $\mathbf{B}$:**
$$
\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Construct $\mathbf{C}$:**
$$
\mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} b_1 - a_1 b_0 & b_2 - a_2 b_0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 - 8 & 1 - 6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -3 & -5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Direct term $\mathbf{D}$:**
$$
\mathbf{D} = b_0 = 2
$$

**Final state space model:**
$$
\mathbf{x}(k+1) = \begin{bmatrix} -4 & -3 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{x}(k) + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} u(k), \quad y(k) = \begin{bmatrix} -3 & -5 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{x}(k) + 2 u(k)
$$

---

**Solution for Question A (b):**

**Step 1 — Characteristic polynomial:**
$$
\det(z\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = \det \begin{bmatrix} z+4 & 3 \\ -1 & z \end{bmatrix} = z(z+4) + 3 = z^2 + 4z + 3 = (z+1)(z+3)
$$

**Step 2 — Eigenvalues:** $\lambda_1 = -1, \lambda_2 = -3$.

**Step 3 — Stability check:** Both eigenvalues have magnitude $\vert \lambda_1 \vert = 1$ and $\vert \lambda_2 \vert = 3 > 1$.

**Conclusion:** The system is **UNSTABLE** because $\vert \lambda_2 \vert = 3 > 1$ (lies outside the unit circle), and $\lambda_1 = -1$ lies on the unit circle boundary (marginally unstable).

---

**Question B [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** Obtain the state space representation of the following system in **diagonal canonical form**:
$$
G(z) = \frac{z^2 + 2z + 1}{(z-1)(z-2)} = \frac{z^2 + 2z + 1}{z^2 - 3z + 2}
$$
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Hence find the **state transition matrix** $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(k) = \mathbf{A}^k$ and the **step response** at $k = 2$ assuming zero initial state and unit step input. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution for Question B (a):**

**Step 1 — Polynomial division** (numerator and denominator both degree 2):
$$
\frac{z^2 + 2z + 1}{z^2 - 3z + 2} = 1 + \frac{(z^2 + 2z + 1) - (z^2 - 3z + 2)}{z^2 - 3z + 2} = 1 + \frac{5z - 1}{(z-1)(z-2)}
$$

So $b_0 = 1$.

**Step 2 — Partial fractions:**
$$
\frac{5z - 1}{(z-1)(z-2)} = \frac{r_1}{z-1} + \frac{r_2}{z-2}
$$
$$
r_1 = \left.\frac{5z - 1}{z - 2}\right\vert_{z=1} = \frac{4}{-1} = -4
$$
$$
r_2 = \left.\frac{5z - 1}{z - 1}\right\vert_{z=2} = \frac{9}{1} = 9
$$

**Step 3 — Construct the diagonal state space model:**
$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} -4 & 9 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{D} = 1
$$

---

**Solution for Question B (b):**

**Step 1 — State transition matrix:**
Since $\mathbf{A}$ is diagonal, $\mathbf{A}^k$ is trivially:
$$
\boldsymbol{\Phi}(k) = \mathbf{A}^k = \begin{bmatrix} 1^k & 0 \\ 0 & 2^k \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2^k \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Step response (general formula for zero initial state):**
$$
y(k) = \mathbf{C}\,\mathbf{A}^k\,\mathbf{x}(0) + \sum_{m=0}^{k-1} \mathbf{C}\,\mathbf{A}^{k-1-m}\,\mathbf{B}\,u(m) + \mathbf{D}\,u(k)
$$
With $\mathbf{x}(0) = \mathbf{0}$ and $u(k) = 1$ for all $k \ge 0$:

For $k = 2$:
$$
y(2) = \mathbf{D}\cdot u(2) + \mathbf{C}\,\mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{B}\,u(1) + \mathbf{C}\,\mathbf{B}\,u(0)
$$
$$
\mathbf{C}\,\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} -4 & 9 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = 5
$$
$$
\mathbf{C}\,\mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} -4 & 9 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -4 & 18 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = 14
$$
$$
y(2) = 1 + 14 + 5 = 20
$$

**Step 3 — Verification by inverse z-transform:** 
The step response in $z$-domain is $\frac{z}{z-1} G(z) = \frac{z(z^2+2z+1)}{(z-1)^2(z-2)}$.
Inverse transform yields $y(2) = 20$ ✓.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Error in $\mathbf{A}$:** Students often forget the **negative sign** on the coefficients of the companion matrix. The matrix entry is $\mathbf{A}_{1j} = -a_j$, NOT $a_j$. This is the single most common mark-losing mistake.
> 2. **Direct Term $\mathbf{D} = b_0$:** Forgetting to include $\mathbf{D} = b_0$ in the output equation loses 1–2 marks. Some textbooks normalize the denominator to 1 and set $b_0$ to its leading coefficient — be consistent.
> 3. **Partial Fraction Error in DCF:** Forgetting to perform polynomial division first when numerator and denominator have the **same degree** will give wrong residues. Always check degrees first.
> 4. **Confusing $\mathbf{B}$ column vectors:** In CCF, $\mathbf{B} = [1, 0, \ldots, 0]^T$. In OCF, $\mathbf{B} = \mathbf{C}_{CCF}^T$ (NOT the same as CCF's $\mathbf{B}$). Mixing these up is a frequent error.
> 5. **Stability Criterion:** Remember, for discrete systems, stability requires eigenvalues **inside the unit circle** ($\vert \lambda \vert < 1$), NOT inside the left half plane. Using the continuous-time criterion is a costly mistake.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **state space model** for discrete systems uses two equations: $\mathbf{x}(k+1) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}(k) + \mathbf{B}u(k)$ and $y(k) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x}(k) + \mathbf{D}u(k)$.
- The matrices $\mathbf{A, B, C, D}$ are **not unique** — different canonical forms give different representations of the same system.
- **Controllable Canonical Form (CCF):** $\mathbf{A}$ is the companion matrix, $\mathbf{B} = [1, 0, \ldots, 0]^T$. Used for pole placement.
- **Observable Canonical Form (OCF):** Obtained by taking the transpose duality of CCF. Used for observer design.
- **Diagonal Canonical Form (DCF):** Obtained by partial fraction expansion. $\mathbf{A}$ is diagonal with poles on the diagonal. Used for mode analysis.
- **Jordan Canonical Form (JCF):** Used when the system has **repeated poles**. $\mathbf{A}$ contains Jordan blocks with 1's on the superdiagonal.
- The **direct transmission term** $\mathbf{D}$ equals the leading coefficient $b_0$ of the numerator polynomial when the denominator is monic.
- For a 2nd-order system, the companion matrix is:
  $\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} -a_1 & -a_2 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ — **always with negative signs in the first row**.
- The **characteristic polynomial** $\det(z\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})$ must equal the denominator of $G(z)$ for the conversion to be correct.
- **Stability condition (discrete):** All eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ must satisfy $\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1$ (inside the unit circle).
- **State transition matrix:** $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(k) = \mathbf{A}^k$. The general solution to the state equation is $\mathbf{x}(k) = \mathbf{A}^k \mathbf{x}(0) + \sum_{m=0}^{k-1} \mathbf{A}^{k-1-m} \mathbf{B} u(m)$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conversion of transfer function model to state space model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET633 - DISCRETE CONTROL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Conversion of transfer function model to state space model ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **discrete-time state space model** is a set of **n** first-order difference equations that completely describe the dynamics of an **n**th-order linear time-invariant (LTI) discrete system. In its canonical representation: $$ x(k+1) = A\,x(k) + B\,u(k) $$ $$ y(k) = C\,x(k) + D\,u(k) $$ Where: - **x(k)** is the **state vector** of dimension **n × 1** - **u(k)** is the **input (control) vector** - **y(k)** is the **output vector** - **A**, **B**, **C**, **D** are the **system matrices** of dimensions **n × n**, **n × p**, **q × n**, **q × p** respectively - **A** → System matrix, **B** → Input matrix, **C** → Output matrix, **D** → Feed-through (direct transmission) matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Transfer Function to State Space Conversion** is the systematic procedure of obtaining the matrices **A, B, C, D** such that the transfer function reconstructed as $G(z) = C(zI - A)^{-1}B + D$ is **identical** to the given pulse transfer function. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of it as "Recipe to Blueprint"**: A transfer function $G(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{U(z)}$ is like a **recipe** that tells you only the input-output relationship (ingredients to dish). It does NOT tell you the *internal cooking steps*. The state space model is the **blueprint of the kitchen** — it specifies the internal variables (state variables, e.g., $x_1, x_2, \ldots$), the connections, and the intermediate processes that produce the final output. For example, the transfer function $\frac{z + 0.5}{z^2 + 1.5z + 0.7}$ is a "black box" description, while the state space representation **unwraps the box** and exposes the internal dynamics through two phase variables ($x_1, x_2$) whose evolution over discrete time-steps $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ is governed by explicit recursive equations. ### 1.3 Why is Conversion Needed? (KTU 2024 Perspective) - **Modern control design techniques** (pole placement, observer design, LQR, Kalman filtering) operate exclusively on **state space models**, not on transfer functions. - State space **unifies SISO and MIMO** systems under one mathematical framework. - Internal states are accessible for **monitoring and estimation**, unlike the black-box transfer function. - For **nonlinear** extensions, state space forms the foundation. > [!TIP] > **Key fact**: For any **minimal realization** of a strictly proper transfer function with **distinct poles**, **n** state variables are needed where **n** is the order of the denominator. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Pre-Conversion Step: Properness Check Before applying any canonical form, the transfer function must be classified: | Type | Condition | Effect on D matrix | |------|-----------|--------------------| | **Strictly Proper** | $\deg(\text{Num}) < \deg(\text{Den})$ | $D = 0$ | | **Proper** | $\deg(\text{Num}) = \deg(\text{Den})$ | $D = b_n / a_n$ (after polynomial division) | | **Improper** | $\deg(\text{Num}) > \deg(\text{Den})$ | Polynomial long division first, residue in $D$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > Always rearrange the transfer function into the **standard negative-power form** before conversion: > $$G(z) = \frac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + b_n z^{-n}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + a_n z^{-n}}$$ ### 2.2 Method 1: Controllable Canonical Form (CCF) / Phase Variable Form This is the **most frequently tested method** in KTU exams. The state variable chosen is the output of each delay (shifter) element. For a 2nd-order transfer function $G(z) = \dfrac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}}$: $$ A = \begin{bmatrix} -a_1 & -a_2 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix},\quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix},\quad C = \begin{bmatrix} b_1 & b_2 \end{bmatrix},\quad D = b_0 $$ For a general **n**th-order system: $$ A = \begin{bmatrix} -a_1 & -a_2 & -a_3 & \cdots & -a_n \\ 1 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}_{n \times n},\quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}_{n \times 1} $$ $$ C = \begin{bmatrix} b_1 & b_2 & \cdots & b_n \end{bmatrix}_{1 \times n},\quad D = b_0 $$ **Logic Steps:** 1. Identify the coefficients $b_0, b_1, \ldots, b_n$ (numerator) and $a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n$ (denominator) after normalizing. 2. The **last row of A** contains negative denominator coefficients: $[-a_1, -a_2, \ldots, -a_n]$. 3. The **sub-diagonal of A** is filled with 1's (representing the shift operations). 4. **B** has 1 in the first entry, rest zeros. 5. **C** contains the numerator coefficients $b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_n$. ### 2.3 Method 2: Observable Canonical Form (OCF) This is the **dual (transpose)** of the CCF. Achieved by transposition of A, swapping B and C, and using C as the output selector. For 2nd order: $$ A_o = \begin{bmatrix} -a_1 & 1 \\ -a_2 & 0 \end{bmatrix},\quad B_o = \begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \end{bmatrix},\quad C_o = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix},\quad D = b_0 $$ For n-th order: $$ A_o = \begin{bmatrix} -a_1 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ -a_2 & 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \ddots & \vdots \\ -a_{n-1} & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \\ -a_n & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \end{bmatrix},\quad B_o = \begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \\ \vdots \\ b_n \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ C_o = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \end{bmatrix},\quad D = b_0 $$ > [!NOTE] > OCF is the **dual** of CCF: $A_o = A_c^T$, $B_o = C_c^T$, $C_o = B_c^T$. Choose OCF when designing **observers** and CCF when designing **controllers**. ### 2.4 Method 3: Diagonal Canonical Form (Parallel Decomposition) Used when the transfer function has **distinct real poles**. Decompose using **partial fractions**: $$ G(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = \frac{k_1}{z - p_1} + \frac{k_2}{z - p_2} + \cdots + \frac{k_n}{z - p_n} + D $$ The state space realization is: $$ A = \begin{bmatrix} p_1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & p_2 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & p_n \end{bmatrix},\quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ \vdots \\ 1 \end{bmatrix},\quad C = \begin{bmatrix} k_1 & k_2 & \cdots & k_n \end{bmatrix} $$ ### 2.5 Method 4: Jordan Canonical Form Used when the system has **repeated roots**. Each repeated pole $p_i$ (with multiplicity $m$) yields an $m \times m$ Jordan block: $$ J = \begin{bmatrix} p_i & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & p_i & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & p_i \end{bmatrix} $$ ### 2.6 Method 5: Cascade (Series) Decomposition Factor $G(z)$ into a product of 1st and 2nd-order sections: $$ G(z) = G_1(z) \cdot G_2(z) \cdots G_r(z) $$ Realize each section in CCF separately, then **cascade** the state space matrices. If $G_i(z)$ has realization $(A_i, B_i, C_i, D_i)$, then cascading gives a block-triangular $A$: $$ A_{\text{cascade}} = \begin{bmatrix} A_1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ B_2 C_1 & A_2 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ B_r C_{r-1} & \cdots & B_r C_2 & A_r \end{bmatrix} $$ ### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Canonical Form | A matrix signature | B signature | C signature | D | |----------------|---------------------|-------------|-------------|-----| | **CCF / Phase Variable** | Companion matrix, last row has $-a_i$, sub-diagonal has 1's | $[1, 0, \ldots, 0]^T$ | $[b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_n]$ | $b_0$ | | **OCF** | Transpose of CCF companion | $[b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_n]^T$ | $[1, 0, \ldots, 0]$ | $b_0$ | | **Diagonal** | Diagonal of poles $p_i$ | $[1, 1, \ldots, 1]^T$ | $[k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_n]$ | $D$ (extra term) | | **Jordan** | Block diagonal with $p_i$'s and 1's on super-diagonal | Modified B | Modified C | $b_0$ | | Recovery Equation | Formula | |-------------------|---------| | **Transfer function from state space** | $G(z) = C(zI - A)^{-1} B + D$ | | **Inverse formula (2×2)** | $(zI - A)^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{\det(zI-A)} \cdot \text{adj}(zI - A)$ | | **Determinant (2×2)** | $\det(zI - A) = (z-a_{11})(z-a_{22}) - a_{12} a_{21}$ | | **Characteristic polynomial** | $\det(zI - A) = z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n$ | | **CCF Discrete equation** | $x_i(k+1) = x_{i+1}(k)$ for $i < n$, $x_n(k+1) = -\sum a_i x_i(k) + u(k)$ | | **Output equation** | $y(k) = b_0 u(k) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i x_i(k)$ | ### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace**: Flight control systems — state space models represent pitch, roll, yaw dynamics simultaneously. - **Robotics**: Multi-DOF manipulator arms — state vector contains joint positions and velocities. - **Process Control**: MIMO chemical reactors with multiple inputs (flow rates) and outputs (temperatures). - **Power Systems**: Multi-machine grid stability studies. - **Digital Filter Design**: Direct Form II realization of IIR filters is essentially a state space canonical form. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Working Example (Run Through the Entire Module 4 Reasoning) **Problem**: Convert the discrete transfer function to **Controllable Canonical Form (CCF)** state space model. $$ G(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = \frac{z + 0.5}{z^2 + 1.5\,z + 0.7} $$ --- #### Step 1: Convert to Negative-Power Form (Standard for State Space) Divide numerator and denominator by $z^2$: $$ \frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = \frac{z \cdot z^{-2} + 0.5 \cdot z^{-2}}{z^2 \cdot z^{-2} + 1.5 z \cdot z^{-2} + 0.7 \cdot z^{-2}} = \frac{z^{-1} + 0.5\,z^{-2}}{1 + 1.5\,z^{-1} + 0.7\,z^{-2}} $$ **Coefficient identification** (writing numerator and denominator as power series in $z^{-1}$): $$ b_0 = 0,\quad b_1 = 1,\quad b_2 = 0.5 $$ $$ a_1 = 1.5,\quad a_2 = 0.7 $$ --- #### Step 2: Introduce Intermediate Variable and Form Difference Equation Define intermediate signal $E(z)$ such that: $$ \frac{E(z)}{U(z)} = \frac{1}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}} $$ Then $E(z)\,[1 + 1.5\,z^{-1} + 0.7\,z^{-2}] = U(z)$, which expands to: $$ E(z) = U(z) - 1.5\,z^{-1} E(z) - 0.7\,z^{-2} E(z) $$ In time domain (delaying by $k$): $$ e(k) = u(k) - 1.5\,e(k-1) - 0.7\,e(k-2) $$ The output equation in $z$-domain: $$ Y(z) = (z^{-1} + 0.5\,z^{-2})\,E(z) $$ In time domain: $$ y(k) = e(k-1) + 0.5\,e(k-2) $$ --- #### Step 3: Define State Variables (Phase Variables) Let the state variables be the **outputs of the unit delay elements**: $$ x_1(k) = e(k-1) $$ $$ x_2(k) = e(k-2) $$ These are **phase variables** — direct outputs of the delay blocks in the CCF realization diagram. --- #### Step 4: Derive the State Difference Equations **For $x_1(k+1)$:** $$ x_1(k+1) = e(k) = u(k) - 1.5\,e(k-1) - 0.7\,e(k-2) $$ Substituting $e(k-1) = x_1(k)$ and $e(k-2) = x_2(k)$: $$ x_1(k+1) = -1.5\,x_1(k) - 0.7\,x_2(k) + u(k) $$ **For $x_2(k+1)$:** $$ x_2(k+1) = e(k-1) = x_1(k) $$ --- #### Step 5: Derive the Output Equation $$ y(k) = e(k-1) + 0.5\,e(k-2) = x_1(k) + 0.5\,x_2(k) $$ --- #### Step 6: Assemble the State Space Matrices Stacking the equations into matrix form: $$ \begin{aligned} \begin{bmatrix} x_1(k+1) \\ x_2(k+1) \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} -1.5 & -0.7 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1(k) \\ x_2(k) \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} u(k) \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} y(k) &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1(k) \\ x_2(k) \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ A = \begin{bmatrix} -1.5 & -0.7 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix},\quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix},\quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix},\quad D = 0 $$ --- #### Step 7: Verification (Mandatory in KTU Valuation) The reconstructed transfer function must equal $G(z)$: $$ G(z) = C(zI - A)^{-1} B + D $$ Compute $zI - A$: $$ zI - A = \begin{bmatrix} z + 1.5 & 0.7 \\ -1 & z \end{bmatrix} $$ Determinant: $$ \det(zI - A) = (z + 1.5)(z) - (0.7)(-1) = z^2 + 1.5\,z + 0.7 $$ Adjoint matrix: $$ \text{adj}(zI - A) = \begin{bmatrix} z & -0.7 \\ 1 & z + 1.5 \end{bmatrix} $$ Therefore: $$ (zI - A)^{-1} B = \frac{1}{z^2 + 1.5\,z + 0.7} \begin{bmatrix} z & -0.7 \\ 1 & z + 1.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{z^2 + 1.5\,z + 0.7} \begin{bmatrix} z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ Final transfer function: $$ C(zI - A)^{-1} B = \frac{1}{z^2 + 1.5\,z + 0.7} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{z + 0.5}{z^2 + 1.5\,z + 0.7} \;\;\checkmark $$ The reconstructed transfer function **exactly matches** the original. Verification successful. --- ### 3.2 Conversion to Observable Canonical Form (OCF) — Same Example Using the dual-transpose relationship: $$ A_o = A^T = \begin{bmatrix} -1.5 & 1 \\ -0.7 & 0 \end{bmatrix},\quad B_o = C^T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix},\quad C_o = B^T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix},\quad D = 0 $$ **Verification**: $$ zI - A_o = \begin{bmatrix} z + 1.5 & -1 \\ 0.7 & z \end{bmatrix} $$ Determinant: $(z+1.5)z - (-1)(0.7) = z^2 + 1.5z + 0.7$ ✓ $$ (zI - A_o)^{-1} B_o = \frac{1}{z^2 + 1.5z + 0.7} \begin{bmatrix} z & 1 \\ -0.7 & z + 1.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{z^2 + 1.5z + 0.7} \begin{bmatrix} z + 0.5 \\ 0.05 + 0.5z \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ C_o (zI - A_o)^{-1} B_o = \frac{z + 0.5}{z^2 + 1.5z + 0.7} \;\;\checkmark $$ --- ### 3.3 Conversion via Parallel Decomposition — Distinct Real Poles Example **Problem**: Convert to state space using parallel decomposition. $$ G(z) = \frac{z + 0.5}{(z+1)(z+2)} = \frac{z + 0.5}{z^2 + 3z + 2} $$ **Step 1**: Partial fraction expansion (in $z$, not $z^{-1}$): $$ \frac{z + 0.5}{(z+1)(z+2)} = \frac{K_1}{z+1} + \frac{K_2}{z+2} $$ Solving: $z + 0.5 = K_1(z+2) + K_2(z+1)$ At $z = -1$: $-1 + 0.5 = K_1(1) \Rightarrow K_1 = -0.5$ At $z = -2$: $-2 + 0.5 = K_2(-1) \Rightarrow K_2 = 1.5$ So: $$ G(z) = \frac{-0.5}{z+1} + \frac{1.5}{z+2} $$ **Step 2**: Identify state dynamics (each term is a 1st-order system $x_i(k+1) = p_i x_i(k) + u(k)$, with $y_i = K_i x_i$): $$ \begin{aligned} x_1(k+1) &= -1 \cdot x_1(k) + u(k), \quad y_1(k) = -0.5 \cdot x_1(k) \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} x_2(k+1) &= -2 \cdot x_2(k) + u(k), \quad y_2(k) = 1.5 \cdot x_2(k) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3**: Combined state space form: $$ \begin{bmatrix} x_1(k+1) \\ x_2(k+1) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1(k) \\ x_2(k) \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u(k) $$ $$ y(k) = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 & 1.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1(k) \\ x_2(k) \end{bmatrix} $$ Note: The A matrix is **diagonal** (each pole is on the diagonal), which is a key signature of the diagonal canonical form. --- ### 3.4 Comparison of Realizations (Analytical Matrix) | Realization | A matrix | Eigenvalues of A | Complexity | Use Case | |-------------|----------|------------------|------------|----------| | **CCF** | $\begin{bmatrix} -1.5 & -0.7 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ | Roots of $z^2 + 1.5z + 0.7$ | Companion | Controller design, pole placement | | **OCF** | $\begin{bmatrix} -1.5 & 1 \\ -0.7 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ | Roots of $z^2 + 1.5z + 0.7$ | Companion (transpose) | Observer design, Luenberger observer | | **Diagonal** | $\begin{bmatrix} p_1 & 0 \\ 0 & p_2 \end{bmatrix}$ | $p_1, p_2$ (poles) | Diagonal | Modal analysis, decoupling | > [!TIP] > **All three realizations have identical eigenvalues (characteristic polynomial)**. They are **algebraically equivalent** — related by a similarity transformation $A_2 = T^{-1} A_1 T$ for some invertible transformation matrix $T$. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Realization Diagram (Controllable Canonical Form) The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **block-level functional architecture** of the CCF realization for a 2nd-order transfer function $G(z) = \dfrac{z + 0.5}{z^2 + 1.5z + 0.7}$. Note the explicit use of **delay elements** ($z^{-1}$ blocks), summing junctions, and coefficient gains — the standard signals-and-systems realization. ```mermaid graph LR classDef inputStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef delayStyle fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef gainStyle fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef sumStyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef outputStyle fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 Uk["u(k)"]:::inputStyle S1(("Σ1")):::sumStyle G1["−1.5"]:::gainStyle G2["−0.7"]:::gainStyle D1["z⁻¹"]:::delayStyle D2["z⁻¹"]:::delayStyle X1["x1(k)"]:::outputStyle X2["x2(k)"]:::outputStyle C1["b1 = 1"]:::gainStyle C2["b2 = 0.5"]:::gainStyle S2(("Σ2")):::sumStyle Yk["y(k)"]:::outputStyle Uk --> S1 G1 --> S1 G2 --> S1 S1 --> D1 D1 --> X1 D1 --> D2 D2 --> X2 X2 --> G1 X1 --> G2 X1 --> C1 X2 --> C2 C1 --> S2 C2 --> S2 S2 --> Yk ``` **Reading the diagram**: - The input $u(k)$ enters summer Σ1 along with the feedback signals scaled by $-1.5$ and $-0.7$. - The summer output propagates through a delay $z^{-1}$ producing state $x_1(k)$. - $x_1(k)$ is further delayed to produce $x_2(k)$. - Output $y(k)$ is formed by summing the weighted states ($1 \cdot x_1$ and $0.5 \cdot x_2$). --- ### 4.2 Conversion Method Selection Flowchart This decision tree guides the choice of canonical form based on the transfer function's pole structure. ```mermaid graph TD classDef startStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef decisionStyle fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef methodStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef resultStyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 A["START: Given Transfer Function G(z)"]:::startStyle B{"Check pole structure"}:::decisionStyle C{"Real distinct poles?"}:::decisionStyle D{"Repeated poles?"}:::decisionStyle E["Use CCF or OCF directly
Companion matrix form"]:::methodStyle F["Diagonal Canonical Form
A is diagonal of poles"]:::methodStyle G["Jordan Canonical Form
A has Jordan blocks"]:::methodStyle H["Cascade Decomposition
Factor into 1st/2nd order sections"]:::methodStyle A --> B B --> C C -- "Yes" --> F C -- "No, repeated" --> D D -- "Yes" --> G D -- "No" --> E B --> H ``` --- ### 4.3 State Space Architecture — Conceptual Block Diagram This diagram shows the **general internal architecture** of a state space model, emphasizing the feedback loops through the A matrix. ```mermaid graph LR classDef vectorStyle fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef matrixStyle fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef opStyle fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outStyle fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 Xk["x(k)
n × 1"]:::vectorStyle A["A
n × n"]:::matrixStyle B["B
n × p"]:::matrixStyle Uk["u(k)
p × 1"]:::vectorStyle SumA(("+")):::opStyle SumB(("+")):::opStyle Xkp1["x(k+1)
n × 1"]:::vectorStyle Delay["z⁻¹
(Unit Delay)"]:::opStyle C["C
q × n"]:::matrixStyle D["D
q × p"]:::matrixStyle Yk["y(k)
q × 1"]:::outStyle Xk --> A A --> SumA Uk --> B B --> SumA SumA --> Xkp1 Xkp1 --> Delay Delay --> Xk Xk --> C Uk --> D C --> SumB D --> SumB SumB --> Yk ``` **Architecture Insight**: - The **delay block** $z^{-1}$ is the heart of any discrete system — it maps state from time $k$ to $k+1$. - The **A matrix** creates internal feedback (state-to-state coupling). - The **B matrix** injects external inputs into the state dynamics. - The **C matrix** observes the state to produce output. - The **D matrix** provides instantaneous (one-step) feed-through from input to output. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** > **Q1.** Define state space model for a discrete-time LTI system. State the dimensions of all four system matrices **A, B, C, D** for an **n**th-order SISO system. **Model Answer:** A discrete-time state space model is a set of **n** first-order difference equations describing the dynamics using internal state variables. The general form is: $$x(k+1) = A\,x(k) + B\,u(k), \quad y(k) = C\,x(k) + D\,u(k)$$ For an **n**th-order SISO system: - **A** is $n \times n$ (system matrix) - **B** is $n \times 1$ (input matrix) - **C** is $1 \times n$ (output matrix) - **D** is $1 \times 1$ (feed-through scalar, zero for strictly proper systems). --- > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** > **Q2.** What is meant by phase variable canonical form? List any **three** salient features of the A matrix in this form. **Model Answer:** The phase variable canonical form (also called controllable canonical form) is a specific state space realization where the state variables are the outputs of successive delay elements, leading to a **companion matrix** structure. Three salient features of the **A matrix**: 1. The **last row** contains the negated denominator coefficients: $[-a_1, -a_2, \ldots, -a_n]$. 2. The **sub-diagonal** (entries just below the main diagonal) contains **1's**, representing the shift operation between successive phase variables. 3. All other entries are **zero**, making A a companion matrix. --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - Nov 2023, Module 4 Full Question]** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Derive the controllable canonical form (CCF) representation for an nth-order discrete transfer function. Clearly write the general structure of the **A, B, C, D** matrices. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For the discrete transfer function $G(z) = \dfrac{z + 0.5}{z^2 + 1.5z + 0.7}$, obtain the state space model in CCF. Verify your result by computing $C(zI - A)^{-1}B$. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution for Question A:** **Part (a) — Derivation:** Consider a strictly proper nth-order transfer function in standard form: $$ G(z) = \frac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + b_n z^{-n}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + a_n z^{-n}} $$ Introduce intermediate variable $E(z)$ such that $\dfrac{E(z)}{U(z)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + \cdots + a_n z^{-n}}$. Then $E(z) = U(z) - \sum a_i z^{-i} E(z)$, giving the difference equation: $$ e(k) = u(k) - a_1 e(k-1) - a_2 e(k-2) - \cdots - a_n e(k-n) $$ Define phase variables $x_i(k) = e(k-i)$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$. Then the state equations become: $$ x_i(k+1) = x_{i+1}(k) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, n-1 $$ $$ x_n(k+1) = -a_n x_1(k) - a_{n-1} x_2(k) - \cdots - a_1 x_n(k) + u(k) $$ Output equation: $$ y(k) = b_1 x_1(k) + b_2 x_2(k) + \cdots + b_n x_n(k) + b_0 u(k) $$ The matrices are therefore: $$ A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 1 \\ -a_n & -a_{n-1} & \cdots & -a_2 & -a_1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ C = \begin{bmatrix} b_n & b_{n-1} & \cdots & b_2 & b_1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = b_0 $$ *(Note: Some textbooks reverse the order of $a_i$ and $b_i$ in the matrix; both representations are equivalent.)* **Part (b) — Numerical solution:** For $G(z) = \dfrac{z + 0.5}{z^2 + 1.5z + 0.7}$: In $z^{-1}$ form: $G(z) = \dfrac{z^{-1} + 0.5 z^{-2}}{1 + 1.5 z^{-1} + 0.7 z^{-2}}$. Therefore $b_0 = 0,\ b_1 = 1,\ b_2 = 0.5,\ a_1 = 1.5,\ a_2 = 0.7$. Using the CCF matrix structure derived above: $$ A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.7 & -1.5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0 $$ (Equivalent alternative form with reversed coefficient ordering is also acceptable: $A = \begin{bmatrix} -1.5 & -0.7 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix},\ B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix},\ C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$.) Verification: $$ zI - A = \begin{bmatrix} z & -1 \\ 0.7 & z + 1.5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \det = z^2 + 1.5z + 0.7 $$ $$ C(zI - A)^{-1}B = \frac{0.5(z+1.5) + 1 \cdot z}{z^2 + 1.5z + 0.7} = \frac{1.5z + 0.75 + z}{z^2 + 1.5z + 0.7} = \frac{2.5z + 0.75}{z^2 + 1.5z + 0.7} $$ Wait — this shows a coefficient discrepancy! Let me re-verify using the alternative form: $$ A = \begin{bmatrix} -1.5 & -0.7 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ zI - A = \begin{bmatrix} z + 1.5 & 0.7 \\ -1 & z \end{bmatrix}, \quad \det = z^2 + 1.5z + 0.7 $$ $$ (zI - A)^{-1}B = \frac{1}{z^2 + 1.5z + 0.7} \begin{bmatrix} z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ C(zI - A)^{-1}B = \frac{z + 0.5}{z^2 + 1.5z + 0.7} \;\;\checkmark $$ This is the correct verified CCF realization. The first representation with reversed coefficients is **NOT** equivalent due to ordering convention; only the second form (with $A = \begin{bmatrix} -1.5 & -0.7 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$) is correct for the coefficient ordering $b_1, b_2$ in $C$. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice Alternative)* **(a)** Explain the **observable canonical form** (OCF). Show how the OCF matrices are obtained from the CCF matrices. Why is OCF preferred for observer design? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using **parallel decomposition**, convert the transfer function $G(z) = \dfrac{z + 0.5}{(z+1)(z+2)}$ to state space form. Identify the A, B, C, D matrices. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **Part (a) — Observable Canonical Form:** The OCF is the **dual (transpose)** of the CCF. Given the CCF matrices $(A_c, B_c, C_c, D_c)$, the OCF matrices are: $$ A_o = A_c^T, \quad B_o = C_c^T, \quad C_o = B_c^T, \quad D_o = D_c $$ For an nth-order system with CCF: $$ A_o = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & -a_n \\ 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & -a_{n-1} \\ 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 & -a_{n-2} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 & -a_1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_o = \begin{bmatrix} b_n \\ b_{n-1} \\ \vdots \\ b_1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C_o = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Why OCF for observer design?** A Luenberger observer reconstructs unmeasured states using the structure: $$ \hat{x}(k+1) = A\hat{x}(k) + Bu(k) + L(y(k) - C\hat{x}(k)) $$ When using OCF, the observer error dynamics decouple into **scalar sub-systems**, allowing independent tuning of each observer pole. This makes OCF the natural choice for **observer gain $L$ synthesis**. **Part (b) — Parallel Decomposition Solution:** Given: $G(z) = \dfrac{z + 0.5}{(z+1)(z+2)}$. **Step 1 — Partial fraction expansion:** $$ \frac{z + 0.5}{(z+1)(z+2)} = \frac{K_1}{z+1} + \frac{K_2}{z+2} $$ Cross-multiplying: $z + 0.5 = K_1(z+2) + K_2(z+1)$. At $z = -1$: $-0.5 = K_1 \Rightarrow K_1 = -0.5$. At $z = -2$: $-1.5 = -K_2 \Rightarrow K_2 = 1.5$. So $G(z) = \dfrac{-0.5}{z+1} + \dfrac{1.5}{z+2}$. **Step 2 — Recognize each term as a 1st-order state system:** For term 1: $x_1(k+1) = -x_1(k) + u(k),\ y_1(k) = -0.5\,x_1(k)$. For term 2: $x_2(k+1) = -2\,x_2(k) + u(k),\ y_2(k) = 1.5\,x_2(k)$. **Step 3 — Combine:** $$ \begin{bmatrix} x_1(k+1) \\ x_2(k+1) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1(k) \\ x_2(k) \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u(k) $$ $$ y(k) = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 & 1.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1(k) \\ x_2(k) \end{bmatrix} $$ Therefore: $$ A = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 & 1.5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0 $$ **Verification:** The poles of the system are eigenvalues of A: $\lambda_1 = -1, \lambda_2 = -2$, which match the original denominator factors $(z+1)(z+2)$ ✓. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert to $z^{-1}$ form first**: Many students write the numerator and denominator coefficients directly in $z$-powers and end up with a wrong A matrix. Always normalize to $z^{-1}$ basis. > 2. **Wrong coefficient ordering in C**: The C matrix coefficients must be **in the same order** as the powers of $z^{-1}$ in the numerator. $b_1$ multiplies $z^{-1}$, $b_2$ multiplies $z^{-2}$, etc. > 3. **Setting D = 0 for non-strictly proper systems**: If the transfer function has the same degree numerator and denominator, the $D$ matrix is **non-zero** (equal to the ratio of leading coefficients after polynomial division). > 4. **Skipping the verification step**: KTU examiners award **partial marks for verification**. Always end with $C(zI-A)^{-1}B = G(z)$ check. > 5. **Sign error in A matrix**: The last row of A in CCF contains $-a_i$, **not** $+a_i$. Students often forget the negative sign. > 6. **Confusing CCF and OCF dimensions**: CCF and OCF produce **different B and C matrices** but the same A matrix (up to transpose). Mixing them up costs 4-5 marks. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Conversion of TF to SS):** - **State space model** = internal representation using state vector $x(k)$, output vector $y(k)$, input $u(k)$, and matrices $A, B, C, D$. - **Pre-requisite check**: Verify **properness** of $G(z)$ before applying any canonical form. - **Standard form**: Always normalize to $G(z) = \dfrac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + \cdots + b_n z^{-n}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + \cdots + a_n z^{-n}}$. - **CCF (Phase Variable Form)**: Companion matrix A, $B = [1, 0, \ldots, 0]^T$, $C = [b_1, \ldots, b_n]$, $D = b_0$. Use for **controller design**. - **OCF**: Transpose of CCF, $B_o = C_c^T$, $C_o = B_c^T$. Use for **observer design**. - **Diagonal Form**: For **distinct real poles**; A is diagonal of poles. Use for **modal analysis**. - **Jordan Form**: For **repeated poles**; A contains Jordan blocks with 1's on super-diagonal. - **Cascade Form**: Decompose $G(z)$ into product of 1st/2nd-order sections; yields block-triangular A. - **All canonical forms have identical eigenvalues** (the poles) — they are **similar** transformations. - **Order of system = dimension of state vector = size of A matrix**. - **Verification formula**: Always check $C(zI - A)^{-1} B + D = G(z)$ at the end of every solution. - **Number of delays in realization diagram = order n of system**. - **Feed-through D = 0** for strictly proper $G(z)$; **non-zero** for proper/improper systems. - **Distinct poles** → 3 canonical choices (CCF, OCF, Diagonal). **Repeated poles** → Jordan form required for diagonal.

topics.title: Discrete time state transition matrix
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET633 - DISCRETE CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Discrete time state transition matrix

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

For a **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) discrete-time system** described by the state equation:

$$x(k+1) = A x(k) + B u(k)$$

The **Discrete-Time State Transition Matrix** is formally defined as the unique matrix $\Phi(k)$ that propagates the state from an initial time to a later time $k$ in the absence of external input. It is mathematically given by:

$$\Phi(k) = A^k$$

The state transition matrix is also expressed via the **resolvent (z-domain) form** as:

$$\Phi(k) = \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{(zI - A)^{-1} z\right\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The state transition matrix $\Phi(k)$ is the discrete-time analogue of the continuous-time state transition matrix $\phi(t) = e^{At}$. In the discrete domain, it takes the simple closed form $\Phi(k) = A^k$ because the solution of the difference equation $x(k+1) = A x(k)$ yields $x(k) = A^k x(0)$.

The complete solution of the forced discrete-time state equation is given by:

$$x(k) = \Phi(k) x(0) + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} \Phi(k-j-1) B u(j)$$

The first term is the **zero-input response** (natural response), and the summation term is the **zero-state response** (forced response).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine you are watching a movie frame by frame. The state of the system (position, velocity, current, etc.) is captured in each frame. The state transition matrix $\Phi(k) = A^k$ is like the **"movie projector"** that, given the very first frame $x(0)$, deterministically projects every subsequent frame $x(k)$ onto the screen. The matrix $A$ tells the projector how each frame transforms into the next, and $A^k$ tells how the first frame morphs into the $k$-th frame after $k$ discrete steps.

Think of it as a **cellular automaton rule**: given a cell's current value, the rule $A$ updates it. Applying the rule $k$ times is equivalent to applying $A^k$ once — that single compound rule is the state transition matrix.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Recognition Trick:** Whenever you see $A^k$ in discrete-time control, immediately recognize it as the **state transition matrix** $\Phi(k)$. This is the "Rosetta Stone" of discrete-time state-space analysis.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- $\Phi(k)$ is a square matrix of order $n \times n$, where $n$ is the order of the system.
- $k$ is a **non-negative integer** ($k \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$).
- $A$ is the **system matrix** (plant matrix), and $\Phi(0) = A^0 = I_n$ (the **n-dimensional identity matrix**).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The State Equation and Its Solution — The "Why" Behind $\Phi(k)$

Starting from the **homogeneous** (unforced) state equation:

$$x(k+1) = A x(k)$$

Iterating step-by-step yields the natural response:

| Step | Substitution | Result |
|------|--------------|--------|
| $k = 0$ | $x(0)$ is given | $x(0)$ |
| $k = 1$ | $x(1) = A x(0)$ | $A x(0)$ |
| $k = 2$ | $x(2) = A x(1) = A^2 x(0)$ | $A^2 x(0)$ |
| $k$ | continue pattern | $A^k x(0)$ |

Hence $x(k) = A^k x(0) = \Phi(k) x(0)$. The "how" is: **repeated matrix multiplication** of $A$ over $k$ steps collapses into a single matrix power $A^k$.

For the **non-homogeneous** (forced) equation $x(k+1) = A x(k) + B u(k)$, the same iteration principle applied with superposition gives:

$$x(k) = A^k x(0) + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} A^{k-j-1} B u(j)$$

## 2.2 Fundamental Properties of the Discrete State Transition Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These properties are **favorite KTU exam questions**. Memorize them cold.

1. **Initial Condition Property:**
$$\Phi(0) = A^0 = I_n$$
The state at $k=0$ is the initial state itself (identity mapping).

2. **Shift Property (Composition Rule):**
$$\Phi(k+1) = A \cdot \Phi(k) = A^{k+1}$$
Iterating: $\Phi(k+1) = A \Phi(k) = \Phi(k) A$ (since powers of a single matrix commute with itself).

3. **Additive Index Property (Semigroup):**
$$\Phi(k_1 + k_2) = \Phi(k_1) \cdot \Phi(k_2) = A^{k_1+k_2}$$
This means the system is **Markovian** — the state at any time depends only on the immediate previous state, not the full history.

4. **Inverse Property:**
$$\Phi^{-1}(k) = \Phi(-k) = A^{-k}$$
This is valid only if $A$ is **non-singular** ($\det(A) \neq 0$). It enables **backward-time** state reconstruction.

5. **Transpose Property:**
$$\Phi^T(k) = (A^k)^T = (A^T)^k$$
The transpose of the state transition matrix is the state transition matrix of the **dual (adjoint) system** $A^T$.

6. **Determinant Property:**
$$\det[\Phi(k)] = [\det(A)]^k$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Save this table — it is the entire formula arsenal you need for Module 4 problems.

| **#** | **Formula / Identity** | **Purpose / When to Use** | **Validity** |
|------|------------------------|----------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\Phi(k) = A^k$ | Direct definition | Always (LTI) |
| 2 | $\Phi(k) = \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{ (zI - A)^{-1} z \}$ | z-transform method | Always |
| 3 | $\Phi(k) = \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{ (I - z^{-1} A)^{-1} \}$ | Alternative resolvent | Always |
| 4 | $\Phi(0) = I$ | Initial condition | Always |
| 5 | $\Phi(k+1) = A \Phi(k)$ | Recursive computation | Always |
| 6 | $\Phi(k_1+k_2) = \Phi(k_1) \Phi(k_2)$ | Semigroup property | Always |
| 7 | $\Phi^{-1}(k) = \Phi(-k)$ | Backward propagation | $\det(A) \neq 0$ |
| 8 | $\Phi(k) = \alpha_0 I + \alpha_1 A + \cdots + \alpha_{n-1} A^{n-1}$ | Cayley-Hamilton reduction | Always |
| 9 | $x(k) = \Phi(k) x(0) + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} \Phi(k-j-1) B u(j)$ | Full forced solution | Always |
| 10 | $y(k) = C \Phi(k) x(0) + C \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} \Phi(k-j-1) B u(j) + D u(k)$ | Output equation solution | Always |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Filter Design:** The state transition matrix $\Phi(k)$ completely characterizes the internal evolution of a digital filter, enabling **stability analysis** via $\vert \lambda_i(A) \vert < 1$ (Schur stability).
- **Spacecraft Attitude Control:** Spacecraft orientation evolves as a discrete state $x(k) = A^k x(0)$; the transition matrix predicts future orientations for **orbital maneuvers**.
- **Numerical Integration of ODEs:** $\Phi(k)$ is the discrete analogue of $e^{At}$ used in computer simulation of continuous systems sampled at intervals $T$.
- **Networked Control Systems:** Predicts the state of a remotely controlled plant over **packet-lossy channels** by computing $A^k$ over the loss steps.
- **Robot Manipulator Trajectory Planning:** Joint angles evolve as $q(k) = A^k q(0)$; the transition matrix enables **feedforward control**.

---
